# Understand GeoJSON Coordinate Precision | Help | Mapbox

[メインコンテンツまでスキップ](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/geojson-coordinate-precision/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

[GeoJSON](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/geojson/)
 is a popular format for encoding geographic data structures. A critical aspect of GeoJSON is the **coordinate precision** used in storing vector data, which directly impacts file size, rendering performance, and location accuracy.

Recommended GeoJSON Coordinate Precision

The recommended coordinate precision for GeoJSON is **6 decimal places**, which provides a good balance between accuracy and file size. This level of precision allows for accurate representation of features while keeping the file size manageable.

What is coordinate precision in GeoJSON?[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/geojson-coordinate-precision/#what-is-coordinate-precision-in-geojson "What is coordinate precision in GeoJSON? への直接リンク")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

In GeoJSON, geographic coordinates are typically expressed as floating-point numbers in **decimal degrees**, describing locations on earth using the [WGS84 datum](https://www.linz.govt.nz/guidance/geodetic-system/coordinate-systems-used-new-zealand/geodetic-datums/world-geodetic-system-1984-wgs84)
. Precision refers to how many decimal places are used to represent latitude and longitude values.

The example below illustrates a GeoJSON Point feature with high coordinate precision, using 9 digits after the decimal point:

point-high-precision.geojson

    {  "type": "Feature",  "geometry": {    "type": "Point",    "coordinates": [102.123456789, 0.987654321]  },  "properties": {}}

A Point feature with lower coordinate precision might look like this, using only 3 digits after the decimal point:

point-low-precision.geojson

    {  "type": "Feature",  "geometry": {    "type": "Point",    "coordinates": [102.031, 0.592]  },  "properties": {}}

Why coordinate precision matters[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/geojson-coordinate-precision/#why-coordinate-precision-matters "Why coordinate precision matters への直接リンク")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

High coordinate precision can lead to **larger file sizes** and **slower rendering times**, especially when dealing with large datasets. Each additional decimal place increases the number of digits stored, which can significantly inflate the size of GeoJSON files.

Conversely, lower precision reduces file size and improves performance but may sacrifice location accuracy. The trade-off between precision and performance is crucial, especially for applications that require real-time rendering or have bandwidth constraints.

| Decimal Places | Approximate Accuracy |
| --- | --- |
| 1   | ~10 km |
| 2   | ~1 km |
| 3   | ~100 m |
| 4   | ~10 m |
| 5   | ~1 m |
| 6   | ~10 cm |
| 7   | ~1 cm |

### Visualizing coordinate precision[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/geojson-coordinate-precision/#visualizing-coordinate-precision "Visualizing coordinate precision への直接リンク")

This interactive map shows a grid of points representing single-digit increments of various coordinate precisions.

Choose a different precision level to see how it affects the coverage area of the grid of points. As the coordinate precision increases, you can see that each point represents a smaller area of the earth, demonstrating the impact of coordinate precision on geographic data representation. You can hover over each point to see its coordinates.

Reducing coordinate precision in GeoJSON[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/geojson-coordinate-precision/#reducing-coordinate-precision-in-geojson "Reducing coordinate precision in GeoJSON への直接リンク")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

If you are processing data programmatically, you can iterate over the coordinates in your GeoJSON features and round them to a specified number of decimal places. For example, using Python, you can use the `round()` function to reduce precision:

    import jsonfrom geojson import Point, Feature, FeatureCollectiondef reduce_precision(coordinates, precision=6):    return [round(coord, precision) for coord in coordinates]

There are also libraries and tools available specifically for use with GeoJSON that can help reduce coordinate precision. See the [Additional resources](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/geojson-coordinate-precision/#additional-resources)
 section below for more information.

Exporting or converting GeoJSON with software may generate high coordinate precision and large file sizes. For example, exporting a vector layer in [QGIS](https://qgis.org/)
 to GeoJSON will produce coordinates with 15 decimal places by default if you do not specify a lower coordinate precision. 15 decimal places is unnecessary for most applications and should be reduced to improve performance and file size.

Additional resources[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/geojson-coordinate-precision/#additional-resources "Additional resources への直接リンク")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   [GeoJSON Specification](https://stevage.github.io/geojson-spec/#section-11.2)
     - Read the official GeoJSON specification for details on coordinate precision and other aspects of the format.
*   [Observable Notebook on Coordinate Precision](https://observablehq.com/@mourner/latitude-and-longitude-precision)
     - Volodymyr Agafonkin explains how latitude and longitude precision affects geographic data representation.
*   [geojson-precision](https://github.com/perrygeo/geojson-precision)
     - A Python library for reducing coordinate precision in GeoJSON files.
*   [turf/truncate](https://turfjs.org/docs/api/truncate)
     - A Turf.js function that reduces the precision of coordinates in GeoJSON features.

このpageは役に立ちましたか？はいいいえ

# Manage your fontstacks | Help | Mapbox

[メインコンテンツまでスキップ](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/manage-fontstacks/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

A **fontstack** is an ordered list consisting of a _primary font_ and _optional fallback font(s)_.

Fonts can only be set on [`symbol`](https://docs.mapbox.com/studio-manual/ja/reference/styles/#symbol-layer)
 layer types. Set fonts from the **Style** tab of each `symbol` layer, under **Text**, in the `Font` input field. Each list of unique font pairings between primary and fallback font(s) will create a new fontstack.

Manage in Mapbox Studio[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/manage-fontstacks/#manage-in-mapbox-studio "Manage in Mapbox Studio への直接リンク")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

When your primary font has missing glyphs, the text will be rendered in the fallback font instead. The default fallback font set by Mapbox Studio is `Arial Unicode MS Bold`. Unicode fonts include more glyphs than conventional fonts, allowing for better multilingual coverage.

In Mapbox Studio you can manage fontstacks using **Components** or **Layers**. This section shows how to manage this example fontstack using either components and layers:

    'Roboto Black', 'Arial Unicode MS Bold';

### Components[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/manage-fontstacks/#components "Components への直接リンク")

In custom styles built with style components, you can set a fallback font using the **Typography** tab. For each component typography property, click the name of the property, scroll to the bottom of the panel, and click **1 font fallback** to change your fallback font.

circlecirclecircle

arrow-leftarrow-rightrotate

heartmapboxmenu

Your browser doesn't support HTML5 video. Open [link to the video](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/assets/medias/troubleshooting--manage-fontstacks--components-eb092caf4a86e7e8f650ebe1686d5f77.mp4)
.

### Layers[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/manage-fontstacks/#layers "Layers への直接リンク")

To manage fontstacks on individual layers in the **Layers** tab, click the name of the layer, click the **Font** property, and click **1 font fallback** to change your fallback font.

circlecirclecircle

arrow-leftarrow-rightrotate

heartmapboxmenu

Manage rendering time[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/manage-fontstacks/#manage-rendering-time "Manage rendering time への直接リンク")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The number of fontstacks paired with the density of text per zoom on your map can significantly slow down your map's load time, especially when using multilingual labels. This is because dense multilingual text, at any set zoom level, will increase rendering time.

Here are some tips to decrease your map's rendering time:

*   Latin scripts can handle more fontstacks, while other scripts like Chinese or Hangul, can require loading dozens of glyph tiles per fontstack.
*   Keeping labels sparse and spread across zoom levels allows for more fontstacks in your map style.
*   For multilingual maps with dense labeling, consider using fewer fontstacks.

Manage offline download size[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/manage-fontstacks/#manage-offline-download-size "Manage offline download size への直接リンク")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

When using one of our mobile SDKs, you can often reduce the amount of space required to download an offline region dramatically by limiting the number of fontstacks in your style. To learn more about reducing offline download sizes, see our [guide to offline maps](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/mobile-offline/)
.

このpageは役に立ちましたか？はいいいえ

# Changelog | Mapbox Search JS | Mapbox

[Skip to main content](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-search-js/guides/changelog/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

1.5.0[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-search-js/guides/changelog/#150 "Direct link to 1.5.0")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

### ✨ Features[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-search-js/guides/changelog/#-features "Direct link to ✨ Features")

*   Improved support and testing for React 16-19

### 🐞 Bug fixes[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-search-js/guides/changelog/#-bug-fixes "Direct link to 🐞 Bug fixes")

*   Fixes a bug in which clicking on a suggestion did not register if the mouse key was held down too long
*   Fixes type generation for React components across React versions
*   Fixes a bug to allow handling custom element attributes without an explicit value

1.4.0[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-search-js/guides/changelog/#140 "Direct link to 1.4.0")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

### ✨ Features[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-search-js/guides/changelog/#-features-1 "Direct link to ✨ Features")

*   Bubbles up a "blur" event to the user when focus is removed from the SearchBox or Geocoder components

### 🐞 Bug fixes[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-search-js/guides/changelog/#-bug-fixes-1 "Direct link to 🐞 Bug fixes")

*   Fixes an issue with browser autofill behavior that had caused web components to hang indefinitely

1.3.0[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-search-js/guides/changelog/#130 "Direct link to 1.3.0")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

### 🐞 Bug fixes[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-search-js/guides/changelog/#-bug-fixes-2 "Direct link to 🐞 Bug fixes")

*   Accessibility updates to remove selection behavior on Tab key press and persist listbox visibility while changing focus to Clear button
*   Remove global-scope CSS selector

1.2.0[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-search-js/guides/changelog/#120 "Direct link to 1.2.0")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

### ✨ Features[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-search-js/guides/changelog/#-features-2 "Direct link to ✨ Features")

*   Adds `flyTo` component option to control the map `flyTo` behavior when selecting a suggestion
*   Adds `customSearch` component option to supplement Mapbox results with client-provided results

1.1.0[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-search-js/guides/changelog/#110 "Direct link to 1.1.0")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

### ✨ Features[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-search-js/guides/changelog/#-features-3 "Direct link to ✨ Features")

*   Enables reverse searches as part of interactive Search Box sessions with a new `allowReverse` option
*   Adds a `flipCoordinates` option for use with either Geocoder or SearchBox components

1.0.0[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-search-js/guides/changelog/#100 "Direct link to 1.0.0")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

### ✨ Features[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-search-js/guides/changelog/#-features-4 "Direct link to ✨ Features")

*   First general availability release (!) 🚀
*   Included frameworks:
    *   Search JS Core - high-level API wrappers to call the [Mapbox Search Service](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/search/)
        
    *   Search JS Web - UI components on top of the Core library
    *   Search JS React - React hooks and wrappers for Search JS Web components
*   Included Search services:
    *   [Search Box](https://www.mapbox.com/search-box)
        
    *   [Address Autofill](https://www.mapbox.com/address-autofill)
        
    *   [Geocoding](https://www.mapbox.com/geocoding)
        
*   Documentation is available at [https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-search-js/guides/](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-search-js/guides/)
    

1.0.0-beta.25[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-search-js/guides/changelog/#100-beta25 "Direct link to 1.0.0-beta.25")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

### 🐞 Bug fixes[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-search-js/guides/changelog/#-bug-fixes-2 "Direct link to 🐞 Bug fixes")

1.0.0-beta.14[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-search-js/guides/changelog/#100-beta14 "Direct link to 1.0.0-beta.14")

*   Improved WAI-ARIA compliance to enable better screen reader compatibility and accessibility features within Autofill and Search Box
*   Adds new `interceptSearch` callback option to conditionally override or augment input text
*   Enables street-first searches in Autofill for better compatibility with international address formats
*   Adds feature type icons to the Autofill listbox UI
*   Adds new `browserAutofillEnabled` option for the Autofill component, plus adds behavior to attempt to suppress browser autocomplete popups while Mapbox suggestions are shown (works in Chrome browsers)

*   Fixes a bug causing `onchange` exceptions in the React `SearchBox` component
*   Fixes a bug that resulted in duplicate DOM node `id` attribute values when using the Autofill or SearchBox components, which caused unstable behavior in some development application frameworks such as Phoenix
*   Fixes an issue in Autofill where hitting the `enter` key while no suggestions are available would throw an error

### ⚠️ Breaking changes[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-search-js/guides/changelog/#%EF%B8%8F-breaking-changes-4 "Direct link to ⚠️ Breaking changes")

### 🐞 Bug fixes[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-search-js/guides/changelog/#-bug-fixes-8 "Direct link to 🐞 Bug fixes")

*   Bug fixes to improve API consistency and component stability

1.0.0-beta.6[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-search-js/guides/changelog/#100-beta6 "Direct link to 1.0.0-beta.6")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   Add `@mapbox/search-js-react` 🚀
*   Expose more interfaces within `index.d.ts`

Was this page helpful?YesNo

# Add a custom theme | Mapbox Search JS | Mapbox

[Skip to main content](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-search-js/example/theming/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

This example makes use of the [Control Theme API](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-search-js/api/web/theming/)
 to theme a listbox with a "retro" style.

JavaScript

    <!DOCTYPE html><html><head><meta charset="utf-8"><title>Add a custom theme</title><!-- Default styling. Feel free to remove! --><link href="https://api.mapbox.com/mapbox-assembly/v1.3.0/assembly.min.css" rel="stylesheet"><script id="search-js" defer="" src="https://api.mapbox.com/search-js/v1.5.0/web.js"></script></head><body><form class="prose">    <h3>Start typing for a custom theme!</h3>    <input name="address" autocomplete="shipping street-address" placeholder="Address"></form><script>// TO MAKE THE MAP APPEAR YOU MUST// ADD YOUR ACCESS TOKEN FROM// https://account.mapbox.comconst ACCESS_TOKEN = 'YOUR_MAPBOX_ACCESS_TOKEN';window.addEventListener('load', () => {    const collection = mapboxsearch.autofill({        accessToken: ACCESS_TOKEN    });    // Our retro theme!    collection.theme = {        variables: {            unitHeader: '28px',            fontFamily: '"MS Sans Serif",Tahoma,Verdana,Segoe,sans-serif',            border: '2px solid #424242',            borderRadius: '0',            boxShadow: '0 0 1px 1px rgba(0,0,0,0.1)',            colorText: '#212529',            colorPrimary: '#08216b',            colorSecondary: '#424242',            colorBackground: 'silver'        },        cssText: `            .Results,            .Modal,            .Input {                border-top-color:#fff;                border-left-color:#fff;                border-right-color:#333;                border-bottom-color:#333;            }        `,    };});</script></body></html>

Was this example helpful?YesNo

# Understanding the Geocoding API | Help | Mapbox

[メインコンテンツまでスキップ](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/geocoding/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

The [Mapbox Geocoding API](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/search/geocoding/)
 performs two main tasks: _forward geocoding_ and _reverse geocoding_. Forward geocoding converts text into geographic coordinates, for example, turning `2 Lincoln Memorial Circle NW` into `-77.050,38.889`. Reverse geocoding converts geographic coordinates into a text description, for example, turning `-77.050,38.889` into `2 Lincoln Memorial Circle NW`.

All geocoding requests require you to submit a _query_, or what you're trying to find. When you make a query, you get a _response_, a JSON-formatted document of the most relevant results from your query. This guide provides an overview of how the Geocoding API works, how to use it, how to provide feedback, and links to relevant documentation to get you started.

Geocoding demo[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/geocoding/#geocoding-demo "Geocoding demo への直接リンク")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

In the demo below, you can type in a location to make a Geocoding API query view then view the response as raw JSON:

The demo uses the [Mapbox GL geocoder plugin](https://github.com/mapbox/mapbox-gl-geocoder)
 to add forward geocoding to a web app built with Mapbox GL JS.

Geocoding results are returned in [GeoJSON](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc7946)
 format and contain both descriptive location information and geographic information.

You can use the geographic information returned in the JSON response to show your query on a map, like in this example, or do additional spatial analysis.

How geocoding works[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/geocoding/#how-geocoding-works "How geocoding works への直接リンク")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The [Mapbox Geocoding API](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/search/geocoding/)
 has two distinct parts: the **source data** we use to define locations and the **tools** we use to search for and return those locations.

### Source data[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/geocoding/#source-data "Source data への直接リンク")

The Mapbox Geocoding API contains [data sources](https://www.mapbox.com/about/maps/#data-sources)
 from governments, open data projects, and private companies. In some cases, results from the Geocoding API may differ from [Mapbox Streets](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/data/tilesets/reference/mapbox-streets-v8/)
 or OpenStreetMap data.

The Mapbox Geocoding API source data contains the following types of geographic information, ordered from the most granular to the largest:

*   **Points of interest (POI):** A named place including commercial businesses, public buildings, monuments, and parks, among other features.
*   **Address:** A specific mailing address, including the address number if applicable.
*   **Neighborhood:** A colloquial name for a smaller area within a place. Neighborhoods do not necessarily have specific, legally defined boundaries. Only present in some countries.
*   **Locality:** An administrative unit that is smaller than a place. Only present in some countries.
*   **Postcode:** A geographic area of the address component used for sorting mail.
*   **Place:** Cities, towns, and villages. Note that some large cities (such as Tokyo and Istanbul) may be categorized as regions rather than places.
*   **District:** An administrative unit that is larger than a place but smaller than a region. Only present in some countries.
*   **Region:** States, provinces, and prefectures. This is typically the largest sub-national administrative unit of a country. Note that some large cities (such as Tokyo and Istanbul) may be categorized as regions rather than places.
*   **Country:** Refers to generally recognized countries or, in some cases, areas with quasi-national administrative status such as Hong Kong. These areas have been assigned a designated country code under ISO 3166-1.

This hierarchy of feature types is also used to determine what will be returned as the encompassing parent features in a Geocoding API response object's [`context` property](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/search/geocoding/#geocoding-response-object)
. For example, if the returned feature is a `place` (like Detroit), then the encompassing parent features in the `context` property will be the `region` (the state of Michigan) and the `country` (United States).

### Customizing your queries[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/geocoding/#customizing-your-queries "Customizing your queries への直接リンク")

The Mapbox Geocoding API accepts many optional parameters. You can use these optional parameters to customize your queries so that the most relevant results are returned. These parameters can be specified using URL query parameters, or they can be specified as options with one of our client-side libraries or plugins. These parameters allow you to view results as you type, filter results by geographic feature type, and limit or bias results to a specified area. For example, to limit your search results to addresses in the Washington DC Metro area, you could set the `type` parameter to `address` and the `bbox` parameter to `-77.08,38.90,-76.99,38.95`. With those parameters set, your query for `Constitution Ave` will only return street addresses in the DC Metro area, and will not include features you're not interested in (like `Constitution Ave, El Paso, Texas 79908, United States`).

Read the [Geocoding API documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/search/geocoding/)
 for more information on available features.

### Language support[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/geocoding/#language-support "Language support への直接リンク")

The Mapbox Geocoding API accepts a `language` query parameter, which allows you to specify the language in which you would like to search. One or more languages can be specified using [ISO 639-1 codes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_ISO_639-1_codes)
. Translation availability varies by language and region, and you can expect more consistent results for areas where the specified language is most widely used. Language support has three different levels:

*   **Global coverage.** These languages are almost always present for `country`, `region`, and prominent `place` features.
*   **Local coverage.** These languages may lack global coverage, but they are almost always present for `country`, `region`, and prominent `place` features where they are widely used.
*   **Limited coverage.** These languages are sometimes present, but coverage tends to be inconsistent or geographically constrained.

For a current list of the languages covered at each level, see the [Geocoding API documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/search/#language-coverage)
.

### Search result prioritization[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/geocoding/#search-result-prioritization "Search result prioritization への直接リンク")

Results from geocoding queries are prioritized differently depending on whether the request was a _forward geocode_ or a _reverse geocode_.

#### Result prioritization in forward geocoding[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/geocoding/#result-prioritization-in-forward-geocoding "Result prioritization in forward geocoding への直接リンク")

When a forward geocoding query (human-readable text like “San Francisco”) is submitted to the Mapbox Geocoding API, the geocoder applies filters on the backend to sort the results that match, or partially match, the searched text. These filters are _textual relevance_ and _prominence score_.

##### Textual relevance[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/geocoding/#textual-relevance "Textual relevance への直接リンク")

When the geocoder sorts and prioritizes results, the first filter that is applied is `relevance`. `relevance` is a value that indicates how well a feature in our dataset matches the query. This property is surfaced in the Geocoding API response, and is a numerical score from `0` (the result does not match the queried text at all) to `1` (the result matches the queried text most completely). You can use the `relevance` property to remove results that don't fully match the query.

In this example query for the address “515 15th St NW, Washington, DC 20004”, the expected result is first in the response with a `relevance` of `0.875`. Other results in other cities, since they don’t match the search text as closely, have a `relevance` score of `0.2`.

    https://api.mapbox.com/search/geocode/v6/forward?q=515%2015th%20St%20NW%2C%20Washington%2C%20DC%2020004&types=address&access_token=YOUR_MAPBOX_ACCESS_TOKEN

##### Prominence score[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/geocoding/#prominence-score "Prominence score への直接リンク")

In the case that multiple features have the same `relevance` score, a second filter called `score` is applied. This is based on the popularity or prominence of a feature. For example, a search for “Paris” will equally match “Paris, France” and “Paris, Texas” — they’ll have the same `relevance` score. The `score` filter helps break this tie on the backend, and surfaces “Paris, France” first since it is a more popular feature:

    https://api.mapbox.com/search/geocode/v6/forward?q=paris&access_token=YOUR_MAPBOX_ACCESS_TOKEN

#### Result prioritization in reverse geocoding[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/geocoding/#result-prioritization-in-reverse-geocoding "Result prioritization in reverse geocoding への直接リンク")

For reverse geocodes, results at a given set of coordinates are sorted by order of **spatial hierarchy**. For example, a more granular feature such as an `address`, `poi`, or `postcode` will return first in the response before feature types like `region` or `country`. The full spatial hierarchy, ordered from the most granular to the largest, is: _point of interest (POI)_, _address_, _neighborhood_, _locality_, _postcode_, _place_, _district_, _region_, and _country_. (For more details about these geographic information types, see the [Source data section](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/geocoding/#source-data)
.)

This reverse geocoding query example returns features closest to the query point (in the case of point features like `address` and `poi`) and features that contain the query point (in the case of polygon features like `place` or `region`), in order of hierarchy from the most granular (address or POI) to the largest feature (country):

    https://api.mapbox.com/search/geocode/v6/reverse?longitude=-122.463&latitude=37.7648&access_token=YOUR_MAPBOX_ACCESS_TOKEN

Providing geocoding feedback[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/geocoding/#providing-geocoding-feedback "Providing geocoding feedback への直接リンク")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

If you find an error or want to contribute map data, you can use our [Improve the map](https://www.mapbox.com/contribute/)
 tool.

このpageは役に立ちましたか？はいいいえ

# Keep access tokens private in open source iOS and Android apps | Help | Mapbox

[メインコンテンツまでスキップ](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/private-access-token-android-and-ios/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

An app using the [Mapbox Maps SDK for iOS](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/ja/maps/guides/)
 or the [Mapbox Maps SDK for Android](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/ja/maps/guides/)
, must provide an [access token](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/access-token/)
 to display Mapbox-hosted maps and use other Mapbox services.

It is important to practice responsible token management with all Mapbox access tokens. This guide expands on general [token management](https://docs.mapbox.com/accounts/ja/guides/tokens/#creating-and-managing-access-tokens)
 strategies to provide guidance specific to iOS and Android applications.

iOS[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/private-access-token-android-and-ios/#ios "iOS への直接リンク")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1.  Create a new plain text file containing your access token, named either `.mapbox` or `mapbox`. To avoid accidentally committing this file to an open-source project, either you can save it to a location outside your project's version-controlled directory, or you can add this file to your project’s [.gitignore](https://help.github.com/articles/ignoring-files/)
     file.
2.  Open your project in Xcode. In the project editor, go to the Build Phases tab, then click the + button to add a new Run Script phase to the end.
3.  Customize the Run Script build phase to run the following code (replacing `~/.mapbox` or `~/mapbox` with the path to the file you added in step 1):
    
        # Look for a global file named 'mapbox' or '.mapbox' within the home directorytoken_file=~/.mapboxtoken_file2=~/mapboxtoken="$(cat $token_file 2>/dev/null || cat $token_file2 2>/dev/null)"if [ "$token" ]; then  plutil -replace MBXAccessToken -string $token "$TARGET_BUILD_DIR/$INFOPLIST_PATH"else  echo 'warning: Missing Mapbox access token'  open 'https://console.mapbox.com/account/access-tokens/'  echo "warning: Get an access token from <https://console.mapbox.com/account/access-tokens/>, then create a new file at $token_file or $token_file2 that contains the access token."fi
    
4.  Add `$(TARGET_BUILD_DIR)/$(INFOPLIST_PATH)` to the build phase’s Input Files section. Otherwise, the access token may be overridden during incremental builds. Optionally, you can also add `~/.mapbox` or `~/mapbox` to this section, so that Xcode will automatically update Info.plist after you change your access token.

When building the project in Xcode, the access token will be inserted into the Info.plist inside your built app, but not into the Info.plist that you’d commit.

Android[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/private-access-token-android-and-ios/#android "Android への直接リンク")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Git-based option[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/private-access-token-android-and-ios/#git-based-option "Git-based option への直接リンク")

Be aware that to enable continuous integration builds with the following approach, you can take advantage of [a Gradle script that the Mapbox Android team uses itself](https://github.com/mapbox/mapbox-gl-native-android/blob/master/gradle/gradle-config.gradle)
.

1.  [Create a local `.gitignore` file in your repository folder](https://help.github.com/articles/ignoring-files/)
    . If the folder already has a `.gitignore` file, locate and open it.
2.  Add the following line to the `.gitignore` file : `YOUR_APP_MODULE_NAME/src/main/res/values/developer-config.xml`. This will tell the Git system to ignore the XML values file that you will create in the next step.
3.  Navigate to your project's `src/main/res/values` folder and make a new file titled `developer-config.xml`.
4.  Create an `mapbox_access_token` String resource and paste your access token into it:

    <string name="mapbox_access_token">PASTE_YOUR_TOKEN_HERE</string>

5.  At this point, the `R.string.mapbox_access_token` string resource should be discoverable wherever you deliver the access token to the `Mapbox` class such as: `Mapbox.getInstance(context, getString(R.string.mapbox_access_token));`
6.  Because of step #3 above, the Git system shouldn't be tracking the `developer-config.xml` file at all.
7.  You're all set! You can use and change the Mapbox access token locally on your machine without the chance of committing and pushing it to a public platform such as GitHub!

### Non-Git option[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/private-access-token-android-and-ios/#non-git-option "Non-Git option への直接リンク")

1.  Find or create a `gradle.properties` file in your Gradle user home folder. The folder is located at `«USER_HOME»/.gradle`. Once the file is created, its path should be `«USER_HOME»/.gradle/gradle.properties`. [More information about Gradle properties](https://docs.gradle.org/current/userguide/build_environment.html#sec:gradle_configuration_properties)
    .
2.  Add a key-value pair to the `gradle.properties` file: `MAPBOX_ACCESS_TOKEN="PASTE_YOUR_TOKEN_HERE";`
3.  Open your app-level `build.gradle` file. This file is usually the one where you define specific dependencies for your app, such as the Mapbox Maps SDK for Android.
4.  Add `MAPBOX_ACCESS_TOKEN` as a `buildConfigField` entry to whichever buildTypes your app has:

    android {	buildTypes {	  debug {	    ...	    buildConfigField 'String', "MapboxAccessToken", MAPBOX_ACCESS_TOKEN	    ...	  }	  release {	  ...	    buildConfigField 'String', "MapboxAccessToken", MAPBOX_ACCESS_TOKEN	  ...	  }	}}

5\. Because your Gradle files have changed, make sure to sync the project with the latest Gradle files. 6. Now you can access your Mapbox access token in your app to initialize the Mapbox Maps SDK for Android: `Mapbox.getInstance(context, BuildConfig.MapboxAccessToken);`

このpageは役に立ちましたか？はいいいえ

# Help

[メインコンテンツまでスキップ](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

How can we help?

### 

Mapbox のはじめ方

Mapbox の主要製品とツールの概要、そして強力な位置情報アプリケーションを構築するための基本ステップとリソースをご紹介します。  
  

[Learn more→](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/)

### 

さらに深く探る

地図の概念や mapbox のサービスについてさらに深く探りましょう。  
  

[Learn more→](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/)

### 

チュートリアル

ステップバイステップのガイドを見つけて、プロジェクトの開始や次のステップに進むのに役立てましょう。  
  

[Learn more→](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/tutorials/)

### 

トラブルシューティング

Mapbox製品に共通する問題を解決します。  
  

[Learn more→](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/troubleshooting/)

### 

用語集

mapbox に関連する用語とその定義を見つけましょう。  
  

[Learn more→](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/)

Mapbox Support
--------------

\*Only subscribers to a paid Mapbox Support plan can submit technical support tickets.

### 

Ticket Portal

Support Plan subscribers can submit and view their technical support tickets here.

[See your open tickets→](https://support.mapbox.com/hc/en-us)

### 

Ticket Portal

Support Plan subscribers can submit and view their technical support tickets here.

[FAQ→](https://docs.mapbox.com/accounts/ja/faq/)
[Open a Ticket→](https://support.mapbox.com/hc/en-us)

### 

Support plans

Benefit from guaranteed response times, customized assistance, and emergency channels.

[Find a support plan→](https://www.mapbox.com/support#support-plans)

### 

Support plans

Explore community run developer resources for Mapbox products.

[Mapbox Developer Discord→](https://discord.gg/uMpcC5RmJh)
[Stack Overflow→](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/tagged/mapbox)
[Reddit→](https://www.reddit.com/r/mapbox/)

### 

Submit Map Updates

Submit a map, address or POI data update, or modification to navigation details such as speed limits or road closures.

[Contribute→](https://labs.mapbox.com/contribute/)

### 

AI Developer Assistant

Use our AI chat to get answers on how to use Mapbox software & troubleshoot issues.

[AI Developer Assistant→](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/ask-ai/)

### 

API Status

Check the status of all Mapbox APIs and SDKs. Set notifications in case status is impacted.

[API Status→](https://status.mapbox.com/)

# Access Tokens | Help | Mapbox

[メインコンテンツまでスキップ](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/access-tokens/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

To use any of Mapbox's tools, APIs, or SDKs, you'll need a Mapbox [access token](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/access-token/)
. You learn the basics about access tokens, such as creating new tokens or deleting existing ones by following the [Getting Started Token Guide](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/access-tokens/)
. This guide covers more advanced topics, like scopes, token rotation, etc.

There are two main types of tokens:

*   **Public Tokens** - for use in client-side applications
*   **Secrets Tokens** - for use in server-side applications

Token Patterns

All public access tokens start with `pk` while all secret access tokens start with `sk`.

Public Tokens[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/access-tokens/#public-tokens "Public Tokens への直接リンク")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Public tokens** are designed to be used in client-side applications, meaning they can be safely exposed in web browsers, mobile apps, and other client environments.

They typically have limited permissions to access resources like tilesets, styles, datasets, and geocoding services. Public tokens restrict users from changing, deleting, or creating resources, granting only read access.

These tokens are ideal for applications where you expect end users to interact with Mapbox maps (e.g., displaying a map in a mobile or web app) without needing administrative privileges.

Since they are exposed in the client, they are considered less secure than private tokens. They should be configured with the least amount of access necessary to limit exposure.

### Your Default public token[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/access-tokens/#your-default-public-token "Your Default public token への直接リンク")

A **default public token** is automatically added to your Mapbox account so you can get started quickly without having to create a token yourself. It is intended for use in development, and should be replaced with a new public token once your app is ready for deployment.

This default public token is located on the right of your [accounts page](https://console.mapbox.com/)
. If you would like to create _additional_ public token for established projects, see our [Creating additional public access tokens](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/access-tokens/#creating-public-tokens)
 below.

Secret Tokens[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/access-tokens/#secret-tokens "Secret Tokens への直接リンク")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Secret tokens** are designed for server-side applications, keeping them hidden from end users.

These tokens often have broader permissions, allowing full access to Mapbox resources, including creating, modifying, and deleting resources such as tilesets, styles, datasets, etc.

Since they provide elevated access, private tokens should never be exposed in client-side code. They are intended for environments where their confidentiality can be guaranteed, such as on a server or in an API.

Creating and deleting access tokens[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/access-tokens/#create-and-deleting-access-tokens "Creating and deleting access tokens への直接リンク")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

You can create and delete access tokens from the [Access Token page](https://console.mapbox.com/account/access-tokens/)
 on your Developer Console. Follow the sections below to learn how to create and delete tokens.

### Creating public tokens[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/access-tokens/#creating-public-tokens "Creating public tokens への直接リンク")

To create a new public token, follow these steps:

1.  Go to the [Create a token page](https://console.mapbox.com/account/access-tokens/create)
     or click the blue **Create a token** button on the [Access Token page](https://console.mapbox.com/account/access-tokens/)
     in the Developer console.
2.  Name your token something relevant to the project it will be used in.
3.  Next scroll to the scope section and select the only the scopes the public token should have access to. To learn more about scopes, see the [Scopes](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/access-tokens/#scopes)
     section.
4.  Click the **Create token** button at the bottom of the page to create your token.
5.  Enter your password to confirm the creation of your token.

Now, you'll be returned to your account's tokens page, where you can copy your new token.

This video walks through the steps above:

Your browser doesn't support HTML5 video. Open [link to the video](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/assets/medias/create-additional-public-token-5aaf4e9db4f4c09375054772c0d6855f.mp4)
.

### Creating secret tokens[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/access-tokens/#creating-secret-access-tokens "Creating secret tokens への直接リンク")

To create a new secret token, follow these steps:

1.  Go to the [Create a token page](https://console.mapbox.com/account/access-tokens/create)
     or click the blue **Create a token** button on the [Access Token page](https://console.mapbox.com/account/access-tokens/)
     in the Developer console.
2.  Name your token something relevant to your token's needs.
3.  Scroll down to the **Secret Scopes** section and check the relevant scope boxes.

*   This will be specified in whatever tutorial or guide you are following along with, so be sure to only allow the necessary scopes required.
*   To learn more about scopes, see the [**Scopes**](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/access-tokens/#scopes)
     section above.

4.  Scroll down and click the **Create token** button at the bottom of the page to create your token.
5.  Enter your password to confirm the creation of your token.

Now, you'll be returned to your account's tokens page, where you can copy your created token.

Storing Secret Tokens

This token is a secret token, which means you will only have one opportunity to copy it, so save this token somewhere secure.

The following video walks through an example of creating a token with the `Downloads:Read` scope, so the developer can add the Mapbox Maps Android SDK to their project.

Your browser doesn't support HTML5 video. Open [link to the video](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/assets/medias/create-secret-access-token-823407693dc7c7f3351fb5c23377d5bf.mp4)
.

### Deleting tokens[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/access-tokens/#deleting-tokens "Deleting tokens への直接リンク")

If you would like to delete a token, follow these steps:

1.  Go to your account's [Access Token page](https://console.mapbox.com/account/access-tokens/)
    . You may be prompted to log into your account.
2.  Find the token you would like to delete in your list of tokens.
3.  Click the 3 dots next to the token and select delete in the dropdown.
4.  Review the information presented on the prompted confirmation screen and confirm the data shown is what you would expect for usage on this token.
5.  Once your review is finished, click the blue **Yes, delete token** button.
6.  Enter your password and click the submit button to delete your token.

You will see a small green prompt at the top of the screen confirming your token has been deleted and the token will no longer appear in your token list.

### Tokens API[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/access-tokens/#tokens-api "Tokens API への直接リンク")

The [Mapbox Tokens API](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/accounts/tokens/)
 let's you create and delete tokens programmatically. This can be useful for rotating tokens or managing large numbers of tokens associated with a single account.

To learn how to create tokens with the Token API see the [Create a Token](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/accounts/tokens/#create-a-token)
 section of the Token API guide.

To learn more about deleting tokens programmatically, see our the [delete a token](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/accounts/tokens/#delete-a-token)
 section of our Token API.

Refreshing Your Default Public token[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/access-tokens/#deleting-public-tokens "Refreshing Your Default Public token への直接リンク")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Your account always has a default public access token. It can be refreshed, but cannot be deleted.

Follow these steps to refresh your default public access token:

1.  Go to your account's [Access Token page](https://console.mapbox.com/account/access-tokens/)
    . You may be prompted to log into your account.
2.  Find your `Default public token` which should be at the top of your list of tokens.
3.  Click the refresh button.
4.  Review the information presented on the confirmation prompt and confirm the data shown is what you would expect.
5.  Once your review is finished, click the blue `Yes, refresh token` button.
6.  Enter your password and click the submit button to delete your token.

Now you have a new default public access token. Note that all references to your previous token will no longer work and will need to be replaced with the new token value.

Scopes[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/access-tokens/#scopes "Scopes への直接リンク")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Each access token you create will have a set of permissions that allow the token to make certain types of requests to Mapbox APIs -- these are called **scopes**. The [API documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/)
 lists the scopes required for each Mapbox API. When creating an access token, you will have the option to add [public or secret scopes](https://docs.mapbox.com/accounts/ja/guides/tokens/#scopes)
 to the token.

**For a complete list of available scopes and recommendations see the [Account documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/accounts/ja/guides/tokens/#scopes)
.**

Security for Scopes and Tokens

We recommend only selecting the scopes necessary for the intended use of the token.

URL restrictions[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/access-tokens/#url-restrictions "URL restrictions への直接リンク")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

You can make access tokens for web maps more secure by adding URL restrictions. When you add a URL restriction to a token, that token will only work for requests that originate from the URLs you specify. Tokens without restrictions will work for requests originating from any URL.

**For more information on requirements and details for implementing URL restrictions, see the [Account documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/accounts/ja/guides/tokens/#url-restrictions)
.**

Token use statistics[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/access-tokens/#token-use-statistics "Token use statistics への直接リンク")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

You can see the use statistics for all your tokens, for any specified period, on your Mapbox account [Statistics page](https://console.mapbox.com/account/statistics/)
.

How do access tokens work?[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/access-tokens/#how-access-tokens-work "How do access tokens work? への直接リンク")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Mapbox uses JSON Web Tokens (JWT) as the token format. Each token is a string delimited by dots into three parts: header, payload, and signature as described in the [Tokens API documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/accounts/#token-format)
. Every token has a metadata object that contains properties with information about the token, like `id` (unique identifier), `note` (human readable name), `scopes` (capabilities), `allowedURLs` (URLs that token is authorized for), and timestamps for `created` and `modified` (last modification). For more information on the metadata object’s properties, see our [Tokens API documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/accounts/#token-metadata-object)

Additional Resources[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/access-tokens/#additional-resources "Additional Resources への直接リンク")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   [Rotating tokens](https://docs.mapbox.com/accounts/ja/guides/tokens/)
    
*   [Using Mapbox securely](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/how-to-use-mapbox-securely/#access-tokens)
    
*   [Managing tokens for team accounts](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/collaboration-best-practices/#setting-up-an-account-for-a-team)
     and [client projects](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/collaboration-best-practices/#setting-up-an-account-for-a-client)
    
*   [Understanding how tokens relate to statistics](https://docs.mapbox.com/accounts/ja/guides/statistics/#filter-usage-data)
    

このpageは役に立ちましたか？はいいいえ

---

# Understanding Android APK size | Help | Mapbox

[メインコンテンツまでスキップ](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/android-apk-size/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

The [Mapbox Maps SDK for Android](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/ja/maps/guides/)
 is a complex and powerful framework, but it only contributes 3–4 megabytes to the ultimate size of your application. This guide suggests a few strategies for reducing the final Android app APK size.

Shrink code and resources[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/android-apk-size/#shrink-code-and-resources "Shrink code and resources への直接リンク")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Forgotten code, menus, images, strings, and more can pile up, but your final APK might not use them all.

[ProGuard](https://developer.android.com/build/shrink-code)
 and [DexGuard](https://www.guardsquare.com/dexguard/)
, even without obfuscation, will remove unused Java code from your code and its dependencies. Don't forget to check for and set up necessary ProGuard rules if you're using other third-party libraries!

Adding `shrinkResources` to your app-level Gradle file will remove unused resource files that live in the `/res` folder. Adding `minifyEnabled` runs ProGuard to shrink code by deleting unused code and other references. **Set both to `true`**. Both are quick wins in making sure that your compiled project only includes what it truly needs.

    android {    ...    buildTypes {        release {            minifyEnabled true            shrinkResources true            ...        }    }}

Drop unnecessary architectures[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/android-apk-size/#drop-unnecessary-architectures "Drop unnecessary architectures への直接リンク")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The Mapbox Maps SDK ships with three architectures:

    ./arm64-v8a/libmapbox-maps.so./x86/libmapbox-maps.so./x86_64/libmapbox-maps.so

Each of these files add up to the resulting APK. If your app doesn't need x86 support, for example, you could drop `x86` and `x86_64` to save some space. See [Leverage ABI splits](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/android-apk-size/#leverage-abi-splits)
 below for details.

Exclude unnecessary modules[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/android-apk-size/#exclude-unnecessary-modules "Exclude unnecessary modules への直接リンク")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The Maps SDK is a multi-module Gradle project with Android Libraries and Android applications. Each of these modules add up to the resulting APK.

If a module is prefixed with the name `plugin` it indicates it's an optional module that will be loaded automatically at runtime if it's part of the compilation classpath. Plugins can be excluded with a Gradle exclusion to disable a plugin fully and to save binary size. By default the SDK includes the majority of plugins found in [`mapbox-maps-android`](https://github.com/mapbox/mapbox-maps-android)
.

For example, if you do not plan to use animations in your application, you could remove the animation plugin:

    implementation ('com.mapbox.maps:android:11.17.1'){    exclude group: 'com.mapbox.plugin', module: 'maps-animation'}

Leverage ABI splits[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/android-apk-size/#leverage-abi-splits "Leverage ABI splits への直接リンク")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This is a feature that lets you build an APK file for each CPU, only containing the relevant native libraries. This process is described in the [Android Studio Project Site](https://developer.android.com/build/configure-apk-splits)
.

If you distribute your app via Google Play, you can use this approach through the [Multiple APK Support](https://developer.android.com/google/play/publishing/multiple-apks)
 distribution feature.

Leveraging APK splits is one of the tips we give users when it comes to shrinking their APK size. Splitting results in building different APK for the different supported ABIs in an application. Google Play is optimized to only download the APK for the ABI of the device that is installing the app.

Find [more information about multiple APKs](https://developer.android.com/build/configure-apk-splits)
 in the Android Studio documentation.

このpageは役に立ちましたか？はいいいえ

---

# How Mapbox Directions services work | Help | Mapbox

[メインコンテンツまでスキップ](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/directions/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

Mapbox Directions services power navigation experiences with intelligent routing, accurate travel time estimates, and real-time traffic awareness. This guide breaks down how the Mapbox routing engine calculates routes for different transportation modes, how travel times are estimated using map and traffic data, and where real-time and typical traffic conditions are available around the world. Whether you're building turn-by-turn directions, delivery optimizations, or isochrone visualizations, understanding these fundamentals will help you make the most of our directions-related APIs.

The directions-related APIs include:

*   [Directions API](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/navigation/#directions)
    
*   [Matrix API](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/navigation/#matrix)
    
*   [Optimization API](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/navigation/#optimization)
    
*   [Isochrone API](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/navigation/#isochrone)
    

### The routing network[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/directions/#the-routing-network "The routing network への直接リンク")

The Mapbox **routing network** can create routes and optimized trips, and is used to calculate the most efficient path for a given mode of transportation (driving, cycling, and walking). The routing network includes a robust network of roads and paths (also known as _ways_) with distinct attributes like speed limits, turn lane restrictions, mode of travel, and accessibility for bikes and pedestrians.

Ways

A _way_ is an [OpenStreetMap](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/osm/)
 term used to describe an ordered list of nodes (points) which normally also has at least one tag, or description. For the purposes of the Mapbox Navigation service APIs, ways can be roads, foot paths, or bicycle lanes.

### Travel times[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/directions/#travel-times "Travel times への直接リンク")

The Mapbox [Directions API](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/navigation/directions/)
, [Matrix API](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/navigation/matrix/)
, and [Optimization API](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/navigation/optimization/)
 all provide estimated trip durations. The time it takes to travel from one point to another is determined by many factors, including:

*   The [routing profile](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/navigation/directions/#routing-profiles)
     used (`walking`, `cycling`, `driving`, `driving-traffic`).
*   The speed stored in the [`maxspeed` tag in OpenStreetMap](http://wiki.openstreetmap.org/wiki/Key:maxspeed)
    .
*   Traffic derived from real-time telemetry data (when the traffic profile is used).

### Traffic data[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/directions/#traffic-data "Traffic data への直接リンク")

Incorporate real-time and typical traffic data using the `mapbox/driving-traffic` routing profile into route selection and ETA generation with the Mapbox [Directions API](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/navigation/#directions)
, [Map Matching API](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/navigation/#map-matching)
, [Matrix API](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/navigation/#matrix)
, either into computing areas of reachability with the Mapbox [Isochrone API](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/navigation/#isochrone)
, or add a traffic layer to road geometries on a visual map. Read more about our traffic tileset in [Mapbox data](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/mapbox-data/)
.

The `mapbox/driving-traffic` routing profile is available globally, but the availability of traffic varies by country. Real-time traffic shows speeds that have been directly observed in the last 15 minutes. Real-time traffic data is useful if there are atypical traffic patterns (for example, during severe weather events or accidents).

When real-time traffic conditions are not available, typical traffic conditions will be used instead. Typical traffic is an estimate for traffic speeds at a specific location, at a specific time, based on historical observations. Typical traffic data reflects normal traffic conditions during a typical week.

#### Traffic Coverage[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/directions/#traffic-coverage "Traffic Coverage への直接リンク")

For the following countries, the returned ETA is calculated using both real-time and typical traffic data along the requested route.

*   Afghanistan
*   Albania

# Understanding iOS framework size | Help | Mapbox

[メインコンテンツまでスキップ](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/ios-framework-size/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

The [Mapbox Maps SDK for iOS](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/ja/maps/guides/)
 is a complex and powerful framework, but it only contributes 3–4 megabytes to the ultimate size of your application. This guide suggests a few strategies for reducing the final app size.

App thinning[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/ios-framework-size/#app-thinning "App thinning への直接リンク")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Apple packages optimized, device-specific versions of your app via [app thinning](https://developer.apple.com/library/content/documentation/IDEs/Conceptual/AppDistributionGuide/AppThinning/AppThinning.html)
. What your users download only includes code and resources required by their specific device. You can [generate an app size report](https://developer.apple.com/library/content/qa/qa1795/_index.html#//apple_ref/doc/uid/DTS40014195-CH1-MEASURE)
 using Xcode or look at the “App Store File Sizes” report in iTunes Connect to see what the actual initial download size of your app will be. Note that these figures represent the maximum amount that your users will download — see the [application updates](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/ios-framework-size/#application-updates)
 section of this page for more information on how updates are optimized.

CPU architectures[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/ios-framework-size/#cpu-architectures "CPU architectures への直接リンク")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Our framework includes a multi-architecture binary that contains slices for `armv7`, `arm64`, `i386`, and `x86_64` CPU architectures. ARM slices are used by physical iOS devices, while `i386` and `x86_64` are used by Simulator and are stripped from your app during the build and archive process. When a user downloads your app from the App Store, they receive only the architecture that their device requires.

Bitcode[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/ios-framework-size/#bitcode "Bitcode への直接リンク")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[Bitcode](https://developer.apple.com/library/content/documentation/IDEs/Conceptual/AppDistributionGuide/AppThinning/AppThinning.html#//apple_ref/doc/uid/TP40012582-CH35-SW2)
 is included for ARM architectures but does not affect the final size of your application. Bitcode is an unoptimized intermediate code representation that is used by Apple for potential recompilation and re-optimization of your app binary, after App Store submission. As metadata solely intended for build optimization, bitcode is never downloaded by your users.

If you have disabled bitcode in your project, it is _not_ necessary for you to remove bitcode from frameworks that contain it. Regardless of whether your app takes advantage of bitcode, the version of your app that the App Store delivers to your users never contains bitcode.

Application updates[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/ios-framework-size/#application-updates "Application updates への直接リンク")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

When you update your application, your users download only the files that have changed in that update. These partial updates are commonly known as “delta updates”. If resources and binaries in your app have not changed, they will not be downloaded again. See Apple’s [Reducing Download Size for iOS App Updates](https://developer.apple.com/library/content/qa/qa1779/_index.html)
 guide for more information on how to take advantage of delta updates.

Debug symbols[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/ios-framework-size/#debug-symbols "Debug symbols への直接リンク")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The Mapbox Maps SDK for iOS framework download includes `dSYM` and `BCSymbolMap` files. These are used for [crash symbolication](https://developer.apple.com/library/content/technotes/tn2151/_index.html#//apple_ref/doc/uid/DTS40008184-CH1-SYMBOLICATION)
 and are not included in the app that your users download.

Dynamic versus static frameworks[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/ios-framework-size/#dynamic-versus-static-frameworks "Dynamic versus static frameworks への直接リンク")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Following Apple’s platform guidelines, Mapbox recommends using the dynamic-linked version of the SDK in your app. If you have installed the SDK via [CocoaPods](https://www.mapbox.com/install/ios/cocoapods/)
 or [Carthage](https://www.mapbox.com/install/ios/carthage/)
, you are already using the dynamic framework. See Apple’s [Overview of Dynamic Libraries](https://developer.apple.com/library/content/documentation/DeveloperTools/Conceptual/DynamicLibraries/100-Articles/OverviewOfDynamicLibraries.html)
 for information on how dynamic libraries work and a discussion of their benefits.

Mapbox does additionally build and publish a statically-linked version of the SDK, but we do not recommend it for general use.

Reducing the size of your app[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/ios-framework-size/#reducing-the-size-of-your-app "Reducing the size of your app への直接リンク")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

We recommend that you read Apple’s [Reducing the size of my App](https://developer.apple.com/library/ios/qa/qa1795/_index.html)
 guide to learn about ways to accurately measure and reduce the size of your app.

このpageは役に立ちましたか？はいいいえ

---

# How to get support while building with Mapbox | Help | Mapbox

[メインコンテンツまでスキップ](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/implementation-support/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

Detailed below are several options for receiving support while working on Mapbox implementations.

Join our Discord[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/implementation-support/#join-our-discord "Join our Discord への直接リンク")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Ask questions in the [Mapbox Developer Discord Community Server](https://discord.gg/uMpcC5RmJh)
 and discuss solutions with thousands of developers building with Mapbox tools and services.

This Discord server is organized into channels corresponding to particular Mapbox products. Before asking a question, make sure that you are posting it in the most relevant channel.

Community Guidelines

Make sure you also abide by the Community Guidelines listed in the **#rules** channel. These guidelines will automatically appear immediately after joining for you to review.

Ask-AI Tool[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/implementation-support/#ask-ai-tool "Ask-AI Tool への直接リンク")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The [Ask-AI tool](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/ask-ai/)
 can answer questions and create code snippets by indexing of all 2000 pages of our documentation as well as any mapbox-related stack overflow posts and generating a response.

Access this tool by viewing its [dedicated webpage](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/ask-ai/)
 or by clicking the **Ask AI** icon in the bottom right corner of each page of our documentation.

Stack Overflow[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/implementation-support/#stack-overflow "Stack Overflow への直接リンク")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[Stack Overflow](https://stackoverflow.com/)
 is a programming forum where users can ask and answer technical questions.

Many Mapbox users have posted and/or answered implementation questions on these forums, so this is an excellent resource to search for community solutions to Mapbox issues.

If an answer to your question is not already posted, you can [create a new forum post](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask)
, but be sure to include relevant tags and as much detail as possible (code snippets, images, environment details, etc.).

Mapbox Tags on Stack Overflow

Stack Overflow uses tags to categorize questions. Mapbox's products fall under several tags, so be sure to choose an appropriate tag for your post:

*   Mapbox GL JS: [`mapbox-gl-js`](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/tagged/mapbox-gl-js)
    
*   Mapbox Android SDKs: [`mapbox-android`](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/tagged/mapbox-android)
    
*   Mapbox iOS SDKs: [`mapbox-ios`](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/tagged/mapbox-ios)
    
*   Mapbox Studio: [`mapbox-studio`](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/tagged/mapbox-studio)
    
*   Directions API: [`mapbox-api-directions`](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/tagged/mapbox-api-directions)
    
*   Geocoding API: [`mapbox-api-geocoding`](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/tagged/mapbox-api-geocoding)
    

Hire a Developer[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/implementation-support/#hire-a-developer "Hire a Developer への直接リンク")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The Mapbox Developer Network is a community of independent developers, designers, geospatial professionals, and other freelancers who work with Mapbox tools who are available for hire. Search, preview, and get in touch with someone who can help you get your project rolling.

To get started, click `Find a Developer` on our [Developer Network](https://www.mapbox.com/developer-network/)
 landing page. You will be able to browse for developers with skills that match your project needs. Once you find some good candidates, you can reach out directly. Adding key details when you first reach out will help developers to understand whether they can take on the project, and will speed up the time to get a response. Consider including the following details on your first message such as:

*   Project title and description
*   Approximate deadline
*   Approximate budget
*   Contact information
*   Non-Disclosure Agreement (if required)

If there's a match, you will hear back from the developers themselves and the process will continue between you and the developer.

Paid Mapbox Support Plan[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/implementation-support/#paid-mapbox-support-plan "Paid Mapbox Support Plan への直接リンク")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Mapbox offers paid [support plans](https://www.mapbox.com/support#support-plans)
 that allow access to 1:1 help from our Technical Support team. Depending on the support plan you choose, our team of experts will be able to help provide guidance on proper API usage, explain how different APIs or workflows could be used to achieve your expected results, and provide recommendations on how to optimize your solution's architecture.
