    }\
  \]
}

In this example, you can see a visual representation of a dataset inside the **Mapbox Studio dataset editor** on the left and the actual contents of the **dataset**, a GeoJSON FeatureCollection, on the right. The contents of the dataset include geometry and properties for each point that you can see on the left, but does not contain any style information. For more details on how to style the points (for example, specify the symbols, colors, and the font for labels), see the [Upload data](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/map-developer-journey/data-guide/)
 guide, which covers converting datasets to tilesets.

### Downloading datasets[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/creating-data/#downloading-datasets "Downloading datasets への直接リンク")

Once you’ve created a dataset, you can export it to a tileset for use in a Mapbox map (see the [How map design works guide](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/map-design/)
 for more information), download it as a GeoJSON FeatureCollection, or access it with the Mapbox Datasets API. Datasets can still be edited after export, but you will need to make sure to sync your downloaded version with your new edits if you would like your local copy to reflect any changes you've made.

To learn how to download and export datasets, see the [manage datasets](https://docs.mapbox.com/studio-manual/ja/reference/datasets/#download-as-geojson)
 section of the Mapbox Studio Manual.

### Dataset to tileset process[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/creating-data/#dataset-to-tileset-process "Dataset to tileset process への直接リンク")

You cannot add datasets directly to styles in the Mapbox Studio style editor. But you can export your dataset as a tileset and add it to your style in the Mapbox Studio style editor or Mapbox GL JS. When you export your dataset to a tileset, it is processed in the same way an upload would be processed.

To learn how to convert a dataset to a tileset using the Mapbox Studio dataset editor, see the [dataset editor](https://docs.mapbox.com/studio-manual/ja/reference/datasets/#dataset-editor)
 section of the Mapbox Studio Manual.

Tilesets[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/creating-data/#tilesets "Tilesets への直接リンク")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Tilesets** are lightweight collections of map data that are optimized for rendering and are not _editable_ but can be _styled_ in the Mapbox Studio style editor.

### What are tilesets used for?[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/creating-data/#what-are-tilesets-used-for "What are tilesets used for? への直接リンク")

Consider uploading your data as a tileset if your data is large and doesn't need to be updated often.

If you created a vector tileset by uploading geospatial data that contains attributes, the resulting vector tileset will inherit the attributes contained in your raw data source. Here are a few examples of what you can do with the attributes belonging to features in a vector tileset:

*   Use [Mapbox Studio](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/tutorials/choropleth-studio-gl-pt-1/)
     to customize the color of a state based on its population density.
*   Use [Mapbox GL JS](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/ja/example/query-similar-features/)
     to highlight counties that share a similar name.

### How tilesets work[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/creating-data/#how-tilesets-work "How tilesets work への直接リンク")

When you upload data as a tileset or export a dataset as a tileset, Mapbox breaks it up into a uniform grid of square tiles at preset zoom levels. It must undergo many processing steps to load quickly and be lightweight.

When you use either the Uploads API or Mapbox Studio (which uses the Uploads API behind the scenes) to turn data into a tileset, two things happen:

1.  **Simplification:** The Mapbox Uploads API analyzes your data for complexity and automatically removes unnecessary vertices at variable rates depending on zoom level. This process is called _simplification_, and it helps make sure your maps load quickly, especially at lower zoom levels where additional complexity would not be visible.
2.  **Determining the zoom extent.** The Mapbox Uploads API keeps your tilesets light by examining your data's extent (the geographic area covered by your data) and complexity and only creates vector tiles for zoom levels that are likely to be required — a [zoom extent](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/zoom-extent/)
     is defined. For datasets with small zoom extents or complex features, this usually means low zoom levels (zoomed out) will be discarded. For large features that are not complex, this usually means higher zoom levels (zoomed in) will be discarded. If you need your tileset to be visible at a different zoom extent, you can [adjust this manually](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/troubleshooting/adjust-tileset-zoom-extent/)
    , but it is important to note the following:

*   Minimum zoom levels can be increased, but not decreased past the assigned minimum zoom level.
*   Maximum zoom levels can be decreased and increased, since data can be [overzoomed](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/overzoom/)
     and visualized to zoom 22.

If you use Mapbox Tiling Service to turn data into a tileset, you will set the simplification level and the zoom extent in the [_tileset recipe_](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-tiling-service/ja/overview/tileset-recipes/)
.

You can create tilesets by uploading data in any of the following formats:

| Format | Upload limit |
| --- | --- |
| CSV | 1 GB transfer limit (300 MB if uploaded with Studio) |
| GeoJSON | 1 GB transfer limit (300 MB if uploaded with Studio) |
| MBTiles | 25 GB transfer limit (300 MB if uploaded with Studio) |
| KML | 260 MB with 15 or fewer layers |
| GPX | 260 MB |
| Shapefile | 260 MB (combined uncompressed size of .shp and .dbf files). You must upload Shapefiles as a compressed (.zip) file. |
| GeoTIFF | 10 GB |

### Raster and vector tilesets[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/creating-data/#raster-and-vector-tilesets "Raster and vector tilesets への直接リンク")

Tilesets can be created with raster or vector data.

If you upload raster data (such as a GeoTIFF), the resulting raster tilesets will only contain raster data, a [pixel-based](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Raster_graphics)
 data format that all digital photographs conform to.

If you upload vector data, this will create a vector tileset containing vector data, which consists of mathematically calculated points, lines, and polygons as coordinate pairs. Mapbox map renderers take advantage of vector tilesets by calculating and rendering vector data directly in your browser.

このpageは役に立ちましたか？はいいいえ

---

# Optimize map label placement | Help | Mapbox

[メインコンテンツまでスキップ](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/optimize-map-label-placement/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

Map labels are applied to Mapbox GL maps as features in [symbol layers](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/style-spec/reference/layers/#symbol)
. Symbol layers are the most complex layer type in the Mapbox Style Specification. The symbol layer type offers detailed typographic styling options for your labels and map data.

This guide will walk through a subset of [layout properties](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/layout-paint-property/)
 that are relevant to _label placement_. You can see the complete list of layout and paint properties for symbol layers, including those responsible for label language, style, and more, in the [Mapbox Style Specification](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/style-spec/reference/layers/#symbol)
.

Label collision[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/optimize-map-label-placement/#label-collision "Label collision への直接リンク")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Collision detection** is the process of preventing labels on a map from overlapping each other. Styles use four properties to prevent label collision:

*   By default [`icon-allow-overlap`](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/style-spec/reference/layers/#layout-symbol-icon-allow-overlap)
     and [`text-allow-overlap`](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/style-spec/reference/layers/#layout-symbol-text-allow-overlap)
     are set to `false`. If set to `true`, the icon or text will be visible even if it collides with other symbols drawn before.
*   By default [`icon-ignore-placement`](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/style-spec/reference/layers/#layout-symbol-icon-ignore-placement)
     and [`text-ignore-placement`](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/style-spec/reference/layers/#layout-symbol-text-ignore-placement)
     are set to `false`. If set to `true`, other symbols can be visible even if they collide with the text or icon.

For reliable collision detection across layers that come from different data sources, use source compositing. Source compositing happens by default for new styles created in Mapbox Studio. If you are adding sources on the client, they will not be composited.

While detecting and preventing label collision helps improve map legibility, it can result in many hidden labels. Use collision detection and variable label placement to keep labels legible while increasing the density of labels displayed on the map.

Label density[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/optimize-map-label-placement/#label-density "Label density への直接リンク")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Variable label placement** is a map design concept in which more than one placement option is attempted for each label. This maximizes the chances that the map will show each label. Variable label placement is powered by three layout properties in the Mapbox Style Specification: `text-variable-anchor`, `text-radial-offset`, and `text-justify`.

The diagram below illustrates each possible position relative to the data point:

anchor: `bottom-right`

justify: `right`

circle

anchor: `bottom`

justify: `center`

circle

anchor: `bottom-left`

justify: `left`

circle

anchor: `right`

justify: `right`

circle

anchor: `center`

justify: `center`

circle

anchor: `left`

justify: `left`

circle

anchor: `top-right`

justify: `right`

circle

anchor: `top`

justify: `center`

circle

anchor: `top-left`

justify: `left`

### `text-variable-anchor`[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/optimize-map-label-placement/#text-variable-anchor "text-variable-anchor への直接リンク")

[**`text-variable-anchor`**](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/style-spec/reference/layers/#layout-symbol-text-variable-anchor)
 accepts an array of text-anchor positions as the input value. The map renderer tries to place the label at the positions in this list in the provided order of the array at collision detection time.

For example, in a layer with `text-variable-anchor` set to `["top", "bottom", "left"]`, the map renderer would first try to place each label anchored at the top. Then, if there wasn't room for the label at that position, it would try to place the label anchored at the bottom. And finally, if the label could not fit on the map anchored at the top or bottom, it would try anchoring on the left side. If none of these anchor positions can be used, the map will not display the label.

If a user changes the map zoom after a label has been placed, the map renderer will try to place the label in its original position _first_ before moving on to the rest of the positions specified in the `text-variable-anchor` array. This exception makes map rendering more efficient, but can sometimes be surprising.

The order in which each label in a layer will go through this process is described in detail below in [Label hierarchy](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/optimize-map-label-placement/#label-hierarchy)
.

### `text-radial-offset`[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/optimize-map-label-placement/#text-radial-offset "text-radial-offset への直接リンク")

[**`text-radial-offset`**](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/style-spec/reference/layers/#layout-symbol-text-radial-offset)
 accepts a scalar value for the offset distance of text from its anchor and is applied to the `text-variable-anchor`. Positive values specify right and down, while negative values specify left and up.

### `text-justify`[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/optimize-map-label-placement/#text-justify "text-justify への直接リンク")

[**`text-justify`**](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/style-spec/reference/layers/#layout-symbol-text-justify)
 specifies the justification of the label. You can set it to one of `auto`, `left`, `center`, or `right`. Specifying `auto` will automatically set the justification towards the anchor position.

### Platform-specific examples[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/optimize-map-label-placement/#platform-specific-examples "Platform-specific examples への直接リンク")

The exact syntax used to implement variable label placement varies by platform. View examples for web and mobile using variable label placement to improve label density:

*   [Mapbox GL JS example](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/ja/example/variable-label-placement/)
    
*   [Maps SDK for Android example](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/ja/maps/examples/variable-label-placement/)
    

Label hierarchy[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/optimize-map-label-placement/#label-hierarchy "Label hierarchy への直接リンク")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Some labels on your map may be more relevant to a wider audience or more important to a particular audience. When two labels collide, the draw order of features determines which label to display and which to hide. There are several factors that contribute to draw order.

### Order of layers[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/optimize-map-label-placement/#order-of-layers "Order of layers への直接リンク")

Map styles contain a collection of style [layers](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/layer/)
. Layers are specified in your style JSON and are drawn in order. This means that the first layer defined in the style JSON will be drawn first and will appear below all layers that follow. The last layer defined in the style JSON will be drawn last, on top of all other layers.

You can increase the chances that a layer's labels will be visible by placing that layer further down in your style JSON.

### Order of features inside layers[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/optimize-map-label-placement/#order-of-features-inside-layers "Order of features inside layers への直接リンク")

For each layer in your style, you must specify a [source](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/source/)
. For vector sources, you must also specify a [source layer](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/source-layer/)
 for each style layer. The draw order of features inside a single vector tileset _source layer_ can be specified in two ways:

*   **At the time of tileset creation**: If you are using Mapbox Tiling Service (MTS), you can specify the order of features inside a single source layer using the `order` option. See the [MTS recipe reference](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-tiling-service/ja/reference/)
    .
*   **At runtime with `symbol-sort-key`**: If you don't have control over the data at the time of tileset creation, you can influence the draw order of features at runtime using [`symbol-sort-key`](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/style-spec/reference/layers/#layout-symbol-symbol-sort-key)
    .

For GeoJSON sources, the draw order of features is determined by the order of features in the feature collection. Features are draw in descending order.

このpageは役に立ちましたか？はいいいえ

---

# Satellite imagery | Help | Mapbox

[メインコンテンツまでスキップ](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/imagery/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

[Mapbox Satellite](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/data/tilesets/reference/mapbox-satellite/)
 is a global basemap of high resolution satellite and aerial imagery. The [Mapbox Satellite Streets](https://www.mapbox.com/maps/satellite)
 style combines the Mapbox Satellite basemap with vector data from [Mapbox Streets](https://www.mapbox.com/maps/streets/)
 to bring contextual information to your map.

This guide will introduce Mapbox imagery resources and explain how you can upload your own imagery to use with Mapbox tools.

To learn more about Mapbox imagery, including data sources and data coverage, read our [Mapbox imagery](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/data/tilesets/guides/imagery/)
 guide.

Key terms[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/imagery/#key-terms "Key terms への直接リンク")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

It can be helpful to understand key terms related to imagery.

For more detailed definitions, click the terms below or visit the [Glossary](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/)
.

*   **aerial imagery**: visual raster data acquired by an instrument on a plane, drone, or other aerial source
*   **satellite imagery**: visual raster data acquired by a spaceborne instrument
*   [**raster**](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/raster/)
    : a pixel-based data format that stores data in a grid structure
*   [**tileset**](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/tileset/)
    : a collection of raster or vector data broken up into a grid

Clockwise from top left: MODIS, Landsat, Maxar satellite, Vexcel aerial.

Use Mapbox imagery[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/imagery/#use-mapbox-imagery "Use Mapbox imagery への直接リンク")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

You can use Mapbox imagery data in the following Mapbox-owned [styles](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/style/)
 or [tilesets](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/tileset/)
, available to anyone using a valid Mapbox [access token](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/access-token/)
.

### Imagery styles[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/imagery/#imagery-styles "Imagery styles への直接リンク")

Click a style name below to learn more about the style in our Styles API documentation:

*   [Mapbox Satellite](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/maps/styles/#mapbox-styles)
    
*   [Mapbox Satellite Streets](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/maps/styles/#mapbox-styles)
    

Learn more about using styles in our [Map Design](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/map-design/#how-to-use-a-style)
 getting started guide.

### Imagery tilesets[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/imagery/#imagery-tilesets "Imagery tilesets への直接リンク")

Click a tileset name to learn about its layers, data sources, and more:

*   [Mapbox Satellite](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/data/tilesets/reference/mapbox-satellite/)
    
*   [Mapbox NAIP](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/data/tilesets/reference/mapbox-naip/)
    

Learn more about using tilesets in our [Mapbox imagery](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/data/tilesets/guides/imagery/#use-mapbox-imagery)
 tileset guide.

Upload your own imagery[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/imagery/#upload-your-own-imagery "Upload your own imagery への直接リンク")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

If you want to use your own imagery with Mapbox, see our documentation for our [Mapbox Tiling Service](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-tiling-service/ja/guides/)
, [Mapbox Studio](https://docs.mapbox.com/studio-manual/ja/)
, and [Uploads API](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/maps/uploads/)
. Make sure you convert your imagery into [GeoTIFF](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/tiff/)
 format before you upload.

Once you have uploaded your data, it will be automatically converted into a custom raster tileset which you can then add to your Mapbox project.

To learn more what you can do with tilesets, see these guides:

*   To add a tileset to a web map, see our [Add a video](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/ja/example/video-on-a-map/)
     example.

Imagery use cases[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/imagery/#imagery-use-cases "Imagery use cases への直接リンク")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

When looking to understand our thousands of customers and millions of users, we focus on the broader application of imagery and how to categorize the use case, be it business, scientific, humanitarian, and government or something else. Our users break down into categories by context:

*   A wide-view user — Customers who visualize global/country datasets
*   An outdoor user — Rural (recreation, non-precision agriculture), non-urban business intelligence, visualization/simulation
*   A basemap user — Most app developers, real estate, business intelligence, fleet and logistics management, navigation

Sampling of typical industry vertical markets and their respective imagery needs

Samnakdong, South Korea © Maxar 2020.

### Drilling down on customer usage[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/imagery/#drilling-down-on-customer-usage "Drilling down on customer usage への直接リンク")

Over the past few years, we have been improving our approach to update our imagery basemap based on using [anonymized telemetry](https://blog.mapbox.com/updating-8-2-million-km%C2%B2-of-high-resolution-satellite-imagery-b68070bdf4b2)
 to identify where people are looking at our maps, and systematically assessing [imagery quality at scale](https://blog.mapbox.com/assessing-satellite-imagery-at-scale-5a55cd505a8b)
.

We combine this with an assessment of aggregated data from our largest satellite users to get a deeper understanding. Looking at the data in the chart below, we see the vast majority of views across our top 100 customers come from zoom levels 17+, or imagery greater than 1-meter resolution (peaks on the right of the charts).

Distribution of zoom level requests for the top 100 mapbox.satellite users.

This approach lets us segment our customers based on their usage patterns. With this information, we can make data-driven decisions on our imagery allocation and forecast which updates will help which customers will find use from an update.

Bay Bridge Toll Plaza, Oakland, CA © Vexcel Data 2022

As a result of these analyses, we are focusing the majority of our attention on higher resolution data sets. We now have high-resolution aerial imagery in the contiguous US, covering about 80% of the country’s population. As we move forward, we are continually working with our imagery partners to keep the highest demand areas up-to-date with high resolution, high-quality imagery.

Data updates[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/imagery/#data-updates "Data updates への直接リンク")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

We update the 4 petabyte (PB — or 4 M gigabytes) global [imagery layer](https://www.mapbox.com/maps/satellite/)
 used for our Mapbox Satellite data from nearly a dozen sources.

Any single image offers a view of a place at a moment in time that can be used to find new development sites, aid in disaster response, serve as a source of ground truth, and track changes.

### How do we determine where to update?[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/imagery/#how-do-we-determine-where-to-update "How do we determine where to update? への直接リンク")

To determine where to update imagery, we take into account industry use cases and needs, customer feedback, and internal data on tile requests and zoom levels.

We optimize our basemap for a balance of recency and aesthetic quality depending on data availability as well as analyzing customer usage to prioritize global imagery updates.

### Where do we source our data?[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/imagery/#where-do-we-source-our-data "Where do we source our data? への直接リンク")

Our satellite data consists of imagery sourced from multiple satellites and airplanes, from both commercial and open sources. View our list of sources on our [data sources page](https://www.mapbox.com/about/maps#data-sources)
.

### What is the breakdown of imagery by zoom level?[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/imagery/#what-is-the-breakdown-of-imagery-by-zoom-level "What is the breakdown of imagery by zoom level? への直接リンク")

Imagery sources and resolutions are tied to specific zoom levels to provide consistency and quality of data.

These zoom levels break down as follows:

*   Zoom levels 0-8 use [de-clouded data](https://blog.mapbox.com/making-clouds-go-away-on-mapbox-satellite-82a3450af515)
     from NASA MODIS satellites.
*   Zoom levels 9-12 use primarily [Maxar satellite imagery](https://www.mapbox.com/blog/30-meter-imagery-update-improved-recency-color-and-resolution-for-seamless-3d-terrain)
     and [NASA/USGS Landsat 5 through 9 imagery](https://blog.mapbox.com/open-aerial-the-data-behind-mapbox-satellite-ed890628f2e5)
     in limited locations.
*   Zoom levels 13-16 use [Maxar's Vivid product](https://www.mapbox.com/blog/3d-satellite-maps-high-res-imagery)
     for the majority of global coverage.
*   Zoom levels 16+ use [Vexcel aerial imagery](https://www.mapbox.com/blog/updated-aerial-imagery-for-the-u-s-canada-and-europe)
     over North American - and some European - cities, and open aerial imagery from the Netherlands, Denmark, France, Germany, Chechia, Poland, Spain, New Zealand, and other regions. image

Clockwise from top left: MODIS, Landsat, Maxar satellite, Vexcel aerial.

### How do we quality control our data?[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/imagery/#how-do-we-quality-control-our-data "How do we quality control our data? への直接リンク")

All our imagery is checked for color fidelity, optimized, and composited together into a single raster tileset by our Satellite team. The end result is an imagery layer that provides varying levels of detail based on the needs of users looking at larger, less detailed areas or smaller, more detailed areas.

Port of Long Beach, Los Angeles, CA © Vexcel Data, 2022

このpageは役に立ちましたか？はいいいえ

# Data coverage and quality | Help | Mapbox

[メインコンテンツまでスキップ](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/mapbox-data/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

**Mapbox data** is the foundation for all our maps, search, and navigation services. We update data continuously to serve customers and use cases across the globe, ingesting updates from open and proprietary sources.

This guide describes Mapbox data that developers can use, including information on data coverage and quality.

To contribute data, see our [Improve this map](https://www.mapbox.com/contribute)
 utility.

Key terms[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/mapbox-data/#key-terms "Key terms への直接リンク")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

It can be helpful to understand key terms related to map data.

For more detailed definitions, click the terms below or visit the [Glossary](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/)
.

*   [**raster**](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/raster/)
    : a pixel-based data format that stores data in a grid structure
*   [**vector tile**](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/vector-tiles/)
    : a lightweight data format for storing vector data
*   [**tileset**](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/tileset/)
    : a collection of raster or vector data broken up into a grid

Map data[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/mapbox-data/#map-data "Map data への直接リンク")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[Mapbox tilesets](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/data/tilesets/)
 offer developers access to a wide variety of map data, including road networks, place names, points of interest, satellite and aerial imagery, elevation data, live traffic data, expertly curated national borders, transit data, and more. The data in these tilesets is suitable for a wide variety of applications, both visual and analytical.

Click a tileset to learn about its layers, data sources, data updates, and more:

### Vector tilesets[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/mapbox-data/#vector-tilesets "Vector tilesets への直接リンク")

Most of the data that appears on Mapbox maps is served by vector tilesets:

*   [Mapbox Streets](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/data/tilesets/reference/mapbox-streets-v8/)
    
*   [Mapbox Terrain](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/data/tilesets/reference/mapbox-terrain-v2/)
    
*   [Mapbox Traffic](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/data/tilesets/reference/mapbox-traffic-v1/)
    
*   [Mapbox Countries](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/data/tilesets/reference/mapbox-countries-v1/)
    
*   [Mapbox Transit](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/data/tilesets/reference/mapbox-transit-v2/)
    
*   [Mapbox Indoor](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/data/tilesets/reference/mapbox-indoor-v1/)
    

### Raster tilesets[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/mapbox-data/#raster-tilesets "Raster tilesets への直接リンク")

Raster tilesets contain aerial and satellite imagery and terrain and elevation data:

*   [Mapbox Satellite](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/data/tilesets/reference/mapbox-satellite/)
    
*   [Mapbox NAIP](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/data/tilesets/reference/mapbox-naip/)
    
*   [Mapbox Terrain-DEM](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/data/tilesets/reference/mapbox-terrain-dem-v1/)
    
*   [Mapbox Terrain-RGB](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/data/tilesets/reference/mapbox-terrain-rgb-v1/)
    

### Premium tilesets[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/mapbox-data/#premium-tilesets "Premium tilesets への直接リンク")

*   [Mapbox Boundaries](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/data/boundaries/guides/)
    

### Custom tilesets[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/mapbox-data/#custom-tilesets "Custom tilesets への直接リンク")

You can learn how to create your own custom tilesets in our [Add new data](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/creating-data/)
 getting started guide.

Data coverage and quality[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/mapbox-data/#data-coverage-and-quality "Data coverage and quality への直接リンク")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Mapbox continuously improves data coverage and quality for all our services.

The following table shows data coverage by country for our [Geocoding](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/search/geocoding/)
 and [Search APIs](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/search/search/)
. Some data are available at a city level and do not appear in this list. Data coverage can change due to various reasons, including construction activities for roads, changes in government regulations, and changes in the licensing agreements with data providers.

See the [Geocoding API documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/search/geocoding/#data-types)
 for definitions of data types.

### Legend[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/mapbox-data/#legend "Legend への直接リンク")

| --- | --- |
| ●   | Service is available within this geography with premium data quality. |
| ○   | Service is available within this geography with basic data quality. |
| —   | Service is not available, or has low data quality. |

**Try out our [Geocoding API](https://docs.mapbox.com/playground/ja/geocoding/)
 to understand the most up-to-date coverage for specific countries.**

Map projections[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/mapbox-data/#map-projections "Map projections への直接リンク")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

By default, Mapbox renders maps and map tiles with the popular [Web Mercator projection](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mercator_projection#Web_Mercator)
 using the [EPSG:3857](https://epsg.io/3857)
 projected coordinate system (sometimes called EPSG:900913). Because Web Mercator is used by most web maps, you can often combine map tiles and layers from other platforms that use the same [projection](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/projection/)
 with your Mapbox maps. You can use [Mapbox GL JS adaptive projections](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/ja/guides/projections/)
 to display map tiles on the web using different projections.

Use our data[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/mapbox-data/#use-our-data "Use our data への直接リンク")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

You can use our map data as source data for your map styles in Mapbox Studio, access our data directly using the Mapbox Maps Service APIs, or contribute to our data sources through OpenStreetMap.

### Mapbox Studio[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/mapbox-data/#mapbox-studio "Mapbox Studio への直接リンク")

You can use our tilesets directly in Mapbox Studio on the [Tilesets page](https://www.mapbox.com/studio/tilesets)
. Whenever you create a new style from Mapbox Standard or Mapbox Standard Satellite in the Mapbox Studio style editor, you are using a Mapbox tileset.

### Maps APIs[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/mapbox-data/#maps-apis "Maps APIs への直接リンク")

With our [Maps APIs](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/maps/)
, you can request Mapbox tilesets programmatically:

*   Retrieve vector tiles with the [Vector Tiles API](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/maps/vector-tiles/)
    .
*   Retrieve features from vector tiles with the [Tilequery API](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/maps/#retrieve-features-from-vector-tiles)
    .
*   Retrieve raster tiles with the [Raster Tiles API](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/maps/raster-tiles/)
    .
*   Retrieve TileJSON metadata for vector and raster tilesets with [Mapbox Tiling Service (MTS)](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/maps/mapbox-tiling-service/#retrieve-tilejson-metadata)
    .
*   Access elevation data using the instructions in our [Access Elevation Data](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/data/tilesets/guides/access-elevation-data/)
     guide.

### Contribute to OpenStreetMap[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/mapbox-data/#contribute-to-openstreetmap "Contribute to OpenStreetMap への直接リンク")

We contribute to OpenStreetMap, and you should too!

[Sign up for a free account](https://www.openstreetmap.org/user/new)
 to start contributing. The OpenStreetMap web editor allows you to draw or edit features such as roads, buildings, parks, traffic signals, and labels. Read [Learn OpenStreetMap](http://learnosm.org/)
 for a step-by-step guide to start contributing to the project.

We've covered how Mapbox works with OpenStreetMap data, the quality controls behind it, and how you can be a part of this growing movement to provide a free and open data set for everyone.

Next steps[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/mapbox-data/#next-steps "Next steps への直接リンク")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Now that you know that you can use Mapbox data and create your own vector or raster tilesets, you are ready to begin building with map data.

These tutorials can help you start building:

*   [Tutorial: Add data to a style](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/tutorials/add-data-to-mapbox-style/)
     using custom data.
*   [Tutorial: Customize label text for a single label](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/tutorials/customize-label-text/)
     using _Mapbox Streets_ data.
*   [Tutorial: Add 3D buildings to a Mapbox Studio style](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/tutorials/z_add-3d-buildings-studio/)
     using _Mapbox Streets_ data.

このpageは役に立ちましたか？はいいいえ

---

# Map design and styles | Help | Mapbox

[メインコンテンツまでスキップ](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/map-design/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

You can use Mapbox tools to control many aspects of the map design process, including uploading custom data, adjusting your map's styling, adding fonts, creating data-driven visualizations, and more.

In this guide, you'll learn about the basic principles of making a **map style** with Mapbox tools as well as more advanced concepts for in-depth customization.

The map below showcases the **Mapbox Standard** style, which you can use as a base map in your applications. You can learn more about the features of this style in the [Mapbox Standard section](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/map-design/#mapbox-standard)
 of this guide.

What's the difference between a map and a style?

In the Mapbox ecosystem, a **map** is an interactive element that you can add to a webpage or mobile app to display styled geospatial data. A **style** is a configuration document that defines the visual appearance of a map.

When showing a map, a style is required. Mapbox maps will default to using the Mapbox Standard Style if you do not specify a style. Styles can be modified after the map loads, for example you can add and remove data layers from a style at runtime to update the map.

How do I make a map style?[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/map-design/#how-do-i-make-a-map "How do I make a map style? への直接リンク")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

There are multiple ways to craft a map style that makes your data shine: You can make a custom style by adding your data on top of the [Mapbox Standard style](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/map-design/#mapbox-standard)
, or build a [custom style](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/map-design/#how-to-create-a-custom-style)
 completely from scratch.

Mapbox Standard[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/map-design/#mapbox-standard "Mapbox Standard への直接リンク")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[Mapbox Standard](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/map-styles/standard/)
 is our latest 3D mapping experience featuring dynamic lighting capabilities, landmark 3D buildings, and more. Beyond introducing a high-quality visual experience, Standard also reduces the technical burden and cartographic ability needed to maintain a map and allows Mapbox to deliver the latest data and design updates to your base map style without a manual upgrade that risks breaking changes.

### Key features[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/map-design/#key-features "Key features への直接リンク")

3D lighting presets

Carefully designed lighting & shadow presets (Day, Night, Dusk and Dawn) and ability to pass custom lighting settings.

3D landmarks and buildings

High-fidelity 3D landmarks with styled windows, walls & roofs and entrance lights as well as extensive global coverage for non-landmark buildings.

3D vegetation and terrain

Detailed 3D vegetation modeled to reflect different geographic biospheres across the globe accurately and 3D terrain.

Automatic updates

A versionless style identifier to bring automatic updates, such as continuous styling, feature and data updates to the style without requiring manual updates.

Simplified configuration

Simplified interface for key styling configurations in runtime and Studio.

Core 2D data features

Detailed iconography, place labels, admin boundaries, highly organized road network data, and more.

Classic styles[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/map-design/#classic-style "Classic styles への直接リンク")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

You can build a **custom map style** from scratch using the [Mapbox Studio style editor](https://docs.mapbox.com/studio-manual/ja/guides/map-styling/#the-style-editor)
. You can use Mapbox's streets-v8 tileset as a data source, or you can upload your own data to Mapbox and use it in your style.

Working with custom styles

Continue to the section [What is a style](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/map-design/#how-to-create-a-custom-style)
 to learn about the concepts required to work with custom styles.

Methods for customizing a map style[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/map-design/#adding-your-data-to-the-map "Methods for customizing a map style への直接リンク")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Whether you use Mapbox Standard or a custom style, there are two main approaches to customizing the look of the map style:

*   **Update map features dynamically at runtime using the Mapbox GL JS or the Mobile Maps SDKs**. Lighten or darken the map based on the time of day, personalize icons and map colors based on your users’ activity, switch languages dynamically, or increase the size of labels based on user preferences to improve legibility.
*   **Create a map style with Mapbox Studio**. Upload data, build styles from scratch, and style groups of layers together using style components. Publish your custom style and load it into your application. If you update the underlying data or you edit and publish updates to the style in Mapbox Studio, those changes will be reflected in your application.

These approaches are not mutually exclusive. You can load a custom style and update map features from that custom style in your application at runtime.

If you prefer working in a visual interface, or if you want to add custom tilesets to the style, consider using the [Mapbox Studio style editor](https://docs.mapbox.com/studio-manual/ja/guides/map-styling/#the-style-editor)
 to maintain your style. If you intend to make small visual changes and if you need to add your data dynamically at runtime, consider changing features dynamically at runtime.

What is a style?[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/map-design/#what-is-a-style "What is a style? への直接リンク")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

A Mapbox **style** is a JSON object that defines exactly how a map should be drawn. It defines almost everything related to a map's appearance.

These four custom map styles feature line traces, weather data, custom fonts, and 3D terrain. Learn more about popular use cases for custom map styles on our [maps page](https://www.mapbox.com/maps/)
.

A style document must contain the following:

*   _References to [sources](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/style-spec/reference/sources/)
    _ containing geospatial data. For example, a style might use a URL for a GeoJSON source or a tileset ID for a tileset source. Except for raster tiles or georeferenced images and videos, sources are pure data with no inherent visual appearance.
    
*   _[Style layers](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/style-spec/reference/layers/)
    _ give sources a visual appearance by specifying which `layout` and `paint` properties Mapbox GL should apply. These properties include colors, fonts, line widths, layer order, and more.
    

It may also include [_glyphs_](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/style-spec/reference/glyphs/)
 and [_sprite_](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/style-spec/reference/sprite/)
 properties. These properties work automatically when you create a style with the Mapbox Studio style editor. In most cases you do not need to change them. The `glyphs` property tells the Mapbox map where to fetch fonts. The `sprite` property tells the Mapbox Map where to look for icons.

Every map depends on a style, so when you change a map style, any map that uses that style will be affected by your changes upon its next load.

Continue reading to see an [example of a style document](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/map-design/#example-of-a-style-and-map)
 and learn [how styles work](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/map-design/#how-styles-work)
.

### What is a map?[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/map-design/#what-is-a-map "What is a map? への直接リンク")

A Mapbox **map** is an interactive element you can add to a webpage or mobile app to display styled map data. It comes with many powerful functions and configurable options that you can use to use to make each map instance look and behave exactly as you want. A Mapbox map's style is one of its many configurable options, but is the one that will have the greatest impact on how your users perceive your map.

Besides having a visual appearance, every Mapbox map also has powerful built-in functions that you can use to add interactive features to your maps or create data-driven visualizations.

For example, you can use a map's functionality to change its style from `'Light'` to `'Dark'` after sunset in a user's location; you can color election precinct polygons according to vote totals from a live data API; or you can enable users to show or hide map layers to visualize air quality data or weather information.

You can learn more about building interactive maps in our [web applications](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/web-apps/)
 and [mobile applications](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/mobile-apps/)
 getting started guides.

### Example of a style and map[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/map-design/#example-of-a-style-and-map "Example of a style and map への直接リンク")

Below is a complete style JSON for a custom map style, and a map rendered using that style:

Inside the style JSON, you can see many [layer](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/style-spec/reference/layers/)
 definitions, including a `background` layer with a beige color property, a `fill` layer for national parks, and a `line` layer for waterways.

On the map, you can see the visual rendering of these layers. You can also interact with the map using pan, zoom, and tilt actions that are handled by the map object, not the style.

How styles work[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/map-design/#how-styles-work "How styles work への直接リンク")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

A Mapbox style must conform to the [Mapbox Style Specification](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/style-spec/)
, an open source standard that enables consistent rendering across many different clients, like web browsers, mobile or embedded devices, or third party libraries or applications designed to read Mapbox styles.

Tools like [Mapbox GL JS](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/ja/)
 and the Mapbox Maps SDKs for [Android](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/ja/maps/guides/)
 and [iOS](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/ja/maps/guides/)
 use Mapbox GL to combine style rules and data sources to render the resulting map completely on the computer or mobile device that has requested it.

Because styles are designed to work with GL-based renderers, you can change your style programmatically after the map has been loaded. This ability, known as [runtime styling](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/runtime-styling/)
, gives you the ability to build highly controlled map interfaces, visualizations, and experiences using Mapbox.

You can learn more about Mapbox technology in our [Introduction to Mapbox](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/)
 getting started guide.

How to create a custom style[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/map-design/#how-to-create-a-custom-style "How to create a custom style への直接リンク")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Anyone can create a custom Mapbox style. This section describes two approaches for this task: using Mapbox Studio, or writing a style manually.

circlecirclecircle

arrow-leftarrow-rightrotate

heartmapboxmenu

You can use the Mapbox Studio style editor to create and edit custom styles. Learn more about this web-based application in the [Mapbox Studio manual](https://docs.mapbox.com/studio-manual/ja/reference/styles/)
.

### Use Mapbox Studio[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/map-design/#use-mapbox-studio "Use Mapbox Studio への直接リンク")

You can use the **Mapbox Studio style editor**, a visual map style editor that works in your web browser, to create and publish your own custom styles. When you use this method, your style will automatically adhere to the required Mapbox Style Specification, and is automatically hosted on Mapbox servers.

With the style editor, you can create and edit **style layers** to control every aspect of a feature's appearance. Most Mapbox styles include **style components**, which allow you to edit groups of related layers like 'Roads' or 'Places' simultaneously.

To add custom data to you style, use Mapbox Studio or the [Mapbox Tiling Service](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-tiling-service/ja/guides/)
 to upload data to your account.

[TUTORIAL\
\
Create a custom style in Mapbox Studio\
\
Follow this tutorial to learn how to create a custom style in Mapbox Studio.](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/tutorials/create-a-custom-style/)

To learn more about publishing your styles, including information on using `draft` or `production` modes, refer to the [Mapbox Studio manual](https://docs.mapbox.com/studio-manual/ja/overview/publish-your-style/)
.

### Write a style manually[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/map-design/#write-a-style-manually "Write a style manually への直接リンク")

You can also write a style document manually and then upload it to your Mapbox account using the [Mapbox Styles API](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/maps/styles/#create-a-style)
. They Styles API also lets you read and change map styles, fonts, and icons. This API is the basis for Mapbox Studio. If you use Mapbox Studio, Mapbox GL JS, or the Mapbox Mobile SDKs, you're already using the Styles API.

If you write your own style document, you can make sure that it conforms to the Mapbox Style Specification using the [Mapbox style utilities npm package](https://github.com/mapbox/mapbox-gl-js/tree/main/src/style-spec#gl-style-validate)

How to use a style[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/map-design/#how-to-use-a-style "How to use a style への直接リンク")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This section will explain how Mapbox style IDs and style URLs work, and show how you can use them to reference Mapbox styles in a web application, mobile application, or third-party library or application.

### Style URLs[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/map-design/#style-urls "Style URLs への直接リンク")

A style URL is a shorthand notation for a [Mapbox Styles API](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/maps/styles/#retrieve-a-style)
 request.

Mapbox style URLs follow this format:

    mapbox://styles/{owner}/{style_id}

The URL contains three pieces of information, separated by forward slashes (`/`):

*   `mapbox://styles` is an alias for the [Mapbox Styles API endpoint](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/maps/styles/)
    .
*   `owner` is the username for the Mapbox account that owns the style. To use [Mapbox-owned styles](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/maps/styles/#mapbox-styles)
    , the `owner` value should be `mapbox`.
*   `style_id` is the unique style identifier (`id`) for the style.

The Mapbox GL client libraries use the style URL you supply to request the right style document from the Mapbox Styles API.

For example, this style URL...

    mapbox://styles/mapbox/streets-v12

...makes an API request like this:

    https://api.mapbox.com/styl@site/src/mapbox/streets-v12?access_token=YOUR_MAPBOX_ACCESS_TOKEN

If you want, you can copy the API request above and paste it into your browser to test the validity of the request. Remember to replace any instances of `YOUR_MAPBOX_ACCESS_TOKEN` with your own Mapbox [access token](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/access-token/)
.

How to find a style URL in Mapbox Studio

Need to find the style URL for one of your own custom styles?1. Log into your [Mapbox account](https://console.mapbox.com/)
.2. Visit your [Styles page](https://console.mapbox.com/studio/)
 in Mapbox Studio.3. Click on the ellipses menu (options) next to the style to see its style URL.

#### Using style URLs[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/map-design/#using-style-urls "Using style URLs への直接リンク")

Now that you know what a style URL is, you are ready to learn how to use one.

If you are adding a Mapbox map to a web or mobile application, you will most often use a style by copying its style URL and pasting it into the map initialization block in your app.

It's important to note that there are two kinds of styles:

*   **Mapbox-owned styles** are professionally designed styles that are freely available for developers to use. You can learn more about these styles in our [Mapbox Styles API documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/maps/styles/#mapbox-styles)
    .
    
*   **Custom styles** are styles that you create yourself, or that are shared by other Mapbox users. You can use a custom style if it is published, and you either own the style or have the correct permissions to access it.
    

The table below offers code examples showing how to use both kinds of styles in web and mobile applications. Click a link to view an example:

| Platform | Mapbox-owned style | Custom style |
| --- | --- | --- |
| globe Web | [Display a map on a webpage](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/example/simple-map/) | [Display a map with a custom style](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/example/custom-style-id/) |
| android Android | [Create a simple map view](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/maps/examples/create-a-simple-map-view/) | [Mapbox Studio style](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/maps/guides/styles/set-a-style/#custom-styles) |
| ios iOS | [Create a simple map view](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/maps/examples/simple-map-view/) | [Apply a style designed in Mapbox Studio](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/maps/examples/custom-style/) |

### Other sharing options[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/map-design/#other-sharing-options "Other sharing options への直接リンク")

Both Mapbox-owned styles and custom styles designed in Mapbox Studio come with a built-in WMTS endpoint that you can use to add your styles to desktop GIS applications like ArcGIS Online, Tableau, CARTO, and Fulcrum.

You can learn about this WMTS endpoint and other sharing options like Leaflet in the [Mapbox Studio manual](https://docs.mapbox.com/studio-manual/ja/guides/publish-your-style/#share-button)
.

Data-driven styles[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/map-design/#data-driven-styles "Data-driven styles への直接リンク")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[**Data-driven styles**](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/data-driven-styling/)
 allow you to change a layer's style based on properties in the layer's data source. For example, you might create a data-driven style rule that sets the color of states in the United States based on the population of each state.

Here are some examples of data-driven styles using different layer types:

[Tutorial: Get started with Mapbox GL JS expressions\
\
Use the age of historical landmarks to set circle sizes on a map.](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/tutorials/mapbox-gl-js-expressions/)

[Tutorial: Build a choropleth map\
\
Use the population data from each US state to set their respective colors.](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/tutorials/choropleth-studio-gl-pt-1/)

[Blog post: Colored line map\
\
Use the difference between the local time at the flight's origin and destination to set the color of a flight path.](https://blog.mapbox.com/track-your-jetlag-with-data-driven-line-color-c9183c5781aa)

[Example: 3D Building map\
\
Use the 'height' and 'min\_height' data from building polygons to extrude the building shapes in 3D.](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/ja/example/3d-buildings/)

### How to use expressions[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/map-design/#how-to-use-expressions "How to use expressions への直接リンク")

You can use [expressions](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/style-spec/reference/expressions/)
 to define the value of any layout property, paint property, or filter within a style. With expressions, you can style data with multiple feature properties at once, apply conditional logic, and manipulate data with arithmetic and string operations for a more sophisticated relationship between your data and how it is styled.

For example, the code snippet below uses two expressions to set the value of the layout property [`icon-image`](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/style-spec/reference/layers/#layout-symbol-icon-image)
.

1.  The [`get`](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/style-spec/reference/expressions/#get)
     expression retrieves the value of the `'marker-number'` property from the data.
2.  The [`match`](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/style-spec/reference/expressions/#match)
     expression displays a `'pin-red'` icon image for features matching the `'marker-number'` value of `'one'`, a `'pin-blue'` icon image for `'two'`, a `'pin-green'` icon image for `'three'`, and a `'pin-white'` icon image for any other values.

    "layout": {  "icon-image": [      "match", ["string", ["get", "marker-number"]],        "one",        "pin-red",        "two",        "pin-blue",        "three",        "pin-green",        "pin-white"      ],  "icon-size": 1}

You can read the [expressions documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/style-spec/reference/expressions/)
 in the Mapbox Style Specification to learn about the different kinds of expression operators you can use, like _lookup_, _decision_, or _math_ operators. You can also learn to use expressions by following step-by-step instructions in these tutorials:

*   [Get started with Mapbox GL JS expressions](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/tutorials/mapbox-gl-js-expressions/)
    
*   [Make a heatmap with Mapbox GL JS](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/tutorials/make-a-heatmap-with-mapbox-gl-js/)
    

Attribution and Terms of Service[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/map-design/#attribution-and-terms-of-service "Attribution and Terms of Service への直接リンク")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Whether you're creating a custom style with Mapbox Studio or building a mobile app with the Android SDK, all Mapbox tools are governed by our [attribution requirements](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/attribution/)
 and our [terms of service](https://www.mapbox.com/tos)
. For more information about either of these requirements, [contact Mapbox Support](https://www.mapbox.com/support)
.

# Transfer styles between accounts | Help | Mapbox

[メインコンテンツまでスキップ](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/transfer-styles-between-accounts/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

There are three approaches to transferring [styles](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/style/)
 between accounts. The first two approaches are true transfers of Mapbox styles from one account to another, but differ depending on whether the style is public or private. The second approach involves referencing a public style from another Mapbox account within your own application.

Approach 1: Copy a public style using a share link[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/transfer-styles-between-accounts/#approach-1-copy-a-public-style-using-a-share-link "Approach 1: Copy a public style using a share link への直接リンク")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

For public styles saved in your account, you can generate a special link that allows other users to copy your style to their account.

If your style is not already public, you can make it public by:

1.  Visiting your [Styles](https://console.mapbox.com/studio/)
     page,
2.  Clicking on the share share icon next to the style you would like to make public, then
3.  Clicking the **Make public** button.
4.  You will be prompted to confirm that you would like to make your style public. If you are sure, click the **Make public** confirmation button.

Once your style is public, you will see a copyable link in that style's share menu. To copy your style to a new account, navigate to this link while logged in to the destination account and click on the **Copy style** button.

How to share a map style using a share link

Learn how to generate a share link to allow other users to copy your style.

Approach 2: Download and re-upload a private style[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/transfer-styles-between-accounts/#approach-2-download-and-re-upload-a-private-style "Approach 2: Download and re-upload a private style への直接リンク")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Transferring private styles between accounts allows you to own a style and make changes to it from a single Mapbox account without making the style public.

You can transfer Mapbox assets between accounts if you have access to the account you're transferring from or if you're given the necessary files.

Styles made with Mapbox Studio can be downloaded in JSON format and re-uploaded as a new style. To download a style, open the style in the Mapbox Studio style editor, click the **Share** button, and click the link next to the **Download** option. You can download draft or production versions of your styles.

You can then upload the JSON file to another Mapbox account:

1.  Unzip the folder you downloaded.
2.  Log into the second account.
3.  Click the **Upload style** link next to the New style button.
4.  Select the `style.json` file inside the unzipped folder and upload.

If you added any custom icons or fonts to your style, you'll need to add them back manually. Open your newly uploaded style in Mapbox Studio, then:

1.  To add fonts, click **Fonts** and then **Upload new font**. Drag and drop the contents of the **fonts** folder from your downloaded style into the appropriate area.

2.  To add [icons](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/icon/)
     (images), click **Images** and then **Upload SVG Image**. Drag and drop the contents of the **icons** folder from your downloaded style into the appropriate area.

How to export (and import) custom map styles between Mapbox accounts

Learn how to export a map style from one Mapbox account and import that map style into another Mapbox account.

Approach 3: Reference a public style[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/transfer-styles-between-accounts/#approach-3-reference-a-public-style "Approach 3: Reference a public style への直接リンク")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

By referencing public styles, you can use Mapbox styles created on other Mapbox accounts in your own application. This approach can be particularly useful if your organization has multiple Mapbox accounts each with their own custom styles, fonts, and sprites.

In this scenario, you have a style from Account A and you’d like Account B to use that style in an application.

By default, Mapbox styles are **Private**. Navigate to your [Styles](https://console.mapbox.com/studio/)
 page and open the menu for the style you want to share. Select **Make public**.

Once you set the style to **Public**, you can reference the style within any application by copying the **Style URL** within the Styles page.

_Special note_: A **Public URL** can be referenced by any application using any access token.

Here’s a quick example of how this might look using our [Mapbox GL JS library](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/ja/example/simple-map/)
:

    mapboxgl.accessToken = '<ACCESS TOKEN FROM ACCOUNT B>';const map = new mapboxgl.Map({  container: 'map', // container id  style: '<PUBLIC STYLE URL FROM ACCOUNT A>', // stylesheet location  center: [-74.5, 40], // starting position [lng, lat]  zoom: 9 // starting zoom});

このpageは役に立ちましたか？はいいいえ

# Use custom Mapbox styles in Tableau | Help | Mapbox

[メインコンテンツまでスキップ](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/use-custom-mapbox-styles-in-tableau/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

The latest versions of [Tableau](https://www.tableau.com/)
 Server, Desktop, Public, and Online all use Mapbox vector maps by default. This guide shows you how to import and use custom Mapbox styles in Tableau.

How to use a Mapbox style in Tableau

Learn how to use any Mapbox style in Tableau.

### Find a Mapbox style's integration URL[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/use-custom-mapbox-styles-in-tableau/#find-a-mapbox-styles-integration-url "Find a Mapbox style's integration URL への直接リンク")

To use a Mapbox style in Tableau, you need that style's _integration URL_.

1.  Go to your [Mapbox Studio styles page](https://console.mapbox.com/studio/)
     and click on the style that you would like to use as your background map in Tableau.
2.  Click on the **Share** button in the options bar at the top of the screen.
3.  Click the **Production** tab.
4.  In the _Develop_ bar, click the **Third party** option.
5.  Choose **Tableau** from the dropdown menu.
6.  Click on the **clipboard** clipboard button to copy the _Integration URL_ to your computer's clipboard. You will use this URL to connect to your Mapbox style from Tableau.

### Change your background map to a custom Mapbox map[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/use-custom-mapbox-styles-in-tableau/#change-your-background-map-to-a-custom-mapbox-map "Change your background map to a custom Mapbox map への直接リンク")

To use a custom Mapbox map as your background map in Tableau:

1.  In Tableau, click on **Map > Background Maps > Map Services**.
2.  In the **Map Services** panel, click the **Add** button.
3.  Select **Mapbox Maps**.
4.  Give the style a name in the _Style Name_ field. You will be able to use this name later to identify the style, so make it descriptive.
5.  In the _Add Mapbox Map_ modal, paste the Mapbox integration URL into the _URL_ field. The _API access token_, _Username_, and _Style ID_ fields will automatically populate.
6.  Click the **OK** button.

The background map will automatically switch the Mapbox map style you imported in the last step.

### Use background maps in Tableau Desktop[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/use-custom-mapbox-styles-in-tableau/#use-background-maps-in-tableau-desktop "Use background maps in Tableau Desktop への直接リンク")

You can access the Mapbox maps you've added to Tableau through the Maps menu:

1.  Click on **Map** in the Tableau menu bar.
2.  Select **Background Maps**.
3.  Click on your desired Mapbox style. The background map will be updated to use the style that you select.

### Show or hide layers in a custom Mapbox map[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/use-custom-mapbox-styles-in-tableau/#show-or-hide-layers-in-a-custom-mapbox-map "Show or hide layers in a custom Mapbox map への直接リンク")

When you set your Tableau background map to use a custom Mapbox map style, you can use the _Map Layers_ panel to either show or hide layers within that Mapbox style. The layers used in a custom Mapbox map style will likely be different than the layers used in the default Tableau map styles, which are described in the Tableau [Customize how your map looks](https://onlinehelp.tableau.com/current/pro/desktop/en-us/maps_options.htm)
 guide.

All your style's layers that are displayed in the Mapbox Studio style editor will be displayed automatically in the Tableau view. To hide a layer, click on the checkbox next to the layer name to deselect it.

* * *

For further guidance on how to customize maps in Tableau, consult the [Tableau documentation](https://onlinehelp.tableau.com/current/pro/desktop/en-us/maps_options.htm)
.

このpageは役に立ちましたか？はいいいえ

# Mapbox Custom Visual for Power BI | Help | Mapbox

[メインコンテンツまでスキップ](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/mapbox-custom-visual-for-power-bi/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

The **Mapbox Custom Visual for Power BI** brings the power of Mapbox's mapping platform directly into Microsoft Power BI, enabling you to create stunning, interactive maps that transform your business data into compelling visual stories.

What is the Mapbox Custom Visual for Power BI?[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/mapbox-custom-visual-for-power-bi/#what-is-the-mapbox-custom-visual-for-power-bi "What is the Mapbox Custom Visual for Power BI? への直接リンク")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The Mapbox Custom Visual is a Power BI integration that allows you to visualize your data on beautiful, customizable maps. Built in partnership with [StarSchema](https://www.starschema.com/)
, this visual combines Power BI's analytics capabilities with Mapbox's advanced mapping features.

### Key Features[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/mapbox-custom-visual-for-power-bi/#key-features "Key Features への直接リンク")

*   **Custom Map Styles**: Use any of Mapbox's professionally designed map styles or create your own custom styles in Mapbox Studio
*   **Data-Driven Styling**: Style map features based on your Power BI data using colors, sizes, and other visual properties
*   **Multiple Layer Support**: Add multiple data layers to create rich, multi-dimensional visualizations
*   **Interactive Controls**: Enable users to interact with your maps through filtering, tooltips, and selection
*   **High Performance**: Render large datasets smoothly with Mapbox's vector tile technology
*   **Real-time Data**: Connect to live data sources for dynamic, up-to-date visualizations

Installation[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/mapbox-custom-visual-for-power-bi/#installation "Installation への直接リンク")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

You can install the Mapbox Custom Visual directly from the Microsoft AppSource marketplace:

1.  Visit the [Mapbox Custom Visual for Power BI](https://appsource.microsoft.com/en-us/product/power-bi-visuals/olgaltatokorlatoltfelelossegutarsasag1643647545884.mapbox_pbi_custom_visual?tab=Overview&exp=ubp8)
     page in AppSource
2.  Click **Get it now** to add the visual to your Power BI environment
3.  Follow the installation instructions provided on the AppSource page

Once installed, the Mapbox visual will be available in your Power BI visualizations panel.

Getting Started[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/mapbox-custom-visual-for-power-bi/#getting-started "Getting Started への直接リンク")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

To use the Mapbox Custom Visual in your Power BI reports:

1.  **Add the Visual**: Select the Mapbox icon from your visualizations panel
2.  **Configure Data**: Drag fields from your dataset to the appropriate data roles (latitude, longitude, size, color, etc.)
3.  **Customize Styling**: Use the formatting options to adjust colors, map styles, and visual properties
4.  **Set Up Interactivity**: Configure cross-filtering and highlighting with other visuals in your report

Use Cases[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/mapbox-custom-visual-for-power-bi/#use-cases "Use Cases への直接リンク")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The Mapbox Custom Visual is ideal for a variety of business scenarios:

*   **Sales Analytics**: Visualize sales performance by region, territory, or customer location
*   **Supply Chain Management**: Track shipments, inventory levels, and distribution networks
*   **Real Estate**: Display property values, market trends, and demographic data
*   **Retail Analytics**: Analyze store performance, customer density, and market size
*   **Risk Management**: Map risk factors, incidents, and compliance data geographically

Support and Resources[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/mapbox-custom-visual-for-power-bi/#support-and-resources "Support and Resources への直接リンク")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The Mapbox Power BI integration is maintained through a partnership with StarSchema. For technical support:

*   **GitHub Repository**: [`mapboxgl-powerbi`](https://github.com/starschema/mapboxgl-powerbi)
     - Report issues, request features, and access documentation
*   **Community Forums**: Ask questions on [Stack Overflow](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/tagged/powerbi)
     (use tags: `powerbi` and `mapbox`)
*   **Power BI Community**: Connect with other users on the [Power BI Community Forums](https://community.fabric.microsoft.com/t5/Power-BI-forums/ct-p/powerbi)
    

Technical Requirements[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/mapbox-custom-visual-for-power-bi/#technical-requirements "Technical Requirements への直接リンク")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   Microsoft Power BI Desktop or Power BI Service
*   Valid Mapbox access token (get one free at [mapbox.com](https://account.mapbox.com/auth/signup/)
    )
*   Internet connectivity for map tile loading

Pricing[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/mapbox-custom-visual-for-power-bi/#pricing "Pricing への直接リンク")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The Mapbox Custom Visual for Power BI is free to use. Standard Mapbox pricing applies for map loads and API usage. View current pricing at [mapbox.com/pricing](https://www.mapbox.com/pricing/)
.

# annotation | Help | Mapbox

[メインコンテンツまでスキップ](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/annotation/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

An **annotation** is a defined location on a map in the [Mapbox Maps SDK for iOS](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/ja/maps/)
 that is represented by a custom [`UIView`](https://developer.apple.com/documentation/uikit/uiview)
 or [`UIImage`](https://developer.apple.com/documentation/uikit/uiimage)
.

You can use annotations to represent point data, such as restaurants in a city, bus stops along a route, or earthquake locations. There are multiple ways to add annotations to your maps in the Mapbox Maps SDK for iOS. These methods are outlined in the [Mapbox Maps SDK for iOS documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/ja/maps/guides/annotations/)
.

**Related resources:**

*   [Mapbox Maps SDK for iOS documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/ja/maps/guides/)
    
*   [Mapbox Maps SDK for iOS Adding points to a map guide](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/ja/maps/guides/annotations/)

# access token | Help | Mapbox

[メインコンテンツまでスキップ](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/access-token/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

You need a Mapbox **access token** to use any of Mapbox's tools, APIs, or SDKs. Mapbox uses access tokens to associate your account with your requests to Mapbox API resources. You can find all your access tokens, create new ones, or delete existing ones on your [Access tokens page](https://console.mapbox.com/account/access-tokens/)
. You can also use the [Mapbox Tokens API](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/accounts/tokens/)
 to programmatically create, update, and delete access tokens.

Access tokens have _token scopes_ that grant access to different actions. Token scopes define which Mapbox APIs can be accessed by a token, as well as which methods can be used to access those APIs. Access tokens have either [public](https://docs.mapbox.com/accounts/ja/guides/tokens/#public-scopes)
 or [secret](https://docs.mapbox.com/accounts/ja/guides/tokens/#secret-scopes)
 scopes.

Your account will always have at least one public access token. This token is your [_default public token_](https://docs.mapbox.com/accounts/ja/guides/tokens/#default-public-access-token)
. When you're logged into your account, this token will automatically be embedded in example code on Mapbox.com.

Here's what an access token looks like in a Mapbox API request (specifically, the [Geocoding API](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/search/geocoding/)
):

    https://api.mapbox.com/search/geocode/v6/forward?q=Los%20Angeles&access_token=YOUR_MAPBOX_ACCESS_TOKEN

**Related resources:**

*   [How do I create an API access token?](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/access-tokens/)
    
*   [List of public and private token scopes](https://docs.mapbox.com/accounts/ja/guides/tokens/#scopes)
    
*   [Default public access token documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/accounts/ja/guides/tokens/#default-public-access-token)
    
*   [Tokens API documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/accounts/#tokens)
    

このpageは役に立ちましたか？はいいいえ

# attribution | Help | Mapbox

[メインコンテンツまでスキップ](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/attribution/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

Mapbox **attribution** is the combination of two visual elements that are placed on all Mapbox maps: the Mapbox _logo_ and _text attribution_. Together, these two elements state that a map was created with Mapbox tools and data.

The Mapbox logo is a small image that contains the stylized word "Mapbox" and the Mapbox logo.

The text attribution contains at least three links: [© Mapbox](https://www.mapbox.com/about/maps/)
, [© OpenStreetMap](http://www.openstreetmap.org/about/)
, and [Improve this map](https://www.mapbox.com/map-feedback/#/-74.5/40/10)
. You _must_ properly attribute and link [Mapbox](https://www.mapbox.com/about/maps/)
 and [OpenStreetMap](http://www.openstreetmap.org/about/)
 when using the Mapbox Streets tileset. Some other Mapbox-provided tilesets require additional attribution, which is stored in the [TileJSON](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/tilejson/)
 of the tileset.

**Related resources:**

*   [Mapbox attribution guide](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/attribution/)
    
*   [Mapbox press page](https://www.mapbox.com/about/press/brand-guidelines)
    : download a ZIP file with the Mapbox logo in various formats and styles
