# latitude and longitude | Help | Mapbox

[メインコンテンツまでスキップ](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/lat-lon/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

**Latitude and longitude** are a pair of numbers (_coordinates_) used to describe a position on the plane of a [geographic coordinate system](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Latitude_and_longitude)
. Across the Mapbox product ecosystem, latitude and longitude are formatted in [decimal degrees](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Decimal_degrees)
 and range from `-90` to `90` for latitude and `-180` to `180` for longitude.

For example, Washington DC has a latitude `38.8951` and longitude `-77.0364`. In API calls you will often see the numbers placed together and separated by a comma: `-77.0364,38.8951`. In some cases, altitude can be included as a third value.

Find a location's coordinates[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/lat-lon/#find-a-locations-coordinates "Find a location's coordinates への直接リンク")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

If you want to find a location's coordinates, use the [Location Helper](https://labs.mapbox.com/location-helper/#3/40.78/-73.97)
 which allows you to search for a location and copy its coordinates for use in your code.

Present coordinates in real time[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/lat-lon/#coordinates-in-real-time "Present coordinates in real time への直接リンク")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The [Get coordinates of the mouse pointer](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/ja/example/mouse-position/)
 Mapbox GL JS example shows how to query the geographic coordinates for the position of the mouse cursor on a web map.

Adding longitude and latitude lines to your map[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/lat-lon/#long-lat-lines "Adding longitude and latitude lines to your map への直接リンク")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

If you want to add visible longitude and latitude lines (a graticule) to your map, there are [community plugins](https://github.com/maptiler/maplibre-grid)
 for Mapbox GL JS available, or you can add a graticule dataset to your map style. A list of graticule datasets is located at [geojson.xyz](https://geojson.xyz/)
.

Coordinate format handling[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/lat-lon/#coordinate-format-handling "Coordinate format handling への直接リンク")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Different Mapbox services handle coordinate order differently. When you start using a Mapbox product, read its documentation to see how the latitude and longitude should be formatted. For example, [Mapbox GL JS](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/ja/api/geography/#lnglat)
 and [most Mapbox web services APIs](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/overview/#coordinate-format)
 use the format `longitude,latitude`, while other products including the [Mapbox Maps SDK for iOS](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/ja/maps/guides/)
 use the format `latitude,longitude`.

[EXAMPLE\
\
Add a default marker to a web map\
\
Learn how to add Markers to a Mapbox GL JS web map using latitude and longitude coordinates.](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/ja/example/geojson-markers/)

**Related resources:**

*   [Mapbox web services APIs coordinate documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/overview/#coordinate-format)
    
*   [Mapbox GL JS `LngLat` object documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/ja/api/geography/#lnglat)

# expression | Help | Mapbox

[メインコンテンツまでスキップ](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/expression/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

An **expression** defines a formula for computing the value of any [layout property, paint property](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/layout-paint-property/)
, or [filter](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/filter/)
 within a map style. Expressions allow you to style data with multiple feature properties at once, apply conditional logic, and manipulate data with mathematical, logical, and string operators. This allows for sophisticated [runtime styling](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/runtime-styling/)
.

For a complete list of all available expressions, see the [Mapbox Style Specification](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/style-spec/reference/expressions/)
.

How to use expressions in Mapbox Studio

Learn how to use expressions for string and data operations to create a map with bilingual labels.

### Expressions in Mapbox GL JS[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/expression/#expressions-in-mapbox-gl-js "Expressions in Mapbox GL JS への直接リンク")

[Mapbox GL JS](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/ja/)
 expressions uses a Lisp-like syntax, using JSON arrays. Expressions in Mapbox GL JS follow this format:

    [expression_name, argument_0, argument_1, ...]

The `expression_name` is the expression operator. For example, you would use [`*`](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/style-spec/reference/expressions/#*)
 to multiply two arguments:

    ['*', ['pi'], ['^', 3, 2]]

The first argument is [`pi`](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/style-spec/reference/expressions/#pi)
, which is an expression that returns the mathematical constant pi. The second argument is another expression in which [`^`](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/style-spec/reference/expressions/#%5E)
 has two arguments of its own. It will return 32, and the result will be multiplied by pi.

### Expressions in the mobile Maps SDKs[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/expression/#expressions-in-the-mobile-maps-sdks "Expressions in the mobile Maps SDKs への直接リンク")

The [Maps SDK for iOS](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/ja/maps/)
 uses [`NSExpression` and `NSPredicate` syntax](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/maps/api/6.4.1/predicates-and-expressions.html)
 for expressions instead of JSON arrays.

In the [Maps SDK for Android](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/ja/maps/)
 expressions are JSON arrays, in which the first element is the expression operator and the other elements are the arguments to the expression. For more information on using expressions in the Maps SDK for Android, see the [expressions documentation page](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/ja/maps/guides/styles/use-expressions/)
. Expressions are an important part of the Maps SDK's concept of _data-driven styling_. The [Mapbox Annotations Plugin for Android](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/ja/plugins/overview/annotation/)
 also simplifies using expressions to adjust the visual properties of annotations on a Mapbox map.

* * *

**Related resources:**

*   [Expression documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/style-spec/reference/expressions/)
    
*   Tutorial: [Get started with Mapbox GL JS expressions](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/tutorials/mapbox-gl-js-expressions/)
    
*   [Maps SDK for Android: Expressions documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/ja/maps/guides/styles/use-expressions/)
    
*   [Maps SDK for iOS: Expressions documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/ja/maps/guides/styles/use-expressions/)
    
*   Tutorial: [Create interactive hover effects with Mapbox GL JS](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/tutorials/create-interactive-hover-effects-with-mapbox-gl-js/)

# icon | Help | Mapbox

[メインコンテンツまでスキップ](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/icon/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

The term **icon** can have different meanings depending on the context of its use. On a map, an icon is generally a visual representation of a point in space. In the context of [markers](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/marker/)
 used in Mapbox products, an icon is the visual part of a marker, which gets paired with geospatial data to represent a feature on a map.

In Mapbox, icons appear most often inside [symbol layers](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/symbol-layer/)
, but they can also be added on top of a map object using platform-native methods. An icon can be a theme image, a custom image, a Maki icon, or a generated icon.

*   _Theme images_ are icon and pattern images provided in Mapbox template styles to style map features. Each [style](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/style/)
     stores these images in its [sprite](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/sprite/)
    , which is managed by the [Mapbox Studio](https://docs.mapbox.com/studio-manual/ja/)
     style editor. Theme images are built into Mapbox template styles. Since they are used to represent existing features in a style, theme images are not recommended for visualizing custom point data.
    
*   _Custom images_ are images that you add to a map style. Custom images can be used as an [`icon-image`](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/style-spec/reference/sprite/)
     or a [`fill-pattern`](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/style-spec/reference/sprite/)
     with a style layer. Custom images added with the Mapbox Studio style editor are available in the style's sprite when the style loads. Custom images added at runtime with a Mapbox SDK or API are available in the style's sprite only after the image has been successfully loaded and added to the style. Custom images are recommended for visualizing custom data.
    
*   _Maki icons_ are images in the open source [Maki](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/maki/)
     icon set made for map designers. Some Mapbox-owned vector tilesets include a `maki` field to make it easier for developers to use Maki icons in map.
    
*   _Generated icons_ are images created with code, as distinct from icons that reference SVG or PNG image files. Generated icons can be used as a fallback for missing images to make an app more robust.
    

**Related resources:**

*   [Add markers](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/map-developer-journey/configure-interactions/#create-markers-and-popups)
     getting started guide
*   Create your own icons with the [Maki icon editor](https://labs.mapbox.com/maki-icons/editor/)
    
*   [Mapbox Streets v8 vector tileset documentation for `maki text`](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/data/tilesets/reference/mapbox-streets-v8/#maki-text)
    

**Related examples:**

*   [Add an icon to the map](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/ja/example/add-image/)
     - _Mapbox GL JS_
*   [Add custom icons with `Markers`](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/ja/example/custom-marker-icons/)
     - _Mapbox GL JS_
*   [Add an animated icon to the map](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/ja/example/add-image-animated/)
     - _Mapbox GL JS_
*   [Add a generated icon to a map](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/ja/example/add-image-generated/)
     - _Mapbox GL JS_
*   [Retrieve a static map with a Maki icon overlay](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/maps/static-images/#example-request-retrieve-a-static-map-with-a-maki-icon-overlay)
     - _Mapbox Static Images API_

# dataset | Help | Mapbox

[メインコンテンツまでスキップ](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/dataset/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

A **dataset** is an editable collection of [GeoJSON](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/geojson/)
 [features](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/features/)
 that you can store in your Mapbox account and access or edit as needed.

You can download a dataset as GeoJSON, or export a dataset to a [tileset](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/tileset/)
 that can be used in a map [style](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/style/)
. Any dataset exported to a tileset can be used in a layer in the [Mapbox Studio style editor](https://docs.mapbox.com/studio-manual/ja/)
 or in the [Data visualization component](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/tutorials/create-a-map-with-data-visualization-component/)
. Any GeoJSON feature or feature collection can be added at runtime as a [source](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/source/)
.

You can interact with datasets with these tools:

*   Use the [Mapbox Studio dataset editor](https://docs.mapbox.com/studio-manual/ja/)
     to create, upload, edit, or download a dataset, or publish a dataset to a tileset.
*   Use the [Mapbox Datasets API](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/maps/datasets/)
     to create or edit a dataset or its features, or access features as GeoJSON.
*   Use the [Mapbox Uploads API](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/maps/uploads/)
     to publish a dataset to a tileset.

**Related resources:**

*   [Mapbox Studio](https://console.mapbox.com/studio/)
     - _Log in_
*   [Mapbox Studio manual dataset documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/studio-manual/ja/reference/datasets/)
    
*   [Datasets API documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/maps/#datasets)
    
*   [Sample workflow for publishing a dataset to a tileset](https://docs.mapbox.com/studio-manual/ja/guides/#sample-workflow)

# point | Help | Mapbox

[メインコンテンツまでスキップ](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/point/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

A **point** is a geospatial [feature](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/features/)
 defined by one pair of [latitude and longitude](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/lat-lon/)
 coordinates.

You can represent point data using a circle layer, a [symbol layer](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/symbol-layer/)
, or a heatmap layer.

**Related resources:**

*   Tutorial: [Add points to a style](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/tutorials/add-data-to-mapbox-style/)
    
*   Example: [Cluster point data with GL JS](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/ja/example/cluster/)
    
*   Example: [Cluster point data with Maps SDK for iOS](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/ja/maps/examples/clustering/)
    
*   Example: [Cluster point data with Maps SDK for Android](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/ja/maps/examples/circle-layer-clustering/)

# Web applications | Help | Mapbox

[メインコンテンツまでスキップ](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/web-apps/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

Mapbox provides many tools to build maps into your website or web-based application. [Mapbox GL JS](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/ja/)
 is a JavaScript library you can use to display your Mapbox maps, add interactivity, and customize the map experience in your application. We also provide many plugins for extending your web map's functionality with drawing tools and interfaces to Mapbox web services APIs like the Mapbox Geocoding API or Mapbox Directions API. Building webpages or web applications with our JavaScript libraries will require writing code, but this guide is designed to provide you with the resources to get started.

Use cases[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/web-apps/#use-cases "Use cases への直接リンク")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Mapbox GL JS

Static Tiles API

Static Images API

How web apps work[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/web-apps/#how-web-apps-work "How web apps work への直接リンク")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

A web mapping library allows you to add a map to a webpage and define the data it contains, its appearance, and a variety of functionality. Think of it like a toolbox filled with many different map-making tools that can be used together to build beautiful and interactive custom experiences.

### Mapbox GL JS[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/web-apps/#mapbox-gl-js "Mapbox GL JS への直接リンク")

[Mapbox GL JS](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/ja/)
 is a JavaScript library for building web applications with our modern mapping technology. This guide walks through some of Mapbox GL JS's essential functions and common patterns, highlighting some of the core concepts that distinguish Mapbox GL JS from other map libraries. For those with experience using [Leaflet](https://leafletjs.com/)
 or OpenLayers, the next few sections will introduce some of the differences in Mapbox GL JS as well as features that should look familiar.

#### Client-side rendering[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/web-apps/#client-side-rendering "Client-side rendering への直接リンク")

At the heart of Mapbox GL JS is client-side rendering. In web apps using Mapbox GL JS, maps are rendered dynamically by combining [vector tiles](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/vector-tiles/)
 with [style rules](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/style-spec/)
 using JavaScript and WebGL. Rendering maps in the browser rather than on a server makes it possible to change the map's style and the data it displays dynamically and in response to user interaction.

#### The camera[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/web-apps/#the-camera "The camera への直接リンク")

The camera is the map's field of view. While the viewpoint in maps made with traditional web mapping libraries is determined by the map's center point and zoom level, Mapbox GL JS also includes parameters like pitch and bearing for adjusting the map's perspective.

*   **Center**: in longitude, latitude order.
*   **Zoom**: any number within the zoom range, including decimals. For example, 1.5 or 6.2 are valid zoom levels.
*   **Bearing**: a value between 0 and 360 degrees that determines the map's bearing, or rotation.
*   **Pitch**: a value between 0 and 85 degrees that determines the map's tilt, or pitch.

Here's an example of combining bearing and zoom:

[PLAYGROUND\
\
Location Helper\
\
To experiment with camera pitch, bearing, tilt, and zoom and get values to use in your code, try our _Location Helper_ tool.](https://labs.mapbox.com/location-helper/#3/40.78/-73.97)

#### Layers[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/web-apps/#layers "Layers への直接リンク")

Traditional JavaScript map libraries often have two distinct categories of what are called "[layers](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/layer/)
": **baselayers**, or image tiles that provide the foundation of the map, and **overlays**, which are often vector data like GeoJSON that are displayed on top of baselayers, sometimes obscuring details like labels.

Mapbox GL JS has **no distinction between baselayers and overlay layers**. This means that map details like labels and [icons](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/icon/)
 and elements like streets and buildings can be modified with JavaScript, like overlays in earlier mapping libraries. Each layer provides rules about how the renderer should draw certain data in the browser, and the renderer uses these layers to draw the map on the screen.

#### Mapbox GL JS and Mapbox GL Native[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/web-apps/#mapbox-gl-js-and-mapbox-gl-native "Mapbox GL JS and Mapbox GL Native への直接リンク")

[Mapbox GL JS](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/ja/)
 and [Mapbox GL Native](https://github.com/mapbox/mapbox-gl-native)
 are two different projects for rendering maps build to the [Mapbox Style Specification](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/style-spec/)
. They are similar, but are used for different purposes and do not have 100% feature parity.

*   **Mapbox GL JS** is a JavaScript library for making maps for the web. It can read the Mapbox Style Specification and uses WebGL to render your maps in modern browsers.
*   **Mapbox GL Native** is the backbone of the Mapbox Maps SDKs for [iOS](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/ja/maps/guides/)
     and [Android](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/ja/maps/guides/)
    . It supports the iOS and Android mobile platforms using OpenGL ES.

Creating a web app[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/web-apps/#creating-a-web-app "Creating a web app への直接リンク")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

To create a web map, you'll need to have some familiarity with HTML, CSS, and JavaScript. If you are new to web maps, explore our [tutorials](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/tutorials/)
 to help you get started.

### Mapbox GL JS[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/web-apps/#mapbox-gl-js-1 "Mapbox GL JS への直接リンク")

Before getting started coding up your Mapbox GL JS map, you'll need to include the relevant JavaScript and CSS files in your webpage. Mapbox provides hosted versions of each that you can include in the `<head>` of your HTML file:

    <script src="https://api.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/v3.14.0/mapbox-gl.js"></script><link  href="https://api.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/v3.14.0/mapbox-gl.css"  rel="stylesheet"/>

The basis of every Mapbox GL JS project is the `mapboxgl.Map` class. The example code in this section demonstrates the minimum you need to add a map to your page.

    const map = new mapboxgl.Map({  container: 'map',  style: 'mapbox://styles/mapbox/streets-v12',  center: [-74.5, 40],  zoom: 9});

*   **Container**: This is the HTML element where you would like to place your map. In the example above, it is an element with `id="map"`.
*   **Style**: The map loads a style via the URL `mapbox://styles/mapbox/streets-v12`. This is a URL to a remote file that the map will download to determine the [tilesets](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/tileset/)
     it includes and how they are styled for the end-user. Mapbox GL JS permits URLs instead of literal data in several places, including data sources. Consider using a style with [style-optimized vector tiles](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/style-optimized-vector-tiles/)
     for more performant maps.
*   **Center**: Where Mapbox GL JS handles coordinates as arrays (here `[-74.50, 40]`), it assumes that the coordinates are in [`longitude, latitude`](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/lat-lon/)
     order (versus `latitude, longitude` in Leaflet). This order corresponds to the order of coordinates in GeoJSON and every other geospatial format, as well as math's X, Y ordering.
*   **Zoom**: The zoom level at which the map should be initialized. With Mapbox GL JS, can be a decimal value.

#### Adding layers to the map[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/web-apps/#adding-layers-to-the-map "Adding layers to the map への直接リンク")

You can add layers to the map using the `addLayer()` method. [`addLayer`](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/ja/api/map/#map#addlayer)
 has only one required parameter: a Mapbox style layer object. It also accepts an optional `before` parameter, which is the ID of an existing layer to insert the new layer before. If you omit this argument, then the renderer will draw the layer on top of the map. The following sections describe the elements of a Mapbox style layer object.

##### Asynchronous[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/web-apps/#asynchronous "Asynchronous への直接リンク")

    map.on('load', () => {  map.addLayer({    id: 'terrain-data',    type: 'line',    source: {      type: 'vector',      url: 'mapbox://mapbox.mapbox-terrain-v2'    },    'source-layer': 'contour'  });});

Since these resources are **remote**, they are **asynchronous**. So code that connects to Mapbox GL JS often uses event binding to change the map at the right time. For instance:

The code uses `map.on('load')` to call `map.addLayer` only after the map's resources, including the style, have been loaded. If it were to run the `map.addLayer` method immediately, it would trigger an error because the style to which you would like to add a layer would not yet exist.

##### Specifying a source[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/web-apps/#specifying-a-source "Specifying a source への直接リンク")

You will need to define a source when you add a new layer. A source accepts a `type` and a `url` (a GeoJSON source will not have a `url`). There are five types of sources, each with its own properties:

*   [vector tiles](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/style-spec/reference/sources/#vector)
    
*   [raster tiles](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/style-spec/reference/sources/#raster)
    
*   [GeoJSON](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/style-spec/reference/sources/#geojson)
    
*   [image](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/style-spec/reference/sources/#image)
    
*   [video](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/style-spec/reference/sources/#video)
    

Tilesets can include multiple subsets of data called [source layers](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/source-layer/)
 (the Mapbox Streets tileset contains source layers for roads, parks, etc). To make sure your layers are referencing the correct source layers, your layer object also needs to include a `source-layer` (often the name of the original file). See this example:

addSource()

You can also add sources using the Mapbox GL JS `addSource()` method. There is no difference in map performance when using this alternative method, but it is sometimes preferable to keep code more readable. Read more about this method in the [Mapbox GL JS documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/ja/api/#Map#addSource)
.

    map.on('load', () => {  map.addLayer({    id: 'rpd_parks',    type: 'fill',    source: {      type: 'vector',      url: 'mapbox://mapbox.3o7ubwm8'    },    'source-layer': 'RPD_Parks'  });});

For more information on each source type, explore the Sources section of the [Mapbox Style Specification](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/style-spec/reference/sources/)
.

##### Specifying layout and paint properties[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/web-apps/#specifying-layout-and-paint-properties "Specifying layout and paint properties への直接リンク")

Layers feature two special properties that enable data styling: [`paint`](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/style-spec/reference/layers/#paint)
 and [`layout`](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/style-spec/reference/layers/#layout)
. These are used to define how data will be rendered on the map. `layout` properties refer to placement and visibility, among other high-level preferences, and are applied early in the rendering process. `paint` properties are more fine-grained style attributes like opacity, color, and translation. They are less processing-intensive and are rendered later.

The following code adds a layer to the map to style the parks data with a green fill.

addLayer()

If you added your source using the alternative `addSource()` method, you will need to include the source id as the `source` in `addLayer()`. Read more about this in the [Mapbox GL JS API documentation.](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/ja/api/#Map#addLayer)

    map.on('load', () => {  map.addLayer({    id: 'rpd_parks',    type: 'fill',    source: {      type: 'vector',      url: 'mapbox://mapbox.3o7ubwm8'    },    'source-layer': 'RPD_Parks',    layout: {      visibility: 'visible'    },    paint: {      'fill-color': 'rgba(61,153,80,0.55)'    }  });});

The final product: a map zoomed to San Francisco with a parks layer with a green fill. The layer is based on a vector source of the city's park lands data.

See the [tutorials section](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/tutorials/)
 for more Mapbox GL JS resources.

Extend your web app with plugins[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/web-apps/#extend-your-web-app-with-plugins "Extend your web app with plugins への直接リンク")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Mapbox GL JS supports a rich ecosystem of plugins you can use to extend the functionality of your web map. There are plugins for adding interactive drawing tools, adding inset maps, integrating with the Mapbox Geocoding API and the Mapbox Directions API, and more! Explore the [Mapbox GL JS plugins page](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/ja/plugins/)
 for more information.

Use Mapbox GL JS with React[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/web-apps/#use-mapbox-gl-js-with-react "Use Mapbox GL JS with React への直接リンク")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Mapbox GL JS can be used with various JavaScript frameworks, including [React](https://facebook.github.io/react/)
. To learn more about using Mapbox GL JS with React, see the [Use Mapbox GL JS in a React app tutorial](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/tutorials/use-mapbox-gl-js-with-react/)
 and our [Mapbox react examples on GitHub](https://github.com/mapbox/mapbox-react-examples)
.

このpageは役に立ちましたか？はいいいえ

---

# marker | Help | Mapbox

[メインコンテンツまでスキップ](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/marker/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

A **marker** is a visual representation of a specific [coordinate](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/lat-lon/)
 on a map. In Mapbox, a marker object has two primary parts: an [icon](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/icon/)
 that defines its visual appearance, and a geospatial data [source](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/source/)
 that defines the location where that icon will appear on a map. In each Mapbox SDK or API, this marker object also has properties and methods that define how you can style it and add user interactions. Each Mapbox SDK and API also has specific methods for adding marker objects to a map, webpage, or mobile view.

Read our [add markers](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/map-developer-journey/configure-interactions/#create-markers-and-popups)
 getting started guide to learn how to use markers to style [point](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/point/)
 data with [Mapbox Studio](https://docs.mapbox.com/studio-manual/ja/)
, [Mapbox GL JS](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/ja/)
, and the Mapbox Maps SDKs for [iOS](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/ja/maps/)
 and [Android](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/ja/maps/)
.

**Related resources:**

*   Tutorial: [Add data to a style](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/tutorials/add-data-to-mapbox-style/)
     - _Mapbox Studio_
*   Tutorial: [Add custom markers to a web map](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/tutorials/custom-markers-gl-js/)
     - _Mapbox GL JS_
*   [Mapbox Maps SDK for Android guide: Annotations](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/ja/maps/guides/annotations/)
    
*   [Mapbox Maps SDK for iOS guide: Markers and annotations](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/ja/maps/guides/annotations/)

# Using recolorable images in Mapbox maps | Help | Mapbox

[メインコンテンツまでスキップ](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/using-recolorable-images-in-mapbox-maps/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

There are many ways to display and style images on Mapbox maps. This guide walks through how to add recolorable images — images that can be assigned a color at runtime.

What are Signed Distance Fields (SDF)?[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/using-recolorable-images-in-mapbox-maps/#what-are-signed-distance-fields-sdf "What are Signed Distance Fields (SDF)? への直接リンク")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

One of the challenges of displaying images on a map is ensuring those images still look sharp regardless of their relative size. To address this challenge, the Mapbox GL map renderer can recognize some optimized image formats and treat them as a Signed Distance Field (SDF). SDFs are a way of rendering images specially designed to preserve sharp outlines from a pixel image even when the image is resized. The Mapbox Style Specification includes several options that allow you to manipulate [icons](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/icon/)
 as SDFs at runtime.

SDF encodes the distance from each pixel to the nearest edge of the image. As a result, raster images (like a PNG) that use a single color and have a transparent background are suitable for SDF.

In Mapbox maps, Mapbox GL generates an SDF from a raster image and renders the icon you see on the map from the SDF. Once the SDF is generated, the initial image is no longer needed.

Vector formats, like [SVGs](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/svg/)
, can also be good candidates for generating an SDF. Although SVGs are a vector image format, the Mapbox GL renderer cannot treat them as vector data in the same way it handles vector tiles or GeoJSON. Converting them to SDFs, however, makes it possible to add a scalable, recolorable SVG to your map.

Benefits and limitations of SDF-enabled images[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/using-recolorable-images-in-mapbox-maps/#benefits-and-limitations-of-sdf-enabled-images "Benefits and limitations of SDF-enabled images への直接リンク")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

In the Mapbox Style Specification, it is possible to use SDFs for symbol layer icons. Mapbox-designed maps, like Mapbox Streets, do not commonly use SDFs for icons for things like points of interests (POIs) and highway shields because SDFs only support a single color. But SDF-enabled images can be appropriate in other situations. Below are some of the benefits and limitations related to SDF-enabled images.

**Benefits**

*   Optimized for displaying resized, recolored, and rotated raster images.
*   Icon color can be assigned at runtime.

**Limitations**

*   Designed to look good in only one, arbitrary, monochrome color.

Here are some examples of when SDF-enabled images may or may not be appropriate:

*   check Appropriate for icons that you want to color based on a data property.
*   check Appropriate for icons that change color when a user hovers or clicks on them.
*   close Not appropriate for more detailed images like highway shields.

Using SDF-enabled images[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/using-recolorable-images-in-mapbox-maps/#using-sdf-enabled-images "Using SDF-enabled images への直接リンク")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Four style specification properties can only be used with SDF-enabled images: [`icon-color`](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/style-spec/reference/layers/#paint-symbol-icon-color)
, [`icon-halo-color`](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/style-spec/reference/layers/#paint-symbol-icon-halo-color)
, [`icon-halo-width`](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/style-spec/reference/layers/#paint-symbol-icon-halo-width)
, and [`icon-halo-blur`](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/style-spec/reference/layers/#paint-symbol-icon-halo-blur)
. This means that regardless of the platform you're developing on, if you want to, for example, change the color of icons in a symbol layer based on the value of a data property, you will need to use an SDF-enabled image as an icon and then use [property expressions](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/style-spec/reference/expressions/)
 to specify the `icon-color` based on a data property.

The examples below all use the following image:

[arrow-downDownload PNG](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/data/shop-15.png)

Alternatives

There are a couple alternatives you could consider to create a similar effect without using SDF-enabled images as icons, including:

*   Add multiple icons to your [sprite](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/sprite/)
     — one in each color you want to be able to display. Then, use property expressions to determine which [`icon-image`](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/style-spec/reference/layers/#layout-symbol-icon-image)
     to use based on a data property.
*   Use [HTML markers in Mapbox GL JS](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/ja/example/custom-marker-icons/)
     or annotations on [iOS](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/ja/maps/guides/annotations/)
     and [Android](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/ja/plugins/overview/annotation/)
    .
*   Use a circle layer and property expressions to specify the `circle-color` based on a data property.

### Mapbox GL JS[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/using-recolorable-images-in-mapbox-maps/#mapbox-gl-js "Mapbox GL JS への直接リンク")

For web applications, you can add SDF-enabled images to your map style on the client using [`addImage()`](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/ja/api/#map#addimage)
 with the `sdf: true` option.

    map.loadImage('./your-file-name.png', (error, image) => {  if (error) throw error;  // add image to the active style and make it SDF-enabled  map.addImage('your-image-id', image, { sdf: true });});

Supported File Types

Only `.png`, `.jpg` and `.webp` files can be used with code above.

Loading images into a style using [`map.loadImage()`](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/ja/api/map/#map#loadimage)
 limits the available file types for images. Other file types like `.svg` are not supported.

Below is an example using a PNG image stored locally in the application, adding it to the map using `style.addImage()`, and setting the color of each icon according to the `STORE_TYPE` data property.

    <!doctype html><html lang="en">  <head>    <meta charset="utf-8" />    <title>Demo: Using recolorable images in Mapbox maps</title>    <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1" />    <script src="https://api.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/v3.14.0/mapbox-gl.js"></script>    <link      href="https://api.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/v3.14.0/mapbox-gl.css"      rel="stylesheet"    />    <style>      body {        margin: 0;        padding: 0;      }      #map {        position: absolute;        top: 0;        bottom: 0;        width: 100%;      }    </style>  </head>  <body>    <div id="map"></div>    <script>      mapboxgl.accessToken = '{{MAPBOX_ACCESS_TOKEN}}';      const map = new mapboxgl.Map({        'container': 'map',        'style': 'mapbox://styles/mapbox/light-v11',        'center': [-105.0178157, 39.737925],        'zoom': 12      });      map.on('load', () => {        map.loadImage('https://docs.mapbox.com/demos/using-recolorable-images-in-mapbox-maps/shop-15.png', (error, image) => {          if (error) throw error;          map.addImage('store-icon', image, { 'sdf': true });          map.addSource('food-stores', {            'type': 'vector',            'url': 'mapbox://examples.dl46ljcs'          });          map.addLayer({            'id': 'stores',            'source': 'food-stores',            'source-layer': 'food_stores-8sw1vy',            'type': 'symbol',            'layout': {              'icon-image': 'store-icon',              'icon-size': 0.5            },            'paint': {              'icon-color': [                'match', // Use the 'match' expression: https://docs.mapbox.com/style-spec/reference/expressions/#match                ['get', 'STORE_TYPE'], // Use the result 'STORE_TYPE' property                'Convenience Store',                '#FF8C00',                'Convenience Store With Gas',                '#FF8C00',                'Pharmacy',                '#FF8C00',                'Specialty Food Store',                '#9ACD32',                'Small Grocery Store',                '#008000',                'Supercenter',                '#008000',                'Superette',                '#008000',                'Supermarket',                '#008000',                'Warehouse Club Store',                '#008000',                '#FF0000' // any other store type              ]            }          });        });      });    </script>  </body></html>

SDF Value Cutoffs and Ranges

To render images with signed distance fields, we create a glyph texture that stores the distance to the next outline in every pixel. Inside of a glyph, the distance is negative; outside, it is positive. As an additional optimization, to fit into a one-byte unsigned integer, Mapbox shifts these ranges so that values between 192 and 255 represent “inside” a glyph and values from 0 to 191 represent "outside". This gives the appearance of a range of values from black (`0`) to white (`255`).

### Maps SDK for iOS[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/using-recolorable-images-in-mapbox-maps/#maps-sdk-for-ios "Maps SDK for iOS への直接リンク")

For iOS applications, you can add SDF-enabled images to your map style on the client using [`addImage`](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/maps/api/latest/documentation/mapboxmaps/stylemanager/addimage(_:id:sdf:contentinsets:)/)
 with the `sdf: true` option.

    // Add iconmapboxMap.style.addImage(UIImage(named: "IMAGE_NAME")!,                                      id: "IMAGE_ID",                                      sdf: true)

Below is an example using a PNG image stored in the application's asset catalog, adding it to the map, and setting the color of each icon according to a data property, `STORE_TYPE`.

    // Add icontry! mapView.mapboxMap.style.addImage(UIImage(named: "shop-15")!,                                      id: "shop-icon",                                      sdf: true,                                      stretchX: [],                                      stretchY: [])// Access a vector tileset that contains places of interestvar source = VectorSource()source.url = "mapbox://examples.dl46ljcs"try! mapView.mapboxMap.style.addSource(source, id: "shop-data")// Create a symbol layer and access the layer contained.var layer = SymbolLayer(id: "shops")// The source property refers to the identifier provided when the source was added.layer.source = "shop-data"layer.sourceLayer = "food_stores-8sw1vy"layer.iconImage = .constant(.name("shop-icon"))let expression = Expression(.match) {    Expression(.get) { "STORE_TYPE" }    "Convenience Store"    UIColor.red        "Convenience Store With Gas"    UIColor.red        "Pharmacy"    UIColor.red        "Specialty Food Store"    UIColor.orange        "Small Grocery Store"    UIColor.green        "Supercenter"    UIColor.blue        "Superette"    UIColor.blue        "Supermarket"    UIColor.blue        "Warehouse Club Store"    UIColor.blue    UIColor.black    }layer.iconColor = .expression(expression)try! mapView.mapboxMap.style.addLayer(layer, layerPosition: nil)

### Maps SDK for Android[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/using-recolorable-images-in-mapbox-maps/#maps-sdk-for-android "Maps SDK for Android への直接リンク")

For Android applications, you can add SDF-enabled images to your map style on the client using [`addImage()`](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/maps/api/latest/mapbox-maps-android/com.mapbox.maps/-style/#com.mapbox.maps/Style/addImage/#kotlin.String#android.graphics.Bitmap/PointingToDeclaration/)
 with the `sdf: true` option.

Below is an example using a PNG image stored locally in the application, adding it to the map using `style.addImage()`, and setting the color of each icon according to a data property, `STORE_TYPE`.

    mapView?.getMapboxMap()?.loadStyle(  styleExtension = style(Style.LIGHT) {    // Add icon.    +image("shop-icon"){      bitmap(BitmapFactory.decodeResource(resources, R.drawable.shop_15))      sdf(true)    }    // Access a vector tileset that contains places of interest.    +vectorSource("shop-data") {        url("mapbox://examples.dl46ljcs")    }    // Create a symbol layer and access the layer contained.    +symbolLayer("shops", "shop-data") {      sourceLayer("food_stores-8sw1vy")      iconImage("shop-icon")      iconColor(        match {          get {            literal("STORE_TYPE")          }          stop {            literal("Convenience Store")            rgb(255.0, 140.0, 0.0)          }          stop {            literal("Convenience Store With Gas")            rgb(255.0, 140.0, 0.0)          }          stop {            literal("Pharmacy")            rgb(255.0, 140.0, 0.0)          }          stop {            literal("Specialty Food Store")            rgb(154.0, 205.0, 50.0)          }          stop {            literal("Small Grocery Store")            rgb(0.0, 128.0, 0.0)          }          stop {            literal("Supercenter")            rgb(0.0, 128.0, 0.0)          }          stop {            literal("Superette")            rgb(0.0, 128.0, 0.0)          }          stop {            literal("Supermarket")            rgb(0.0, 128.0, 0.0)          }          stop {            literal("Warehouse Club Store")            rgb(0.0, 128.0, 0.0)          }          rgb(255.0, 0.0, 0.0)        }      )    }  })

### Mapbox Studio[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/dive-deeper/using-recolorable-images-in-mapbox-maps/#mapbox-studio "Mapbox Studio への直接リンク")

It is not possible to add, style, or preview images as SDFs in Mapbox Studio.

このpageは役に立ちましたか？はいいいえ

# Add your data | Help | Mapbox

[メインコンテンツまでスキップ](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/map-developer-journey/data-guide/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

Add your data
=============

Learn about the different ways you can overlay your data on a map.

[Get started](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/map-developer-journey/)

![](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/img/dev-journey-map/arrow.svg)

[Add map](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/map-developer-journey/start-mapping/)

![](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/img/dev-journey-map/arrow.svg)

[Add data](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/map-developer-journey/data-guide/)

![](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/img/dev-journey-map/arrow.svg)

[Configure interactions](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/map-developer-journey/configure-interactions/)

![](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/img/dev-journey-map/arrow.svg)

[Deploy](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/map-developer-journey/post-launch-checklist/)

This guide focuses on the process of taking geographic data that _already exists_ and transforming it into a format that can be used on a web or mobile map. This could be data that you downloaded from an open data portal, data that you drew in a GIS application, aerial imagery taken by a drone, or something else.

How do I add my data to a map?[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/map-developer-journey/data-guide/#how-do-i-add-my-data-to-a-map "How do I add my data to a map? への直接リンク")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Below are four options to add your custom data to a map. If your data has to be hosted externally, skip to [Option 4](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/map-developer-journey/data-guide/#option-4-load-your-data-at-runtime)
.

### **Option 1: Upload data with Mapbox Studio**[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/map-developer-journey/data-guide/#option-1-upload-data-with-mapbox-studio "option-1-upload-data-with-mapbox-studio への直接リンク")

Best for styling your data on a map using a no-code style editor and to quickly publish map changes to your live app with minimal changes to client-side code.

This option converts your source data into a tileset that is hosted on the Mapbox platform. [Tilesets](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/tileset/)
 are a styleable format optimized for high-performant rendering across all platforms.

**Supported formats:** MBTiles, KML, GPX, GeoJSON, Shapefile (zipped), CSV file, GeoTIFF

**Limitations:** 300 MB filesize limit.

[TUTORIAL\
\
Data in Mapbox Studio\
\
Understand how to prepare and upload geospatial data in Mapbox Studio.](https://docs.mapbox.com/studio-manual/ja/guides/geospatial-data/)

### **Option 2: Use the Mapbox Tiling Service (MTS)**[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/map-developer-journey/data-guide/#option-2-use-the-mapbox-tiling-service-mts "option-2-use-the-mapbox-tiling-service-mts への直接リン��ク")

Best for jobs of any size and update frequency where you want more control over how your source data is transformed and tiled. MTS is also a good option if you want to programmatically create tilesets.

**Supported formats:** Line-delimited GeoJSON, GeoJSON (Tilesets CLI). Note that there are several publicly available tools (like [mapshaper](https://mapshaper.org/)
) that convert formats like Shapefiles, KML, and CSV to GeoJSON.

**Limitations:** Each uploaded file must be below 20 GB. The maximum combined total size of all files that compose a tileset source is 50 GB. Read more about [MTS restrictions and limits](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/maps/mapbox-tiling-service/#mapbox-tiling-service-restrictions-and-limits)
.

[TUTORIAL\
\
Get started with the Mapbox Tiling Service (MTS)\
\
Create and publish a custom tileset using sample data.](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/tutorials/get-started-mts-and-tilesets-cli/)

### **Option 3: Use the Uploads API**[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/map-developer-journey/data-guide/#option-3-use-the-uploads-api "option-3-use-the-uploads-api への直接リンク")

If you want to programmatically create a tileset and cannot use MTS (because GeoJSON is not a suitable format for your data or your data is in a raster format), use the Uploads API.

**Supported formats:** MBTiles, KML, GPX, GeoJSON, Shapefile (zipped), CSV file, GeoTIFF

Get started with the [Uploads API](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/maps/uploads/)
.

### **Option 4: Load your data at runtime**[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/map-developer-journey/data-guide/#option-4-load-your-data-at-runtime "option-4-load-your-data-at-runtime への直接リンク")

If your data lives in a third-party data-source, you can load it on the client during runtime using the `addSource` method. The examples below show how you can load an externally-hosted GeoJSON on web or mobile.

Web

iOS

Android

Web

iOS

Android

    map.on('load', () => {map.addSource('earthquakes', {type: 'geojson',// Use a URL for the value for the data property.data: 'https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/assets/earthquakes.geojson'});    map.addLayer({        'id': 'earthquakes-layer',        'type': 'circle',        'source': 'earthquakes',        'paint': {            'circle-radius': 4,            'circle-stroke-width': 2,            'circle-color': 'red',            'circle-stroke-color': 'white'        }    });});

Web

iOS

Android

    let earthquakeSource = GeoJSONSource(id: "earthquakes")earthquakeSource.data = .url("https://www.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/assets/earthquakes.geojson")earthquakeSource.generateId = true        do {  try mapView.mapboxMap.addSource(earthquakeSource)} catch {  print("Ran into an error adding a source: (error)")}

Web

iOS

Android

    style.addSource(geoJsonSource("earthquakes") {url("https://www.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/assets/earthquakes.geojson")})

Dive even deeper with these code examples:

| Web | iOS | Android |
| --- | --- | --- |
| *   [Load data from an external GeoJSON file](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/ja/example/external-geojson/)<br>    <br>*   [Add a third party vector tile source](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/ja/example/third-party/)<br>    <br>*   [Add an external Web Map Service layer to your map](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/ja/example/wms/) | *   [Load data from an external GeoJSON file](https://github.com/mapbox/mapbox-maps-ios/blob/main/Sources/Examples/All%20Examples/HeatmapLayerGlobeExample.swift)<br>    <br>*   [Add a third party vector tile source](https://github.com/mapbox/mapbox-maps-ios/blob/main/Sources/Examples/All%20Examples/ExternalVectorSourceExample.swift)<br>    <br>*   [Add a third party raster tile source](https://github.com/mapbox/mapbox-maps-ios/blob/main/Sources/Examples/All%20Examples/RasterTileSourceExample.swift) | *   [Load data from an external GeoJSON file](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/ja/maps/examples/heatmap-layer/)<br>    <br>*   [Add a third-party vector tile source](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/ja/maps/examples/external-vector-source/)<br>    <br>*   [Add a third-party raster tile source](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/ja/maps/examples/tilejson/) |

How do I style my data?[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/map-developer-journey/data-guide/#how-do-i-style-my-data "How do I style my data? への直接リンク")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

You can either style your data at runtime or in Studio.

*   **Runtime Styling**: This option is ideal for event-based styling (for example, if a user hovers over data and it changes color). This option is also good if you want styling variants but don't want to support multiple styles in Studio.
*   **Styling in Studio**: This option is ideal if you want to style your data without having to alter client-side code or redeploy your application. It also keeps all your map styling in one place.

### **Style your data dynamically at runtime**[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/map-developer-journey/data-guide/#style-your-data-dynamically-at-runtime "style-your-data-dynamically-at-runtime への直接リンク")

After adding your data to a new layer, style it using one of the available [paint properties](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/style-spec/reference/layers/#fill)
 for that layer type.

The example snippet below show how you can style custom line data on web with Mapbox GL JS:

    map.addLayer({  'id': 'route',  'type': 'line',  'source': 'route',  'layout': {    'line-join': 'round',    'line-cap': 'round'  },  'paint': {    'line-color': '#888',    'line-width': 8  }});

### **Style your map in Studio**[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/map-developer-journey/data-guide/#style-your-map-in-studio "style-your-map-in-studio への直接リンク")

Any data uploaded to our platform is accessible from within Studio for quick styling. Changes you publish in Studio will be reflected in your mobile and web applications with no additional code changes needed.

Learn more about [styling in Studio](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/tutorials/aa-standard-in-studio/?tut-step=5)
.

How much will it cost?[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/map-developer-journey/data-guide/#how-much-will-it-cost "How much will it cost? への直接リンク")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Tilesets uploaded in Mapbox Studio are free, and do not incur processing or hosting charges. Tilesets uploaded using the Mapbox Tiling Service (MTS) or the Uploads API are billed by [tileset hosting and tileset processing](https://www.mapbox.com/pricing/#processing10m)
.

このpageは役に立ちましたか？はいいいえ

---

# Search Products Overview | Help | Mapbox

[メインコンテンツまでスキップ](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/search/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

Mapbox provides a full suite of products and services that enable you to add location-based search to your applications or data projects. These products include front-end search libraries and SDKs for web and mobile applications, as well as web services APIs for integration into any environment.

In this guide, you'll learn about common search UX patterns and the Mapbox search products and services that support them. You'll also find links to documentation, code examples, and API playgrounds to help you get started with location-based search.

Search UX Patterns[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/search/#search-ux-patterns "Search UX Patterns への直接リンク")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Below you can find common user experience patterns related to location-based search that you can implement in your applications using Mapbox products and services.

### Interactive Text Search[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/search/#interactive-text-search "Interactive Text Search への直接リンク")

Interactive text search combined with an interactive map is the most common location search pattern for web and mobile applications. A floating search box allows users to search for points of interest, locations, and addresses. When a search result is selected, the map flies to its location and displays a marker.

Type in the search box to test it out. Results are biased towards New York City.

The demo integrates [Mapbox Search JS](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-search-js/ja/)
 (powered by the [Search Box API](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/search/search-box/)
) with the [Mapbox GL JS](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/ja/)
 library for rendering interactive maps.

### Category Search[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/search/#category-search "Category Search への直接リンク")

Category search allows users to search for points of interest (POIs) by category, such as restaurants, hotels, or parks. These are usually connected to the map to search for category matches in the current viewport or near the user's location.

Click a category to see the search results. Results are biased to the center of the map. You can try moving to a different location and searching again.

### Coordinate Search (reverse geocoding)[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/search/#coordinate-search-reverse-geocoding "Coordinate Search (reverse geocoding) への直接リンク")

Coordinate search, also known as reverse geocoding, allows users to search for addresses or points of interest by entering coordinates. This is useful for applications that need to display location information based on a user's current location or a selected point on a map.

Drag the marker to a new location to request a Coordinate Search result for nearby points of interest (POIs). The result's `name` property will be displayed in the panel at the top left of the map.

### Address Autocomplete[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/search/#address-autocomplete "Address Autocomplete への直接リンク")

Address Autocomplete is the auto-filling and validating of address forms in web and mobile apps (also referred to as Address Autofill or Address Form Fill). Address autocomplete is a common feature in e-commerce, delivery, and other applications that require users to enter addresses, and helps prevent errors and improve the user experience.

Try address autofill using a live example in the Mapbox Search JS documentation:

[EXAMPLE\
\
Address Autofill Example\
\
Try address autofill in a simulated online checkout experience.](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-search-js/ja/example/autofill-checkout-js/)

To get started with address autocomplete, see the guide for your platform:

[GUIDE\
\
Address Autofill\
\
Learn about address autofill in Mapbox Search JS.](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-search-js/ja/guides/autofill/)
[GUIDE\
\
Address Form Fill\
\
Learn about address form fill in an iOS application.](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/ja/search/guides/address-formfill/)
[GUIDE\
\
Address Autofill\
\
Learn about address autofill in an Android application.](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/ja/search/guides/address-autofill/)

Frontend search products[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/search/#frontend-search-products "Frontend search products への直接リンク")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Mapbox provides front-end search products for web and mobile applications. These products interact with the Mapbox Search APIs behind the scenes and provide standard user interface components that make it easier to add search functionality to applications with minimal effort.

### Mapbox Search JS[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/search/#mapbox-search-js "Mapbox Search JS への直接リンク")

[Mapbox Search JS](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-search-js/ja/)
 is a JavaScript library that provides a search box for use in web applications. The package includes React components as well as web components and is applicable in any web development environment. The search box provides autocomplete suggestions as users type, and displays search results on a map.

To get started with Mapbox Search JS, see its [documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-search-js/ja/)
 and [code examples](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-search-js/ja/examples/)
.

[EXAMPLE\
\
Add Search Box to a Web Map\
\
View sample code for adding a search box to a web map using Mapbox Search JS.](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-search-js/ja/example/add-search-box-to-map/)

### Search SDK for Android[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/search/#search-sdk-for-android "Search SDK for Android への直接リンク")

The [Mapbox Search SDK for Android](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/ja/search/)
 is a software development kit (SDK) that provides a search box for use in Android applications. The search box provides autocomplete suggestions as users type, and displays search results on a map.

To get started with the Maps SDK for Android, see its [documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/ja/search/)
 and [code examples](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/ja/search/examples/)
.

[EXAMPLE\
\
Basic search + Maps SDK integration\
\
Add search UI to a map in an Android application.](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/ja/search/examples/maps-sdk-integration/)

### Search SDK for iOS[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/search/#search-sdk-for-ios "Search SDK for iOS への直接リンク")

The [Search SDK for iOS](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/ja/search/)
 is a software development kit (SDK) that provides a search box for use in iOS applications. The search box provides autocomplete suggestions as users type, and displays search results on a map.

To get started with the Maps SDK for iOS, see its [documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/ja/search/)
 and [code examples](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/ja/search/examples/)
.

[EXAMPLE\
\
Search UI SDK and Maps SDK integration\
\
Add a drop-in search UI to a Mapbox map in an iOS application.](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/ja/search/examples/search-ui-and-maps-sdk/)

These front-end search products may also include "core" APIs, interfaces that allow you to interact with the search functionality programmatically and bring your own user interface components. See search wrappers section below.

### Frontend Search Product Pricing[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/search/#search-product-pricing "Frontend Search Product Pricing への直接リンク")

Pricing for frontend search products is based on usage of the underlying search APIs. See the [Search API Pricing](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/search/#search-api-pricing)
 section below for more information.

Search APIs[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/search/#search-apis "Search APIs への直接リンク")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Mapbox has two web services APIs for searching locations and addresses: the **Search Box API** and the **Geocoding API**. You can access these APIs from any environment that can make HTTP requests, such as web browsers, mobile applications, and server-side code.

The differences between the Search Box API and the Geocoding API are subtle, but important. The Search Box API includes points of interest (POIs) in its results, while the Geocoding API does not. The Geocoding API also supports both permanent and temporary geocoding requests, batch processing, and structured input.

| Feature | Search Box API | Geocoding API |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Includes Points of Interest (POIs) | check | close |
| Session-based billing | check | close |
| Permanent Storage Allowed\* | close | check |
| Batch Processing | close | check |
| Structured Input | close | check |

\* Permanent geocoding incurs higher costs than temporary geocoding. See the [Mapbox Pricing Page](https://www.mapbox.com/pricing#permanent-geocoding-api)
 for details.

### Search Box API[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/search/#search-box-api "Search Box API への直接リンク")

The Search Box API supports **interactive search**, **category search**, **forward search,** and **reverse search**. Search Box API responses can include addresses, places, and points of interest (POIs). The typical use case for the Search Box API is to provide search suggestions as users type in a search box, and to retrieve detailed information about a selected search result.

| Search Type | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Interactive Search | Provides autocomplete search suggestions, intended for use as a user types in a search input. |
| Category Search | Searches for points of interest (POIs) by category. |
| Forward Search | Converts a full search query to a list of matching locations (intended for non-interactive search input) |
| Reverse Search | Converts coordinates to a list of matching locations. |

To get started with the Search Box API, see its [reference documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/search/search-box/)
 or explore its API Playgrounds:

[PLAYGROUND\
\
Search Box API Playground - Suggest and Retrieve\
\
Experiment with parameters and see response data for the `/suggest` and `/retrieve` endpoints of the Search Box API.](https://docs.mapbox.com/playground/ja/search-box/)
[PLAYGROUND\
\
Search Box API Playground - Forward and Reverse\
\
Experiment with parameters and see response data for the `/forward` and `/reverse` endpoints of the Search Box API.](https://docs.mapbox.com/playground/ja/search-box/)

### Geocoding API[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/search/#geocoding-api "Geocoding API への直接リンク")

The Geocoding API supports non-interactive geocoding of addresses and reverse geocoding of coordinates. The typical use case for the Geocoding API is to enrich your data with location information (for example, batch geocoding to add point coordinates to a list of addresses), or to power other location-based services.

Note

The Geocoding API does not include points of interest (POIs) in its results. If you need to search for POIs, use the Search Box API instead.

To get started with the Geocoding API, see its [reference documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/search/geocoding/)
 or try its API Playground:

[PLAYGROUND\
\
Geocoding API Playground\
\
Experiment with request parameters for the Geocoding API using a graphical interface, and inspect the API response object.](https://docs.mapbox.com/playground/ja/geocoding/)

### Address Autocomplete[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/search/#address-autocomplete-1 "Address Autocomplete への直接リンク")

Since address autocomplete is a frontend-specific feature, it is intended to be accessed via the frontend search products listed above and is not available as a separate web API.

### Wrapping Libraries[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/search/#wrapping-libraries "Wrapping Libraries への直接リンク")

You can use Mapbox Search APIs directly, but you can also use one of several wrapper libraries to integrate them into applications across platforms. These libraries provide a more convenient way to interact with the APIs and may include additional features or functionality.

*   **Mapbox Search JS** - Use [`Search JS Core`](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-search-js/ja/guides/#search-js-core)
     to interact with both the Search Box API and Geocoding API in a **web or node.js environment**.
*   **Mapbox Search SDK for iOS** - Use the [`Search Core`](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/search/api/core/2.9.0-rc.2/)
     module for iOS to interact with the Search Box API and Geocoding API in an **iOS environment**.
*   **Mapbox Search SDK for Android** - Use the [`Search Core`](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/search/api/core/2.8.0-rc.1/)
     module for Android to interact with the Search Box API and Geocoding API in an **Android environment**.

### Other use cases[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/search/#other-use-cases "Other use cases への直接リンク")

*   **Bulk Geocoding** - Convert a list of addresses to coordinates in a single HTTP POST request to the Geocoding API.
*   **Address Validation** - Validate user-entered addresses to clean up data and prevent errors.
*   **GPS Trace Lookup** - Use reverse geocoding to convert GPS coordinates from vehicles to human-readable addresses.
*   **Discover Administrative Boundaries** - Reverse geocode coordinates to find the city, state, and country where a user point is located.

### Search API Pricing[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/search/#search-api-pricing "Search API Pricing への直接リンク")

Pricing for search APIs depends on the product and type of request.

The Search Box API's interactive search (using the suggest and retrieve endpoints) is session-based. A single user making multiple requests in a short period of time is considered a single session. The Search Box API's forward and reverse search endpoints are not session-based, so each request is billed separately.

All Geocoding API requests are billed separately, but temporary versus permanent geocoding requests are priced differently.

For full details, see the [Mapbox Pricing page](https://www.mapbox.com/pricing#search)
.

このpageは役に立ちましたか？はいいいえ

# Mapbox のはじめ方 | Help | Mapbox

[メインコンテンツまでスキップ](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

ようこそ、Mapbox へ！このガイドでは、Mapbox の主要な製品とツールの概要と、強力な位置情報アプリケーションを構築するための基本ステップやリソースを紹介します。

Mapbox とは？[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/#what-is-mapbox "Mapbox とは？ への直接リンク")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Mapbox は、開発者がカスタム地図、ナビゲーション、検索機能をアプリに組み込むための構成要素を提供する、現代的な**位置情報プラットフォーム**です。Mapbox Studio のような強力なツールや豊富な API、ライブラリ、SDK を使って、Web、モバイル、ゲーム、組み込みシステム向けの高性能な地理空間体験を構築できます。

プロジェクトの一環として地図を作成する場合でも、複雑な地理空間アプリを構築する場合でも、Mapbox はそのビジョンを実現するためのサービスとソフトウェアを提供します。プラットフォームは柔軟性が高く、スケーラブルで使いやすく、あらゆるスキルレベルの開発者に対応しています。

[無料アカウントを作成する](https://console.mapbox.com/)

プラットフォーム概要[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/#platform-overview "プラットフォーム概要 への直接リンク")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Mapbox は、API、SDK、ツールで構成されるモジュール型プラットフォームを提供し、開発者がそれらを組み合わせて完全にカスタマイズされたロケーション体験を構築できます。製品群は主に次の4カテゴリに分類されます：

[PLAYGROUND\
\
インタラクティブ開発者チートシート\
\
製品カタログをビジュアルで確認できるチートシートを試してみてください](https://labs.mapbox.com/developer-cheatsheet)

### Maps（地図）[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/#maps%E5%9C%B0%E5%9B%B3 "Maps（地図） への直接リンク")

地図は Mapbox プラットフォームの基盤です。Mapbox はインタラクティブマップ、静的マップ、カスタムスタイルなど多彩なマッピング製品を提供しており、すべて堅牢なグローバル空間データに基づいています。

![Mapbox Maps](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/609ed46055e27a02ffc0749b/668ed88b0d1cb50a971bf2e9_64d2310b77a293e18725a871_London_Day%2520-Mapbox%2520Standard-2023-MKTG-approved%2520(rounded).png)

[Mapbox GL JS](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/ja/)
、[iOS](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/ja/maps/)
、[Android](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/ja/maps/)
 向け Maps SDK は、Web やモバイルアプリで高性能なインタラクティブマップを描画するための強力なライブラリです。3D 地形、ベクトルタイル、動的スタイリングといった高度な機能に対応し、インタラクションとユーザー体験の完全な制御が可能です。

Maps API はマップデータへのプログラムによるアクセスを提供し、データ管理やカスタムマップ体験の構築に活用できます。

[GUIDE\
\
Map 製品の概要\
\
Mapbox Maps 製品の概要を読む](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/maps/)

### Search（検索）[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/#search%E6%A4%9C%E7%B4%A2 "Search（検索） への直接リンク")

Mapbox の Search 製品は、ジオコーディング、リバースジオコーディング、地点検索などの機能を提供します。

![Mapbox Search Box](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/6050a76fa6a633d5d54ae714/642be30ada547980c2c8b481_search-box__colout_01.jpg)

フロントエンドの検索ライブラリや SDK は、UI コンポーネントを簡単に導入でき、アプリに素早く検索機能を追加できます。[Mapbox Search API](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/search/)
 は、カスタム検索体験を実現するためのデータアクセスを提供します。

[GUIDE\
\
Search 製品の概要\
\
Mapbox Search 製品の概要を読む](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/search/)

### Navigation（ナビゲーション）[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/#navigation%E3%83%8A%E3%83%93%E3%82%B2%E3%83%BC%E3%82%B7%E3%83%A7%E3%83%B3 "Navigation（ナビゲーション） への直接リンク")

[iOS](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/ja/navigation/guides/)
、[Android](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/ja/navigation/guides/)
 向け Navigation SDK は、インタラクティブなターンバイターンナビゲーション、ルート最適化、リアルタイム交通情報を提供します。UI コンポーネントをすぐに統合でき、スタイルやナビ体験の完全なカスタマイズも可能です。

![Mapbox Navigation](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/6050a76fa6a633d5d54ae714/6596080d45084a14ff2582b2_img-hero.webp)

[Navigation API](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/navigation/)
 はルーティング、交通、ナビゲーションデータへのアクセスを提供します。具体的には [Directions API](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/navigation/directions/)
（ターンバイターン）、[Isochrone API](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/navigation/isochrone/)
（到達時間分析）、[Map Matching API](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/navigation/map-matching/)
（GPS トレースの道路スナップ）などがあります。

[GUIDE\
\
Navigation 製品の概要\
\
Mapbox Navigation 製品の概要を読む](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/navigation/)

### データ製品[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/#%E3%83%87%E3%83%BC%E3%82%BF%E8%A3%BD%E5%93%81 "データ製品 への直接リンク")

Mapbox のデータ製品は、地図やアプリケーションを補完する追加のデータレイヤーやインサイトを提供します。交通、移動、行政境界などのプレミアムデータセットが含まれます。

すべての Mapbox データ製品については [ドキュメントのランディングページ](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/#data)
 をご覧ください。

Map 開発者ジャーニー[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/#build-with-mapbox "Map 開発者ジャーニー への直接リンク")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

\_\_インタラクティブな地図\_\_に\_\_カスタムデータ\_\_と\_\_ユーザー操作\_\_を組み込むことは、Mapbox 初心者が最もよく行う開発の流れです。以下のジャーニーでは、このステップをガイドし、必要なドキュメントやリソースも紹介します。

[Get started](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/map-developer-journey/)

![](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/img/dev-journey-map/arrow.svg)

[Add map](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/map-developer-journey/start-mapping/)

![](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/img/dev-journey-map/arrow.svg)

[Add data](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/map-developer-journey/data-guide/)

![](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/img/dev-journey-map/arrow.svg)

[Configure interactions](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/map-developer-journey/configure-interactions/)

![](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/img/dev-journey-map/arrow.svg)

[Deploy](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/map-developer-journey/post-launch-checklist/)

[Map 開発者ジャーニーをたどる](https://docs.mapbox.com/getting-started/map-developer-journey/)

料金を理解する[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/#%E6%96%99%E9%87%91%E3%82%92%E7%90%86%E8%A7%A3%E3%81%99%E3%82%8B "料金を理解する への直接リンク")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Mapbox では、開発者フレンドリーな価格モデルを採用しており、開発初期は無料枠を提供し、本番運用に移行すると従量課金に切り替わります。成長に応じて費用が発生する仕組みです。[製品ごとの料金](https://www.mapbox.com/pricing/)
をご確認ください。

年間契約割引や他のオプションについては、[営業チーム](https://www.mapbox.com/contact/sales/)
にお問い合わせください。

アカウント管理[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/#manage-your-account "アカウント管理 への直接リンク")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[Mapbox Console](https://console.mapbox.com/)
 は、アカウント管理のハブです。アクセストークンの作成と管理、使用状況の確認、請求設定の変更などが行えます。また、マップスタイルやデータセットの管理、Mapbox Studio でのマップデザインもここからアクセスできます。

インスピレーションを得る[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/#get-inspired "インスピレーションを得る への直接リンク")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

次のリソースでプロジェクトのヒントを得ましょう：

*   Mapbox の [ブログ](https://www.mapbox.com/blog)
     で新機能や製品情報をチェック
*   [カスタマーショーケース](https://mapbox.com/showcase)
     で多様な業界での導入事例を見る
*   [ウェビナー](https://www.mapbox.com/webinars)
     に参加するか、[YouTube チャンネル](https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCIHl9sd2brgvjBlSETKYDcg)
    で過去のセッションを視聴
*   [X（旧Twitter）](https://x.com/mapbox)
    、[Instagram](https://www.instagram.com/mapbox/)
    、[BlueSky](https://bsky.app/profile/mapbox.com)
    、[LinkedIn](https://www.linkedin.com/company/mapbox)
     をフォロー
*   [Mapbox GL JS のインタラクティブなサンプル](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/ja/example/)
    とコードスニペットをチェック（多くはモバイルにも応用可能）
*   [iOS](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/tutorials/maps-sdk-ios-examples-app/)
    、[Android](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/tutorials/maps-sdk-android-examples-app/)
     向けのサンプルアプリとソースコードを参照

ヘルプを得る[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/#get-help "ヘルプを得る への直接リンク")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

If you have questions about your account, billing or email verification issues, you can create a [support ticket](https://support.mapbox.com/hc/en-us/requests/new?ticket_form_id=360000279191)
.

If you would like to contact sales to ask about our product offerings, you can contact the sales team through the [following form](https://www.mapbox.com/contact/sales?page=/contact)
.

If you are experiencing technical issues while implementing Mapbox, we offer [paid plans](https://www.mapbox.com/support#support-plans)
 which allow you to create tickets and contact our technical support team directly.

You can also find helpful guides and tutorials in our [help docs](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/)
. You can post questions on [Stack Overflow](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask)
 or in our [Mapbox Developer Discord](https://discord.com/invite/uMpcC5RmJh)
 to seek help from the broader community of Mapbox developers.
