# Maps Products Overview | Help | Mapbox

[メインコンテンツまでスキップ](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/maps/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

Mapbox provides a full suite of products and services that enable developers to add interactive maps and location visualizations to applications or data projects. These offerings include front-end libraries and SDKs for web and mobile apps, along with powerful APIs that can be integrated into any environment.

This guide introduces common map design and user experience patterns, as well as the Mapbox tools and services that support them. Find links to documentation, code examples, and API playgrounds for getting started.

Mapping Patterns[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/maps/#mapping-patterns "Mapping Patterns への直接リンク")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Mapbox maps integrate into any user interface. Popular use cases include:

*   **Store Locators**: Find the nearest store or location.
*   **Real Estate Apps**: Display properties on a map, with filters for price, size, and other attributes.
*   **Data Dashboards**: Visualize data on a map, such as live vehicle locations, heatmaps or choropleth maps.
*   **Logistics**: Plan a route from one location to another, showing the route and key turns along the way.
*   **News and Media**: Display news articles or media on a map, with filters for location, date, and other attributes.
*   **Games**: Create location-based games or experiences, such as scavenger hunts or augmented reality games.

See the [Mapbox Customer Showcase](https://www.mapbox.com/showcase)
 for examples of how other companies are using Mapbox maps in their applications.

Below are some common mapping patterns and the Mapbox products that support them.

### User Data on a Mapbox Style[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/maps/#user-data-on-a-mapbox-style "User Data on a Mapbox Style への直接リンク")

The simplest way to use Mapbox maps is to add your data to a predefined Mapbox map style. Mapbox **styles** are also called a 'basemap' and include things like waterways, boundaries, labels, streets, etc. The default map style, [Mapbox Standard](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/map-styles/standard/guides/)
 provides the initial map, upon which data layers can be added in a variety of ways, including:

*   **Markers and Annotations**: Add UI overlay components to represent points on the map.
*   **Custom Layers**: Add custom layers to the map style either at runtime from local or third-party sources, or in advance using [Mapbox Studio](https://studio.mapbox.com/)
    . Custom layers can represent, points, lines, or polygons, or even 3D models and imagery.

### User Data on a Custom Style[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/maps/#user-data-on-a-custom-style "User Data on a Custom Style への直接リンク")

For users who want full control over the map design, [Mapbox Studio](https://docs.mapbox.com/studio-manual/ja/guides/)
 provides a powerful interface for creating custom map styles. Use Mapbox Studio to create a custom style from scratch or change/extend a Mapbox style. With a custom style, it is also possible to add custom data layers using the same methods described above.

See our [style gallery](https://www.mapbox.com/gallery)
 for examples of custom map styles created with Mapbox Studio.

### Static Map Images[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/maps/#static-map-images "Static Map Images への直接リンク")

For use cases that do not require an interactive map, Mapbox provides static map images. Static maps are useful for embedding maps in emails, PDFs, or other documents where interactivity is not required, or where loading an interactive map is not practical.

### Spatial Data Pipelines[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/maps/#spatial-data-pipelines "Spatial Data Pipelines への直接リンク")

It is possible to upload smaller datasets in Mapbox Studio and use them in map styles, but for larger or frequently-changing datasets, it is recommended to use the [Mapbox Tiling Service (MTS)](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-tiling-service/ja/)
to build automated data pipelines that generate vector tiles from data.

Use MTS to programmatically keep tilesets up-to-date and provide the latest data to users. MTS supports a wide range of data formats and can handle large datasets, making it a powerful tool for building automated data pipelines.

Other Mapping Use Cases[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/maps/#other-mapping-use-cases "Other Mapping Use Cases への直接リンク")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Game Development[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/maps/#game-development "Game Development への直接リンク")

The Mapbox Maps SDK for Unity supports building location-based games and experiences. Use Mapbox maps to create immersive environments, add real-world locations to a game, and provide players with a unique and engaging experience

### Indoor Mapping[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/maps/#indoor-mapping "Indoor Mapping への直接リンク")

Mapbox rendering technology is also used in indoor mapping applications, such as airports, shopping malls, and other large venues. Use Mapbox maps to create interactive indoor maps that help users navigate complex spaces or venues.

Frontend Map Products[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/maps/#frontend-map-products "Frontend Map Products への直接リンク")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Mapbox provides a suite of frontend libraries and SDKs for rendering maps in web and mobile applications.

### Map Rendering Libraries and SDKs[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/maps/#map-rendering-libraries-and-sdks "Map Rendering Libraries and SDKs への直接リンク")

Mapbox map rendering libraries are designed to be easy to use and flexible, allowing for highly customized map experiences for any use case. They support advanced features like 3D terrain, vector tiles, and dynamic styling, and allow for full control of interactivity and user experience.

The renderers have feature parity, allowing for consistent map user experiences across platforms.

| Renderer | Platform | Language |
| --- | --- | --- |
| [Mapbox GL JS (v3)](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/ja/) | Web | JavaScript |
| [Maps SDK for iOS (v11)](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/ja/maps/) | iOS | Swift, Objective-C |
| [Maps SDK for Android (v11)](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/ja/maps/) | Android | Java, Kotlin |

The [Maps SDK for Flutter](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/flutter/)
 abstracts the iOS and Android SDKs, allowing developers to build cross-platform mapping applications using a single codebase.

### Map Renderer Pricing[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/maps/#map-renderer-pricing "Map Renderer Pricing への直接リンク")

**Mapbox GL JS** is billed by Map Loads, which is the number of times a map is loaded in a web application. Mapbox GL JS has a free tier, and is billed based on usage. For full details, see the [Mapbox GL JS pricing page](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/ja/guides/pricing/)
.

The **Maps SDKs for iOS and Android** are billed by the number of monthly active users (MAUs) of a mobile application. The Maps SDKs have a free tier, and are billed based on usage. For full details, see the [Maps SDK for iOS pricing page](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/ja/maps/guides/pricing/)
 and the [Maps SDK for Android pricing page](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/ja/maps/guides/pricing/)
.

The Maps SDK for Flutter abstracts the iOS and Android SDKs, so billing is the same as if the SDKs were used directly.

### Turf[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/maps/#turf "Turf への直接リンク")

[Turf](https://turfjs.org/)
 is a geospatial analysis library for JavaScript that provides a wide range of functions for working with geographic data. For example, Turf can calculate distances and areas or expand points and polygons with a _buffer_ operation. Turf is designed to work with GeoJSON data, and can be used in conjunction with Mapbox GL JS to display geospatial analysis on a map.

Turf is not a Mapbox product, but it is often referenced in the Mapbox documentation and examples. Many Turf functions have been ported to Swift ([`turf-swift`](https://github.com/mapbox/turf-swift)
) and Java ([`mapbox-sdk-turf`](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/ja/java/guides/turf/)
), for use along with the Maps SDKs for iOS and Android.

### Plugins and Extensions[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/maps/#plugins-and-extensions "Plugins and Extensions への直接リンク")

Mapbox GL JS has a rich ecosystem of plugins and extensions that add additional functionality to the library. Many plugins are community-driven, and can be used to extend the capabilities of Mapbox GL JS. See the full list of [Mapbox GL JS plugins](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/ja/plugins/)
 for more information.

[`rnmapbox`](https://github.com/rnmapbox/maps)
 is a community-maintained [React Native](https://reactnative.dev/)
 wrapper for Mapbox GL JS, allowing for Mapbox maps in React Native applications. Like the Maps SDK for Flutter, it abstracts the iOS and Android SDKs for cross-platform application development using a single codebase.

Maps APIs[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/maps/#maps-apis "Maps APIs �への直接リンク")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The [Mapbox Maps APIs](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/maps/)
 provide programmatic access to map data and services. _Most users getting started with Mapbox maps do not need to use the APIs directly, as the frontend libraries and SDKs provide a higher-level interface for working with maps._ The APIs are useful for advanced users who want to build custom map experiences or integrate Mapbox maps into their applications in a more programmatic way.

### Static Images API[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/maps/#static-images-api "Static Images API への直接リンク")

The [Static Images API](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/maps/static-images/)
 renders a static image of a map style at a specified location and zoom level. Customization options are available for the map style, size, overlays, and other parameters to generate the desired image. This is useful for embedding maps in emails, PDFs, or other documents where interactivity is not required or practical.

[PLAYGROUND\
\
Static Images API Playground\
\
Experiment with request parameters for the Static Images API using a graphical interface, and inspect the resulting map image.](https://docs.mapbox.com/playground/ja/static/)

### Tilequery API[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/maps/#tilequery-api "Tilequery API への直接リンク")

The [Tilequery API](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/maps/tilequery/)
 supports querying of vector tiles for specific features or data point without downloading the entire tile. Developers integrate this API to retrieve information about specific locations or features on the map, such as points of interest, roads, or other geographic data.

[PLAYGROUND\
\
Tilequery API Playground\
\
Experiment with request parameters for the Tilequery API using a graphical interface, and inspect the response data.](https://docs.mapbox.com/playground/ja/tilequery/)

### Mapbox Tiling Service (MTS)[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/maps/#mapbox-tiling-service-mts "Mapbox Tiling Service (MTS) への直接リンク")

[Mapbox Tiling Service (MTS)](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-tiling-service/ja/)
 is a powerful tool for generating vector or raster tiles from spatial data. Developers use MTS to create custom tilesets that can be used in custom map styles. MTS supports a wide range of data formats and can handle large datasets, making it a powerful tool for building automated data pipelines.

[TUTORIAL\
\
Get started using the Mapbox Tiling Service and the Tilesets CLI\
\
Experiment with request parameters for the Tilequery API using a graphical interface, and inspect the response data.](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/tutorials/get-started-mts-and-tilesets-cli/)

### Tile APIs[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/maps/#tile-apis "Tile APIs への直接リンク")

The following APIs provide access to tilesets hosted on Mapbox cloud infrastructure using the standard z/x/y tiling scheme. These APIs are useful when consuming tilesets in third-party libraries or applications that support zxy tiling schemes such as GIS software, data science packages, and custom mapping implementations.

*   The [Vector Tiles API](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/maps/vector-tiles/)
     serves vector tiles from Mapbox-hosted vector tilesets.
*   The [Raster Tiles API](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/maps/raster-tiles/)
     serves raster tiles from Mapbox-hosted raster tilesets.
*   The [Static Tiles API](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/maps/static-tiles/)
     serves rendered images of map styles. This is useful for legacy map renderers that use raster tiles, allowing developers to use the same Mapbox-hosted data in a variety of frontend environments.

### Styles API[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/maps/#styles-api "Styles API への直接リンク")

The [Styles API](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/maps/styles/)
 allows for programmatic access to [map styles](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/style-spec/guides/)
. Developers use this API to create, update, and delete map styles, as well as retrieve information about existing styles. This can be useful for automatically generating map styles based on user input or other data, or for managing a large number of map styles via a custom interface.

Learn more about how map styles work in the [Mapbox Style Specification](https://docs.mapbox.com/ja/style-spec/guides/)
.

### Maps API Pricing[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/maps/#maps-api-pricing "Maps API Pricing への直接リンク")

Mapbox Maps APIs have a free tier, and are billed based on usage. The pricing is based on the number of requests made to the API, with different rates for different types of requests. For example, static images are billed per image generated, while vector tiles are billed per tile requested. For full details, see the [Maps section](https://www.mapbox.com/pricing#maps)
 of the [pricing page](https://www.mapbox.com/pricing/)
.

**When used via the Mapbox renderers, vector and raster tiles usage is not billed separately and are included in the usage pricing for the renderer.** For details see the pricing guides for [Mapbox GL JS](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/ja/guides/pricing/)
, the [Maps SDK for iOS](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/ja/maps/guides/pricing/)
, and the [Maps SDK for Android](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/ja/maps/guides/pricing/)
.

Mapbox Tiling Service is billed based on a combination of data processing size, processing resources used, and storage size. For full details, see the [Tilesets section](https://www.mapbox.com/pricing#tilesets)
 of the [pricing page](https://www.mapbox.com/pricing/)
.

このpageは役に立ちましたか？はいいいえ

---

# Navigation Products Overview | Help | Mapbox

[メインコンテンツまでスキップ](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/navigation/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

Mapbox provides a full suite of products and services for adding navigation experiences and routing-related data to applications or data projects. These products include front-end navigation libraries and SDKs for web and mobile applications, as well as web services APIs for integration into any environment.

This guide covers common navigation UX patterns and use cases, and provides an overview of the Mapbox products and services that support them. Also included are links to documentation, code examples, and API playgrounds to help you get started with Mapbox navigation products.

Navigation UX Patterns[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/navigation/#navigation-ux-patterns "Navigation UX Patterns への直接リンク")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The following are the most common UX patterns using Mapbox navigation products:

### Route Preview[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/navigation/#route-preview "Route Preview への直接リンク")

Route preview is a common navigation pattern that shows a user the route they will take on a map before they start navigating. This pattern is often used in driving, walking, and cycling directions, and helps the user understand the route and make any necessary adjustments before starting navigation.

Route Previews are rendered using a response from the [Directions API](http://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/navigation/directions/)
.

### Interactive Navigation[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/navigation/#interactive-navigation "Interactive Navigation への直接リンク")

Interactive navigation presents a user with a map and turn-by-turn directions as they navigate, making use of a mobile device's live location to guide them to their destination. This pattern is used in driving, walking, and cycling directions, and is often used in conjunction with voice guidance, traffic data, and other features to help users reach their destination.

Interactive Navigation is possible with the Navigation SDKs for [iOS](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/ja/maps/)
 and [Android](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/ja/maps/)
, which are both powered by the [Directions API](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/navigation/directions/)
.

Your browser doesn't support HTML5 video. Open [link to the video](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/assets/medias/getting-started--navigation--demo-79a875d680bdfd57c3bc301941eafa3a.mp4)
.

Other Navigation Use Cases[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/navigation/#other-navigation-use-cases "Other Navigation Use Cases への直接リンク")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Mapbox Navigation products and services support a wide range of use cases beyond the most common patterns listed above. Many of these are backend or data pipeline use cases that involve analyzing locations and routing in real-time.

### Calculate Travel Time and Distance between Multiple Points[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/navigation/#calculate-travel-time-and-distance-between-multiple-points "Calculate Travel Time and Distance between Multiple Points への直接リンク")

For scenarios such as a fleet of vehicles on the move that may need to visit multiple locations, it is important to calculate the travel time and distance between multiple points at one time. This can help optimize routes, plan for traffic, and make sure that vehicles arrive at their destinations on time.

To calculate travel time and distance between multiple points, use the [Matrix API](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/navigation/matrix/)
.

### Spatial Joins with Time-Distance Isochrones[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/navigation/#spatial-joins-with-time-distance-isochrones "Spatial Joins with Time-Distance Isochrones への直接リンク")

Spatial joins with time-distance isochrones are used to find all points within a certain travel time or distance of a given point. This can be useful for finding nearby points of interest, planning delivery routes, or analyzing the accessibility of a location.

To retrieve time-distance isochrones, us the [Isochrone API](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/navigation/isochrone/)
.

### Route Optimization (The Traveling Salesperson Problem)[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/navigation/#route-optimization-the-traveling-salesperson-problem "Route Optimization (The Traveling Salesperson Problem) への直接リンク")

Route optimization is used to find the most efficient route that visits a set of locations. This is a common problem in logistics, delivery, and transportation, and can help save time, fuel, and money by optimizing routes for multiple vehicles or deliveries.

To optimize routes, use the [Optimization API](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/navigation/optimization/)
.

### Cleanup of Fuzzy Route Data for Display or Analysis[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/navigation/#cleanup-of-fuzzy-route-data-for-display-or-analysis "Cleanup of Fuzzy Route Data for Display or Analysis への直接リンク")

GPS tracks regularly log a vehicle's location, but these points can be noisy or inaccurate. Map matching cleans up fuzzy route data by snapping GPS points to the road network, making it easier to display the actual route on a map or analyze the traveled road segments and maneuvers.

To clean up fuzzy route data, use the [Map Matching API](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/navigation/map-matching/)
.

### Electric Vehicle Charging[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/navigation/#electric-vehicle-charging "Electric Vehicle Charging への直接リンク")

Electric vehicle charging is a growing market, and EV drivers need to find charging stations along their route. Drivers not only need to know where the charging stations are, but also need to know which stations are available, what types of connectors they support, and how long it will take to charge their vehicle.

To find EV charging stations, use the [EV Charge Finder API](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/navigation/ev-charge-finder/)
.

Frontend Navigation products[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/navigation/#frontend-navigation-products "Frontend Navigation products への直接リンク")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Mapbox provides frontend products that allow integration of navigation experiences and data for web and mobile applications. These products include SDKs for iOS and Android, as well as plugins for Mapbox GL JS.

### Navigation SDK for iOS[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/navigation/#navigation-sdk-for-ios "Navigation SDK for iOS への直接リンク")

The [Mapbox Navigation SDK for iOS](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/ja/navigation/)
 is a software development kit (SDK) that provides turn-by-turn navigation for iOS applications. It includes features such as voice guidance, traffic data, and route preview, and can be customized to match the look and feel of your application. It also includes a core library that provides routing and navigation functionality without the UI components.

To get started with the Navigation SDK for iOS, see its [documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/ja/navigation/)
.

[GUIDE\
\
Get Started with the Mapbox Navigation SDK for iOS\
\
Learn how to install the Navigation SDK for iOS and add turn-by-turn navigation to an iOS app.](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/ja/navigation/guides/install/)

### Navigation SDK for Android[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/navigation/#navigation-sdk-for-android "Navigation SDK for Android への直接リンク")

The [Mapbox Navigation SDK for Android](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/ja/navigation/)
 is a software development kit (SDK) that provides turn-by-turn navigation for Android applications. It includes features such as voice guidance, traffic data, and route preview, and can be customized to match the look and feel of your application. It also includes a core library that provides routing and navigation functionality without the UI components.

To get started with the Navigation SDK for Android, see its [documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/ja/navigation/)
 and [code examples](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/ja/navigation/examples/)
.

[GUIDE\
\
Get Started with the Mapbox Navigation SDK for Android\
\
Learn how to install the Navigation SDK for Android and add turn-by-turn navigation to an Android app.](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/ja/navigation/guides/install/)
[EXAMPLE\
\
Add a complete turn-by-turn navigation experience\
\
Add a full-featured navigation experience to an Android app.](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/ja/navigation/examples/turn-by-turn-experience/)

### Mapbox GL Directions Plugin[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/navigation/#mapbox-gl-directions-plugin "Mapbox GL Directions Plugin への直接リンク")

Though real-time navigation is less common in web applications, showing route previews on a map along with text-based directions is a common need for store locators and other web app use cases. The [Mapbox GL Directions plugin](https://github.com/mapbox/mapbox-gl-directions)
 is a library that provides written directions and route preview for web applications that use [Mapbox GL JS](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/ja/)
. It includes features such as route previews, traffic data, and route optimization, and can be customized to match the look and feel of your application. It is integrated with [Mapbox Search APIs](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/search/#search-apis)
 to allow users to quickly search for an origin and destination, and provides an intuitive interface for searching for an origin and destination and displaying the route on a map.

[See the Mapbox GL Directions plugin on Githubchevron-right](https://github.com/mapbox/mapbox-gl-directions)

[EXAMPLE\
\
Display navigation directions on a map\
\
Add a route preview and text-based directions to a web app using the Mapbox GL Directions plugin.](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/ja/example/mapbox-gl-directions/)

### Frontend Navigation Product Pricing[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/navigation/#frontend-navigation-product-pricing "Frontend Navigation Product Pricing への直接リンク")

Pricing for the mobile Navigation SDKs is based on a combination of monthly active users and the number of monthly trips calculated. See the [Navigation SDK section](https://www.mapbox.com/pricing#navigation-sdk-v3x-metered-trips)
 of the Mapbox Pricing page for more information

Pricing for Mapbox GL Directions is based on usage of the underlying APIs. See the [Navigation API Pricing](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/navigation/#navigation-api-pricing)
 section below for more information.

Navigation APIs[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/navigation/#navigation-apis "Navigation APIs への直接リンク")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Directions API[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/navigation/#directions-api "Directions API への直接リンク")

The [Directions API](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/navigation/directions/)
 provides routing and navigation functionality for web and mobile applications. It allows you to calculate routes between two or more locations, and provides turn-by-turn directions, traffic data, and route optimization. The response includes spatial data for displaying the calculated route on a map. The Directions API is used by the Navigation SDKs for iOS and Android, as well as the Mapbox GL Directions plugin.

It also supports Electric Vehicle (EV) routing, which allows you to calculate routes that are optimized for electric vehicles, taking into account real-time factors such as battery range, charging stations, and charging time.

To get started with the Directions API, see its [reference documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/navigation/directions/)
 or try its API Playground using one of the links below.

[PLAYGROUND\
\
Directions API Playground\
\
Experiment with request parameters for the Directions API using a graphical interface, and inspect the API response object.](https://docs.mapbox.com/playground/ja/directions/)
[PLAYGROUND\
\
Directions API Playground - Electric Vehicle Routing\
\
Experiment with request parameters for the Directions API for an Electric Vehicle (EV) using a graphical interface, and inspect the API response object.](https://docs.mapbox.com/playground/ja/directions/?engine=electric&alternatives=false&annotations=state_of_charge%2Cduration)

### Matrix API[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/navigation/#matrix-api "Matrix API への直接リンク")

The [Matrix API](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/navigation/matrix/)
 allows you to calculate travel times and distances between multiple points in a single request. This can be useful for scenarios such as route optimization, delivery planning, and fleet management.

To get started with the Matrix API, see its [reference documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/navigation/matrix/)
 or try its API playground:

[PLAYGROUND\
\
Matrix API Playground\
\
Create a matrix, experiment with request parameters, see a visualization of the response and inspect the response object.](https://docs.mapbox.com/playground/ja/matrix/)

### Isochrone API[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/navigation/#isochrone-api "Isochrone API への直接リンク")

The [Isochrone API](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/navigation/isochrone/)
 allows you to calculate isochrones, which are polygons that represent all points that can be reached within a certain travel time or distance from a given point. This can be useful for scenarios such as spatial joins, accessibility analysis, and delivery planning.

To get started with the Isochrone API, see its [reference documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/navigation/isochrone/)
 or try its API playground:

[PLAYGROUND\
\
Isochrone API Playground\
\
Experiment with request parameters for the Isochrone API using a graphical interface, and inspect the API response object.](https://docs.mapbox.com/playground/ja/isochrone/)

### Map Matching API[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/navigation/#map-matching-api "Map Matching API への直接リンク")

The [Map Matching API](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/navigation/map-matching/)
 allows you to clean up fuzzy route data by snapping GPS points to the road network. This can be useful for scenarios such as GPS trace lookup, route analysis, and route display.

To get started with the Map Matching API, see its [reference documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/navigation/map-matching/)
.

### EV Charge Finder API[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/navigation/#ev-charge-finder-api "EV Charge Finder API への直接リンク")

The EV Charge Finder API allows you to find electric vehicle charging stations near a point or along a route. This can be useful for scenarios such as electric vehicle routing, trip planning, and charging station discovery.

To get started with the EV Charge Finder API, see its [reference documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/ja/navigation/ev-charge-finder/)
 or try its API Playground:

[PLAYGROUND\
\
EV Charge Finder API Playground\
\
Experiment with request parameters for the EV Charge finder API using a graphical interface, and inspect the API response object.](https://docs.mapbox.com/playground/ja/ev-charge-finder/)

### Wrapping Libraries[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/navigation/#wrapping-libraries "Wrapping Libraries への直接リンク")

You can use Mapbox Navigation APIs directly, but you can also use one of several wrapper libraries to integrate them into applications across platforms. These libraries provide a more convenient way to interact with the APIs and may include additional features or functionality.

*   **Mapbox Navigation SDK for iOS** - Use the [`Navigation Core`](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/navigation/api/3.8.0/navigation/documentation/mapboxnavigationcore/)
     module to handle navigation without a UI in an **iOS environment**.
*   **Mapbox Navigation SDK for Android** - Use the [`Navigation Core`](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/navigation/api/coreframework/3.7.3/navigation/)
     module to handle navigation without a UI in an **Android environment**.
*   **Mapbox Java SDK** - Use the Java SDK's [Services module](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/ja/java/guides/services/)
     to interact with the Directions, Matrix, Isochrone, and Map Matching APIs in a **Java environment** (including Android App development).

### Navigation API Pricing[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/navigation/#navigation-api-pricing "Navigation API Pricing への直接リンク")

All Mapbox navigation APIs are priced based on usage and include a generous free tier for testing.

For full details, see the [Mapbox Pricing page](https://www.mapbox.com/pricing#navigation/)
.

# Configure interactions | Help | Mapbox

[メインコンテンツまでスキップ](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/map-developer-journey/configure-interactions/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

Configure interactions
======================

Turn your maps into powerful interactive experiences.

[Get started](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/map-developer-journey/)

![](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/img/dev-journey-map/arrow.svg)

[Add map](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/map-developer-journey/start-mapping/)

![](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/img/dev-journey-map/arrow.svg)

[Add data](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/map-developer-journey/data-guide/)

![](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/img/dev-journey-map/arrow.svg)

[Configure interactions](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/map-developer-journey/configure-interactions/)

![](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/img/dev-journey-map/arrow.svg)

[Deploy](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/map-developer-journey/post-launch-checklist/)

Mapbox has many options available to turn your maps into powerful interactive experiences across all platforms. Here are some of the most popular ways to add interactivity to maps with Mapbox.

Add search[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/map-developer-journey/configure-interactions/#add-search "Add search への直接リンク")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Need to interactively search for locations in your product? Or convert addresses into geographic coordinates? Try adding search or geocoding into your app.

To add search follow along with [GL JS](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/example/mapbox-gl-geocoder/)
, or get started with [iOS](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/search/guides/install/)
 and [Android](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/search/guides/get-started/)
.

Web

iOS

Android

Web

iOS

Android

    // Example from: https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/example/mapbox-gl-geocoder/map.addControl(  new MapboxGeocoder({    accessToken: mapboxgl.accessToken,    mapboxgl: mapboxgl  }));

Web

iOS

Android

    // Example from: https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/search/guides/install/import UIKitimport MapboxSearchimport MapboxSearchUIclass ViewController: UIViewController {let searchController = MapboxSearchController()  override func viewDidLoad() {    super.viewDidLoad()    searchController.delegate = self    let panelController = MapboxPanelController(rootViewController: searchController)    addChild(panelController)  }}extension ViewController: SearchControllerDelegate {func searchResultSelected(_ searchResult: SearchResult) { }func categorySearchResultsReceived(results: [SearchResult]) { }func userFavoriteSelected(_ userFavorite: FavoriteRecord) { }}

Web

iOS

Android

    // Example from: https://docs.mapbox.com/android/search/examples/search/package com.mapbox.search.sample.api;import android.os.Bundle;import android.util.Log;import androidx.annotation.Nullable;import androidx.annotation.NonNull;import androidx.appcompat.app.AppCompatActivity;import com.mapbox.search.ResponseInfo;import com.mapbox.search.SearchEngine;import com.mapbox.search.SearchEngineSettings;import com.mapbox.search.SearchOptions;import com.mapbox.search.SearchSelectionCallback;import com.mapbox.search.common.AsyncOperationTask;import com.mapbox.search.result.SearchResult;import com.mapbox.search.result.SearchSuggestion;import com.mapbox.search.sample.R;import java.util.List;public class ForwardGeocodingJavaExampleActivity extends AppCompatActivity {  private SearchEngine searchEngine;  private AsyncOperationTask searchRequestTask;  private final SearchSelectionCallback searchCallback = new SearchSelectionCallback() {      @Override      public void onSuggestions(@NonNull List<SearchSuggestion> suggestions, @NonNull ResponseInfo responseInfo) {          if (suggestions.isEmpty()) {              Log.i("SearchApiExample", "No suggestions found");          } else {              Log.i("SearchApiExample", "Search suggestions: " + suggestions + "Selecting first...");              searchRequestTask = searchEngine.select(suggestions.get(0), this);          }      }      @Override      public void onResult(@NonNull SearchSuggestion suggestion, @NonNull SearchResult result, @NonNull ResponseInfo info) {          Log.i("SearchApiExample", "Search result: " + result);      }      @Override      public void onResults(@NonNull SearchSuggestion suggestion, @NonNull List<SearchResult> results, @NonNull ResponseInfo responseInfo) {          Log.i("SearchApiExample", "Category search results: " + results);      }      @Override      public void onError(@NonNull Exception e) {          Log.i("SearchApiExample", "Search error: ", e);      }  };  @Override  protected void onCreate(@Nullable Bundle savedInstanceState) {      super.onCreate(savedInstanceState);      searchEngine = SearchEngine.createSearchEngineWithBuiltInDataProviders(          new SearchEngineSettings(getString(R.string.mapbox_access_token))      );      final SearchOptions options = new SearchOptions.Builder()          .limit(5)          .build();      searchRequestTask = searchEngine.search("Paris Eiffel Tower", options, searchCallback);  }  @Override  protected void onDestroy() {      searchRequestTask.cancel();      super.onDestroy();  }}

Learn more about our [Search](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/search/)
 products including the Search Box API and the Geocoding API.

[Test the Search Box API, experiment with query parameters, and view the results.](https://docs.mapbox.com/playground/ja/search-box/)

[Use this Developer API Sandbox to test forward and reverse geocoding queries, experiment with query parameters, and view the results.](https://docs.mapbox.com/playground/ja/geocoding/)

Create markers and popups[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/map-developer-journey/configure-interactions/#create-markers-and-popups "Create markers and popups への直接リンク")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Markers and annotations[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/map-developer-journey/configure-interactions/#markers-and-annotations "Markers and annotations への直接リンク")

[Markers](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/marker/)
 are one of the most popular ways to highlight and visualize locations or data on a map on web.

Mobile devices do _not_ contain markers. You can mimic the same functionality on [iOS](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/ja/maps/guides/markers-and-annotations/)
 and [Android](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/ja/maps/guides/annotations/)
 in two different ways, either with a symbol layer if you want to mimic a default marker or a point annotation if you want to use more complex features.

If you want to learn more, start by exploring one of these tutorials.

| Mapbox GL JS | iOS | Android |
| --- | --- | --- |
| *   [Add custom markers in Mapbox GL JS](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/tutorials/custom-markers-gl-js/)<br>    <br>*   [Add custom icons with Markers](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/example/custom-marker-icons/) | *   [Add a marker in iOS](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/maps/examples/add-markers/)<br>    <br>*   [Add dynamic view annotations in iOS](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/maps/examples/view-annotation-basic/)<br>    <br>*   [Add custom point annotation in iOS](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/maps/examples/custom-point-annotation/) | *   [Add a marker in Android](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/maps/examples/add-a-marker-symbol/)<br>    <br>*   [Add dynamic view annotations in Android](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/maps/examples/view-annotation-dynamic/)<br>    <br>*   [Add custom point annotations in Android](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/maps/examples/add-point-annotations/) |

### Show information dynamically with popups[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/map-developer-journey/configure-interactions/#show-information-dynamically-with-popups "Show information dynamically with popups への直接リンク")

Once you have data showing up on your map, you can use a [Popup](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/api/markers/#popup)
 to show relevant and contextual information when needed.

#### Popups in GL JS[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/map-developer-journey/configure-interactions/#popups-in-gl-js "Popups in GL JS への直接リンク")

On web you can explore different types of interactions, maybe you want a popup on hover or on click.

[TUTORIAL\
\
Display a popup on hover\
\
When a user hovers over a custom marker, show a Popup containing more information.](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/ja/example/popup-on-hover/)
[TUTORIAL\
\
Display a popup on click\
\
When a user clicks a circle, show a Popup containing more information.](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/ja/example/popup-on-click/)

#### User Interaction Guides for Mobile[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/map-developer-journey/configure-interactions/#user-interaction-guides-for-mobile "User Interaction Guides for Mobile への直接リンク")

For mobile, it’s important to pair the different types of content with the right gestures - we’ve put together guides on user interaction for both Android and iOS:

[GUIDE\
\
Android User Interaction Guide\
\
Learn how user interaction works in the Mapbox Maps SDK for Android.](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/ja/maps/guides/user-interaction/)
[GUIDE\
\
iOS User Interaction Guide\
\
Learn how user interaction works in the Mapbox Maps SDK for iOS.](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/ja/maps/guides/user-interaction/)

UI Controls in GL JS[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/map-developer-journey/configure-interactions/#ui-controls-in-gl-js "UI Controls in GL JS への直接リンク")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

It's important to consider how your users will interact with your map. Mapbox provides some commonly used map [UI controls](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/api/markers/#fullscreencontrol)
 right out of the box including a scale element and on-screen zoom and tilt controls.

[TUTORIAL\
\
Display zoom and rotation controls\
\
Add zoom and rotation controls to the map.](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/ja/example/navigation/)
[TUTORIAL\
\
Display map scale\
\
Add a scale to the map.](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/ja/example/navigation-scale/)

Use camera movement and animations[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/map-developer-journey/configure-interactions/#use-camera-movement-and-animations "Use camera movement and animations への直接リンク")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Your browser doesn't support HTML5 video. Open [link to the video](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/assets/medias/how-mapbox-works--configure-interactions--camera-demo-compressed-c01330fbad2aa7335e9418a4e8a83565.mp4)
.

Create stunning cinematic effects or smoothly transition map viewers from one place to another using the map camera. Start with GL JS by following one of these tutorials:

*   [Animate the camera around a point with 3D terrain](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/ja/example/free-camera-point/)
    
*   [Fly to a location after search: Use a custom camera animation with a geocoder](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/ja/example/mapbox-gl-geocoder-with-flyto/)
    

Mapbox’s mobile SDKs also give you the ability to display the user's location:

*   [Android: Display the user's location](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/ja/maps/examples/location-tracking/)
    
*   [iOS: Display the user's location](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/ja/maps/examples/tracking-mode/)
    

Dynamically cluster data[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/getting-started/map-developer-journey/configure-interactions/#dynamically-cluster-data "Dynamically cluster data への直接リンク")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Are you working with [GeoJSON](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/geojson/)
 [point](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/ja/glossary/point/)
 data? Sometimes there is too much data to show cleanly when zoomed out of the map, this is where clustering comes in. Clustering can intelligently group data points so that they are revealed intentionally as a user zooms into and out of the map.

[Example: Create and style clusters\
\
Use Mapbox GL JS' built-in functions to visualize points as clusters.](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/ja/example/cluster/)

[Example: Display HTML clusters with custom properties\
\
An example of a circle layer combined with HTML markers.](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/ja/example/cluster-html/)

# attribution | Help | Mapbox

[Skip to main content](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/glossary/attribution/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

Mapbox **attribution** is the combination of two visual elements that are placed on all Mapbox maps: the Mapbox _logo_ and _text attribution_. Together, these two elements state that a map was created with Mapbox tools and data.

The Mapbox logo is a small image that contains the stylized word "Mapbox" and the Mapbox logo.

The text attribution contains at least three links: [© Mapbox](https://www.mapbox.com/about/maps/)
, [© OpenStreetMap](http://www.openstreetmap.org/about/)
, and [Improve this map](https://www.mapbox.com/map-feedback/#/-74.5/40/10)
. You _must_ properly attribute and link [Mapbox](https://www.mapbox.com/about/maps/)
 and [OpenStreetMap](http://www.openstreetmap.org/about/)
 when using the Mapbox Streets tileset. Some other Mapbox-provided tilesets require additional attribution, which is stored in the [TileJSON](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/glossary/tilejson/)
 of the tileset.

**Related resources:**

*   [Mapbox attribution guide](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/dive-deeper/attribution/)
    
*   [Mapbox press page](https://www.mapbox.com/about/press/brand-guidelines)
    : download a ZIP file with the Mapbox logo in various formats and styles

Glossary
========

Find Mapbox-related terms and their definitions.

# access token | Help | Mapbox

[Skip to main content](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/glossary/access-token/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

You need a Mapbox **access token** to use any of Mapbox's tools, APIs, or SDKs. Mapbox uses access tokens to associate your account with your requests to Mapbox API resources. You can find all your access tokens, create new ones, or delete existing ones on your [Access tokens page](https://console.mapbox.com/account/access-tokens/)
. You can also use the [Mapbox Tokens API](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/accounts/tokens/)
 to programmatically create, update, and delete access tokens.

Access tokens have _token scopes_ that grant access to different actions. Token scopes define which Mapbox APIs can be accessed by a token, as well as which methods can be used to access those APIs. Access tokens have either [public](https://docs.mapbox.com/accounts/guides/tokens/#public-scopes)
 or [secret](https://docs.mapbox.com/accounts/guides/tokens/#secret-scopes)
 scopes.

Your account will always have at least one public access token. This token is your [_default public token_](https://docs.mapbox.com/accounts/guides/tokens/#default-public-access-token)
. When you're logged into your account, this token will automatically be embedded in example code on Mapbox.com.

Here's what an access token looks like in a Mapbox API request (specifically, the [Geocoding API](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/search/geocoding/)
):

    https://api.mapbox.com/search/geocode/v6/forward?q=Los%20Angeles&access_token=YOUR_MAPBOX_ACCESS_TOKEN

**Related resources:**

*   [How do I create an API access token?](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/access-tokens/)
    
*   [List of public and private token scopes](https://docs.mapbox.com/accounts/guides/tokens/#scopes)
    
*   [Default public access token documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/accounts/guides/tokens/#default-public-access-token)
    
*   [Tokens API documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/accounts/#tokens)
    

Was this page helpful?YesNo

# Map Developer Journey | Help | Mapbox

[Skip to main content](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/map-developer-journey/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

Map Developer Journey
=====================

Understand the developer journey of adding a map to your application.

[Get started](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/map-developer-journey/)

![](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/img/dev-journey-map/arrow.svg)

[Add map](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/map-developer-journey/start-mapping/)

![](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/img/dev-journey-map/arrow.svg)

[Add data](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/map-developer-journey/data-guide/)

![](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/img/dev-journey-map/arrow.svg)

[Configure interactions](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/map-developer-journey/configure-interactions/)

![](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/img/dev-journey-map/arrow.svg)

[Deploy](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/map-developer-journey/post-launch-checklist/)

To get started, you need to sign up for a Mapbox account, which will automatically create an access token for you to use. This access token is a unique identifier that allows you to authenticate your requests to the Mapbox APIs and SDKs.

[Create a free account](https://console.mapbox.com/)

Once you have an access token, go to [Add a map](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/map-developer-journey/start-mapping/)
.

Was this page helpful?YesNo

---

# dataset ID | Help | Mapbox

[Skip to main content](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/glossary/dataset-id/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

A **dataset ID** is a [dataset's](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/glossary/dataset/)
 unique identifier. Any time you create a dataset with the [Mapbox Studio](https://docs.mapbox.com/studio-manual/)
 dataset editor, it generates a dataset ID. You can use a dataset's ID to make requests related to the dataset using the [Mapbox Datasets API](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/maps/datasets/)
.

**Related resources:**

*   [Mapbox Studio dataset editor](https://console.mapbox.com/studio/datasets)
    
*   [Datasets API documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/maps/datasets/)
    

Was this page helpful?YesNo

---

# Post-launch checklist | Help | Mapbox

[Skip to main content](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/map-developer-journey/post-launch-checklist/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

Post-launch checklist
=====================

A short list of items to review after publishing your map.

[Get started](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/map-developer-journey/)

![](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/img/dev-journey-map/arrow.svg)

[Add map](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/map-developer-journey/start-mapping/)

![](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/img/dev-journey-map/arrow.svg)

[Add data](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/map-developer-journey/data-guide/)

![](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/img/dev-journey-map/arrow.svg)

[Configure interactions](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/map-developer-journey/configure-interactions/)

![](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/img/dev-journey-map/arrow.svg)

[Deploy](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/map-developer-journey/post-launch-checklist/)

As you iterate on your map, here are some key resources and actions you might consider.

Update your map's data[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/map-developer-journey/post-launch-checklist/#update-your-maps-data "Direct link to Update your map's data")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

If the underlying source data of your map changes, there are several ways for you to update your map:

*   **Externally Hosted Data:** If your data is hosted externally and loaded at runtime, update the external source or replace the source link if it has changed.
*   **Studio Uploads:** For data originally uploaded in Studio, open the style and update the layer's source with the new data.
*   **Using APIs directly:** If you used the Uploads API or Mapbox Tiling Service:
    *   Upload a new tileset and update the tileset ID in your Studio style or client-side code.
    *   Replace your existing tileset. See [Uploads API](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/maps/uploads/#create-an-upload)
         and [MTS](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/maps/mapbox-tiling-service/#replace-a-tileset-source)
         for detailed instructions.

Note

Tileset changes take around 15 minutes to reflect on your maps. Learn more about [API caching](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/dive-deeper/api-caching/)
.

Before publishing your changes, inspect your map to verify that no broken references occur due to data changes.

Update your map's style[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/map-developer-journey/post-launch-checklist/#update-your-maps-style "Direct link to Update your map's style")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

You can always update the visual look and feel of your map:

*   **In Studio:** Edit the style directly. Changes are saved as drafts and won't deploy to your production apps until you publish them.
*   **Client-side code:** For custom layers, update the paint properties. For the Mapbox Standard Style, change the basemap's [configuration properties](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/guides/migrate-to-v3/#explore-new-features)
    .

Note

Style changes take around 15 minutes to reflect on your maps. Learn more about [API caching](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/dive-deeper/api-caching/)
.

Update your SDK[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/map-developer-journey/post-launch-checklist/#update-your-sdk "Direct link to Update your SDK")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

It's vital to keep your SDK up-to-date for optimal performance and security of your web or mobile app, and to take advantage of our latest features. We release new versions of our SDKs approximately every six weeks. You can track these updates and view detailed changelogs for each platform:

*   **Android SDK**: Follow [the changelog](https://github.com/mapbox/mapbox-maps-android/blob/main/CHANGELOG.md)
     for Android updates.
*   **iOS SDK**: Follow [the changelog](https://github.com/mapbox/mapbox-maps-ios/blob/main/CHANGELOG.md)
     for iOS updates.
*   **GL JS**: Stay informed on new [GL JS releases](https://github.com/mapbox/mapbox-gl-js/releases)
    .

Monitor your map's usage[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/map-developer-journey/post-launch-checklist/#monitor-your-maps-usage "Direct link to Monitor your map's usage")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Stay informed about your map's performance and billing:

*   **Statistics:** Visit your account's [Statistics page](https://console.mapbox.com/account/statistics/)
     for API usage data.
*   **Invoices:** Check your [Invoice page](https://console.mapbox.com/account/invoices/)
     view your upcoming and past invoices.

If you have any questions about your implementation, pricing, or other ways you can work with our platform:

*   Post your question to our [Discord community](https://discord.gg/uMpcC5RmJh)
    
*   View our [support plans](https://www.mapbox.com/support#support-plans)
    
*   Contact our [Sales team](https://www.mapbox.com/contact/sales)
    

Was this page helpful?YesNo

---

# feature querying | Help | Mapbox

[Skip to main content](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/glossary/feature-querying/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

Feature querying is the process by which [features](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/glossary/features/)
 in a tileset are identified at a specific location. For example, if you have a map with a [layer](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/glossary/layer/)
 representing city parks, you can use Mapbox tools to see if a park feature exists at a specific [coordinate](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/glossary/lat-lon/)
.

Feature querying powers many advanced map visualizations that allow users to select specific points to discover the data lying underneath. Feature querying can be performed on web maps using [Mapbox GL JS](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/)
, the [Mapbox Maps SDK for iOS](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/maps/)
, or the [Mapbox Maps SDK for Android](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/maps/)
.

**Related resources:**

*   [Mapbox GL JS `queryRenderedFeatures` documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/api/#map#queryrenderedfeatures)
    
*   [Mapbox Maps SDK for Android `queryRenderedFeatures` documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/maps/api/latest/mapbox-maps-android/com.mapbox.maps.plugin.delegates/-map-feature-query-delegate/query-rendered-features.html)
    
*   [Mapbox Maps SDK for iOS `queryRenderedFeatures` documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/maps/api/latest/documentation/mapboxmaps/mapboxmap/mapfeaturequeryable-implementations/)
    

Was this page helpful?YesNo

---

# font stack | Help | Mapbox

[Skip to main content](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/glossary/font-stack/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

A font stack is an ordered list of fonts that contains a primary font and an optional fallback font or fonts. In this example, `"Open Sans Regular"` is the primary font and `"Arial Unicode MS Regular"` is the fallback font:

    ["Open Sans Regular", "Arial Unicode MS Regular"]

The font stack is defined within a map's symbol [layer](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/glossary/layer/)
. The font stack defines the order in which the renderer should try to find the characters used within the layer. If the renderer cannot find a character within the first font, then it will try to use the next one. It will repeat this process until it can find a font that has that character.

A [style object](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/maps/#the-style-object)
 itself does not have a main font stack. Rather, individual layers within the style object have their own font stacks that are defined within the [`text-font`](https://docs.mapbox.com/style-spec/reference/layers/#layout-symbol-text-font)
 property of a symbol layer, as detailed in the [Mapbox Style Specification](https://docs.mapbox.com/style-spec/)
.

**Related resources:**

*   Troubleshooting guide: [Manage your fontstacks](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/dive-deeper/manage-fontstacks/)
    
*   [Mapbox Studio symbol layer documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/studio-manual/reference/styles/#symbol-layer)
    
*   [Mapbox GL JS `text-font` documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/style-spec/reference/layers/#layout-symbol-text-font)

# feature | Help | Mapbox

[Skip to main content](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/glossary/features/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

A geospatial **feature** is an individual (or in some cases, a group of) [points](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/glossary/point/)
, [lines](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/glossary/line/)
, or [polygons](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/glossary/polygon/)
 in a [dataset](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/glossary/dataset/)
 or in a [tileset](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/glossary/tileset/)
. These points, lines, and polygons represent an entity in the real world, such as a landmark, a road, or a park.

Each feature is defined by its geometry and properties:

*   **Geometry:** The shape and the location of each feature.
*   **Properties:** This can contain any JSON object. Some examples of common properties, often known as metadata, include `title` and `description`.

**Related resources:**

*   [Feature querying](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/glossary/feature-querying/)
    
*   Getting started: [Create new data](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/dive-deeper/creating-data/)
    

Was this page helpful?YesNo

---

# annotation | Help | Mapbox

[Skip to main content](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/glossary/annotation/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

An **annotation** is a defined location on a map in the [Mapbox Maps SDK for iOS](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/maps/)
 that is represented by a custom [`UIView`](https://developer.apple.com/documentation/uikit/uiview)
 or [`UIImage`](https://developer.apple.com/documentation/uikit/uiimage)
.

You can use annotations to represent point data, such as restaurants in a city, bus stops along a route, or earthquake locations. There are multiple ways to add annotations to your maps in the Mapbox Maps SDK for iOS. These methods are outlined in the [Mapbox Maps SDK for iOS documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/maps/guides/annotations/)
.

**Related resources:**

*   [Mapbox Maps SDK for iOS documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/maps/guides/)
    
*   [Mapbox Maps SDK for iOS Adding points to a map guide](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/maps/guides/annotations/)
    

Was this page helpful?YesNo

---

# bounding box | Help | Mapbox

[Skip to main content](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/glossary/bounding-box/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

A **bounding box** is a way to display a rectangular area of a map. It is expressed as a set of coordinate pairs, with the first coordinate pair referring to the southwestern corner of the box (the minimum [longitude and latitude](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/glossary/lat-lon/)
) and the second referring to the northeastern corner of the box (the maximum longitude and latitude).

Below is a visualization of a bounding box created around the state of Vermont from our [Location Helper](https://labs.mapbox.com/location-helper)
 tool.

Bounding boxes are useful for describing the spatial limits of geographic features. For example, a you could create a bounding box to describe the limits of an administrative boundary polygon, or describe the limits of a set of point features.

Once a bounding box is defined, you can use it to set the initial view of a map to make sure that the area within the bounding box is fully visible regardless of aspect ratio. You can also use a bounding box to control the view of an existing map programmatically.

### Mapbox GL JS[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/glossary/bounding-box/#mapbox-gl-js "Direct link to Mapbox GL JS")

This [Mapbox GL JS](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/)
 snippet creates a bounding box using the [`LngLatBounds` method](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/api/geography/#lnglatbounds)
 and then calls [`fitBounds`](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/api/map/#map#fitbounds)
 to move the map's camera, ensuring that the bounding box is fully in view.

    const southWest = new mapboxgl.LngLat(-73.9876, 40.7661);const northEast = new mapboxgl.LngLat(-73.9397, 40.8002);const boundingBox = new mapboxgl.LngLatBounds(southWest, northEast);map.fitBounds(boundingBox);

[EXAMPLE\
\
Fit map to a bounding box\
\
This GL JS example shows you how to use `fitBounds` to zoom & pan to a bounding box.](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/example/fitbounds/)
[EXAMPLE\
\
Initialize a map with a bounding box\
\
This GL JS example demonstrates how a bounding box can be used to center a map regardless of the map's aspect ratio.](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/example/initialize-with-bounding-box/)

### Mobile SDKs[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/glossary/bounding-box/#mobile-sdks "Direct link to Mobile SDKs")

Our mobile SDK's for [iOS](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/maps/guides/)
, [Android](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/maps/guides/)
 & [Flutter](https://docs.mapbox.com/flutter/maps/guides/)
 use the `CoordinateBounds` class to create a bounding box and use the `camera` and `setCamera` methods of the `mapboxMap` object to set the camera view to the bounding box.

iOS

Android

Flutter

iOS

Android

Flutter

    let boundingBox = CoordinateBounds(southwest: CLLocationCoordinate2D(latitude: -0.90204, longitude: -158.63249),northeast: CLLocationCoordinate2D(latitude: 66.77350, longitude: 10.68889))// Center the camera on the boundslet camera = mapView.mapboxMap.camera(for: boundingBox, padding: .zero, bearing: 0, pitch: 0)

iOS

Android

Flutter

    let boundingBox = CoordinateBounds(Point.fromLngLat(-158.63249 -0.90204),Point.fromLngLat(10.68889, 66.77350),false)// Define camera boundsprivate val cameraBoundsOptions = CameraBoundsOptions.Builder().bounds(boundingBox).minZoom(10.0).build()// Fit camera to the bounding boxmapboxMap.setCamera(cameraBoundsOptions)

iOS

Android

Flutter

    let boundingBox = CoordinateBounds(southwest: Point( coordinates: Position(42.73834, -73.45338)),northeast: Point( coordinates: Position(45.02491,  -71.48691)),infiniteBounds: false)// Center the camera on the bounding boxlet camera = mapboxMap.cameraForCoordinateBounds(boundingBox);mapboxMap.setCamera(camera);

Note

A bounding box's edges are not explicitly parallel on all map [projections](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/guides/projections/)
 as they follow longitude & latitude. In this image you can see that the bounding box edges curve to follow longitude/latitude in the [`globe`](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/guides/projections/#globe)
 projection.

**Related resources:**

Any Mapbox library that creates maps or initiates geocoding requests has a class or object for using bounding boxes in your code:

*   [Mapbox Geocoding API `bbox` parameter documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/search/geocoding/#forward-geocoding-with-search-text-input)
    
*   [Mapbox GL JS `LngLatBounds` documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/api/geography/#lnglatbounds)
    
*   [Mapbox Maps SDK for iOS `CoordinateBounds` documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/maps/api/latest/documentation/mapboxmaps/coordinatebounds/)
    
*   [Mapbox Maps SDK for Android `CoordinateBounds` class documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/maps/api/latest/mapbox-maps-android/com.mapbox.maps/-coordinate-bounds/)
    
*   [Mapbox Java SDK `MapboxGeocoding.Builder.bbox()` method documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/java/api/libjava-services/7.0.0/com/mapbox/api/geocoding/v5/MapboxGeocoding.Builder.html#bbox-java.lang.String-)
    
*   [Mapbox Search SDK for iOS `BoundingBox` documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/search/api/core/2.9.0-rc.2/Structs/BoundingBox.html)
    
*   [Turf.js `turf.bbox` documentation](https://turfjs.org/docs/api/bbox)
    
*   [Location helper - interact with a map and get bounding box values to use in your code.](https://labs.mapbox.com/location-helper)
    

Was this page helpful?YesNo

# basemap | Help | Mapbox

[Skip to main content](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/glossary/basemap/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

A map style's **basemap** generally refers to the background map features, such as roads, buildings, labels, parks, points of interest, and bodies of water. Developers building with Mapbox usually add additional data layers on top of the basemap to create custom interactive maps for their applications.

Mapbox provides two **map styles** that serve as basemaps:

1.  [Mapbox Standard](https://docs.mapbox.com/map-styles/standard/guides/)
    , which is a general-purpose street map style suitable for a wide range of applications.
2.  [Mapbox Standard Satellite](https://docs.mapbox.com/map-styles/standard/guides/#mapbox-standard-satellite)
    , which provides a satellite imagery layer with minimal vector-based labels and features, ideal for applications that require a more realistic representation of the Earth's surface.

Both styles have configuration options that allow developers to customize the appearance of the basemap, such as adjusting colors, or setting the visibility of certain features.

There are two main approaches to using these basemaps in your application:

1.  You can instantiate a new map in your application using either of these styles, adding your layers at runtime.
2.  You can create a custom style in [Mapbox Studio](https://console.mapbox.com/)
     based on either of these styles, adding your layers and customization in the style editor, then use that custom style in your application.

In both scenarios, Mapbox Standard or Mapbox Standard Satellite are included as an [`import`](https://docs.mapbox.com/style-spec/reference/imports/)
 in the resulting map style, with user-defined layers integrated at various points in the layer stack using [slots](https://docs.mapbox.com/style-spec/reference/slots/)
.

Or you can create your own basemap style from scratch in Mapbox Studio by adding and styling various map features according to your design requirements. This approach provides maximum flexibility but requires more effort to set up.

Was this page helpful?YesNo

---

# bearing | Help | Mapbox

[Skip to main content](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/glossary/bearing/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

A **bearing** is the direction you’re facing, measured clockwise as an angle from true north on a compass. This can also be called a _heading_. In this system, north is `0°`, east is `90°`, south is `180°`, and west is `270°`. When you are viewing a Mapbox map, the bearing rotates the map around its center the specified number of degrees.

The bearing of your map view can be adjusted in many Mapbox products, including [Mapbox Studio](https://docs.mapbox.com/studio-manual/)
, [Mapbox GL JS](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/)
, the Mapbox Maps SDKs for [Android](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/maps/)
 and [iOS](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/maps/)
, and the [Static Images API](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/maps/static-images/)
.

The bearing of a vehicle as it starts from a waypoint can also be set in the [Directions API](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/navigation/directions/)
 and the [Optimization API](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/navigation/optimization-v1/)
 using the optional `bearings` parameter, and in the [Mapbox Navigation SDKs](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/navigation/)
 for iOS and Android.

**Related resources:**

_Mapbox GL JS:_

*   [Mapbox GL JS map options documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/api/#map)
    

_Android:_

*   [Mapbox Maps SDK for Android bearing documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/maps/guides/camera-and-animation/camera/)
    
*   [Mapbox Navigation for Android route generation documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/navigation/v2/guides/turn-by-turn-navigation/route-generation/)
    

_iOS:_

*   [Mapbox Maps SDK for iOS bearing documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/maps/guides/camera-and-animation/camera/)
    
*   [Mapbox Navigation for iOS route generation documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/navigation/guides/turn-by-turn-navigation/route-generation/#request-a-route-in-a-specific-direction)
    

_Web services APIs:_

*   [Static Images API documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/maps/#static-images)
    
*   [Directions API documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/navigation/#retrieve-directions)
    
*   [Optimization API documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/navigation/#retrieve-an-optimization)
    

_Tools_

*   [Location helper - interact with a map and get bearing values to use in your code](https://labs.mapbox.com/location-helper/)

# compute unit (CU) | Help | Mapbox

[Skip to main content](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/glossary/compute-unit/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

Pricing

For more information on how the [Mapbox Tiling Service (MTS)](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-tiling-service/guides)
 and the [Uploads API](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/maps/uploads)
 are priced, see the [tilesets pricing guide](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-tiling-service/guides/pricing)
.

Compute units (also called CUs) represent the processing resources a tileset processing job consumes on Mapbox infrastructure. Tilesets processing uses CUs for billing and will appear on your invoice as **Tilesets Processing CU**. This applies to tileset processing jobs from both the [Mapbox Tiling Service (MTS)](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-tiling-service/guides/)
 and the [Uploads API](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/maps/uploads/)
.

For more information on CUs, see the [tilesets pricing guide](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-tiling-service/guides/pricing/#compute-units-cus)
.

* * *

**Related resources:**

*   [Mapbox Tiling Service pricing](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-tiling-service/guides/pricing/)
    
*   [Uploads API pricing](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/maps/uploads/#pricing)

Add a map
=========

Get up and running with your first map in a few minutes.

[Get started](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/map-developer-journey/)

The next step in this developer journey is to install the map rendering library or SDK for your platform and render the default map style. Our **Get Started** guides include step-by-step instructions for installing Mapbox map rendering on web, iOS, and Android. Each guide includes "Hello, world!" example code to get a map up and running using the versatile Mapbox Standard Style, centered on a location of your choice.

Follow along with the Get Started guide for your platform to get an interactive Mapbox map loaded into your app.

.

# CSV | Help | Mapbox

[Skip to main content](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/glossary/csv/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

The **CSV** (comma-separated values) format is common for table data, like the kind you would use in Excel or other spreadsheet programs. CSV files are not mappable unless they contain geographic information including [latitude and longitude](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/glossary/lat-lon/)
. You can upload CSVs to Mapbox using [Mapbox Studio](https://docs.mapbox.com/studio-manual/)
 or the [Uploads API](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/maps/uploads/)
.

When you upload CSV files, follow these guidelines:

*   CSV files must contain coordinates (latitude and longitude) to be uploaded to Mapbox Studio.
*   The order of the columns that contain latitude and longitude values does not matter, as long as the values contained in the columns are correctly formatted coordinates and have [valid column headers](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/troubleshooting/csv-upload-errors/#file-format)
    .
*   CSV files are for point data only.
*   CSV files must be in [UTF-8](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/UTF-8)
     encoding.
*   Data imported via CSV will be encoded as a `string` type in the [Mapbox Studio dataset editor](https://console.mapbox.com/studio/datasets)
    . If you want to code any of your imported data as numbers, you can edit the fields individually in the dataset editor or upload your data in [GeoJSON format](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/glossary/geojson/)
    .

**Related resources:**

*   [Troubleshooting CSV files](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/troubleshooting/csv-upload-errors/)
    
*   Studio Manual [geospatial data page](https://docs.mapbox.com/studio-manual/overview/geospatial-data/)
    : transfer limits for uploading CSV files as tilesets or datasets
*   Example CSV file with geographic information:

[arrow-downDownload sample CSV](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/data/airports.csv)

Was this page helpful?YesNo

---

# filter | Help | Mapbox

[Skip to main content](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/glossary/filter/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

In the Mapbox Style Specification, a **filter** is a property at the [layer level](https://docs.mapbox.com/style-spec/reference/layers/#filter)
 that determines which features should be rendered in a style [layer](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/glossary/layer/)
. Filters are written as [expressions](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/glossary/expression/)
, which give you fine-grained control over which features to include: the style layer only displays the features that match the filter condition that you define. Filters are called **predicates** in iOS and macOS.

This code from the [Filter symbols by text input](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/example/filter-markers-by-input/)
 example includes a filter expression that singles out all features where `"icon"` is equal to `symbol` (a variable that is set to the selected feature's icon property):

    map.addLayer({  id: layerID,  type: 'symbol',  source: 'places',  layout: {    'icon-image': symbol + '-15',    'icon-allow-overlap': true  },  filter: ['==', 'icon', symbol]});

Older versions of the Mapbox Style Specification used a [property-based filter syntax](https://docs.mapbox.com/style-spec/reference/other/#other-filter)
. While that older syntax still works, it will ultimately be deprecated and replaced by filter expressions.

**Related resources:**

*   [Filter documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/style-spec/reference/layers/#filter)
    
*   [Expression documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/style-spec/reference/expressions/)
    
*   Example: [Highlight features containing similar data](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/example/query-similar-features/)
    
*   Example: [Filter symbols by text input](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/example/filter-markers-by-input/)
    
*   Troubleshooting: [Optimize expressions](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/troubleshooting/mapbox-gl-js-performance/#optimize-expressions)
    

Was this page helpful?YesNo
