# data-driven styling | Help | Mapbox

[Skip to main content](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/glossary/data-driven-styling/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

**Data-driven styling** allows you to style spatial data based on its properties. For example, with data-driven styling you can change the radius of a circle at an intersection based on the number of pedestrians crossing the intersection, change the color of a state polygon based on the population of each state, or use conditional logic to create bilingual labels. Data-driven styling is available in the following products:

*   [Mapbox Studio](https://docs.mapbox.com/studio-manual/)
    
*   [Mapbox GL JS](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/)
    
*   [Mapbox Maps SDK for iOS](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/maps/)
    
*   [Mapbox Maps SDK for Android](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/maps/)
    

**Related resources:**

*   Getting started: [Data-driven styles](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/dive-deeper/map-design/#data-driven-styles)
    

_Mapbox Maps SDK for iOS_

*   [Expressions documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/maps/guides/styles/use-expressions/)
    

_Mapbox Maps SDK for Android_

*   [Expressions documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/maps/guides/styles/use-expressions/)
    

_Mapbox GL JS_

*   Example: [Style circles with a data-driven property](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/example/data-driven-circle-colors/)
    
*   Tutorial: [Get started with Mapbox GL JS expressions](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/tutorials/mapbox-gl-js-expressions/)

# Add your data | Help | Mapbox

[Skip to main content](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/map-developer-journey/data-guide/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

Add your data
=============

Learn about the different ways you can overlay your data on a map.

[Get started](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/map-developer-journey/)

![](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/img/dev-journey-map/arrow.svg)

[Add map](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/map-developer-journey/start-mapping/)

![](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/img/dev-journey-map/arrow.svg)

[Add data](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/map-developer-journey/data-guide/)

![](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/img/dev-journey-map/arrow.svg)

[Configure interactions](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/map-developer-journey/configure-interactions/)

![](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/img/dev-journey-map/arrow.svg)

[Deploy](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/map-developer-journey/post-launch-checklist/)

This guide focuses on the process of taking geographic data that _already exists_ and transforming it into a format that can be used on a web or mobile map. This could be data that you downloaded from an open data portal, data that you drew in a GIS application, aerial imagery taken by a drone, or something else.

How do I add my data to a map?[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/map-developer-journey/data-guide/#how-do-i-add-my-data-to-a-map "Direct link to How do I add my data to a map?")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Below are four options to add your custom data to a map. If your data has to be hosted externally, skip to [Option 4](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/map-developer-journey/data-guide/#option-4-load-your-data-at-runtime)
.

### **Option 1: Upload data with Mapbox Studio**[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/map-developer-journey/data-guide/#option-1-upload-data-with-mapbox-studio "Direct link to option-1-upload-data-with-mapbox-studio")

Best for styling your data on a map using a no-code style editor and to quickly publish map changes to your live app with minimal changes to client-side code.

This option converts your source data into a tileset that is hosted on the Mapbox platform. [Tilesets](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/glossary/tileset/)
 are a styleable format optimized for high-performant rendering across all platforms.

**Supported formats:** MBTiles, KML, GPX, GeoJSON, Shapefile (zipped), CSV file, GeoTIFF

**Limitations:** 300 MB filesize limit.

[TUTORIAL\
\
Data in Mapbox Studio\
\
Understand how to prepare and upload geospatial data in Mapbox Studio.](https://docs.mapbox.com/studio-manual/guides/geospatial-data/)

### **Option 2: Use the Mapbox Tiling Service (MTS)**[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/map-developer-journey/data-guide/#option-2-use-the-mapbox-tiling-service-mts "Direct link to option-2-use-the-mapbox-tiling-service-mts")

Best for jobs of any size and update frequency where you want more control over how your source data is transformed and tiled. MTS is also a good option if you want to programmatically create tilesets.

**Supported formats:** Line-delimited GeoJSON, GeoJSON (Tilesets CLI). Note that there are several publicly available tools (like [mapshaper](https://mapshaper.org/)
) that convert formats like Shapefiles, KML, and CSV to GeoJSON.

**Limitations:** Each uploaded file must be below 20 GB. The maximum combined total size of all files that compose a tileset source is 50 GB. Read more about [MTS restrictions and limits](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/maps/mapbox-tiling-service/#mapbox-tiling-service-restrictions-and-limits)
.

[TUTORIAL\
\
Get started with the Mapbox Tiling Service (MTS)\
\
Create and publish a custom tileset using sample data.](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/tutorials/get-started-mts-and-tilesets-cli/)

### **Option 3: Use the Uploads API**[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/map-developer-journey/data-guide/#option-3-use-the-uploads-api "Direct link to option-3-use-the-uploads-api")

If you want to programmatically create a tileset and cannot use MTS (because GeoJSON is not a suitable format for your data or your data is in a raster format), use the Uploads API.

**Supported formats:** MBTiles, KML, GPX, GeoJSON, Shapefile (zipped), CSV file, GeoTIFF

Get started with the [Uploads API](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/maps/uploads/)
.

### **Option 4: Load your data at runtime**[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/map-developer-journey/data-guide/#option-4-load-your-data-at-runtime "Direct link to option-4-load-your-data-at-runtime")

If your data lives in a third-party data-source, you can load it on the client during runtime using the `addSource` method. The examples below show how you can load an externally-hosted GeoJSON on web or mobile.

Web

iOS

Android

Web

iOS

Android

    map.on('load', () => {map.addSource('earthquakes', {type: 'geojson',// Use a URL for the value for the data property.data: 'https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/assets/earthquakes.geojson'});    map.addLayer({        'id': 'earthquakes-layer',        'type': 'circle',        'source': 'earthquakes',        'paint': {            'circle-radius': 4,            'circle-stroke-width': 2,            'circle-color': 'red',            'circle-stroke-color': 'white'        }    });});

Web

iOS

Android

    let earthquakeSource = GeoJSONSource(id: "earthquakes")earthquakeSource.data = .url("https://www.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/assets/earthquakes.geojson")earthquakeSource.generateId = true        do {  try mapView.mapboxMap.addSource(earthquakeSource)} catch {  print("Ran into an error adding a source: (error)")}

Web

iOS

Android

    style.addSource(geoJsonSource("earthquakes") {url("https://www.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/assets/earthquakes.geojson")})

Dive even deeper with these code examples:

| Web | iOS | Android |
| --- | --- | --- |
| *   [Load data from an external GeoJSON file](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/example/external-geojson/)<br>    <br>*   [Add a third party vector tile source](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/example/third-party/)<br>    <br>*   [Add an external Web Map Service layer to your map](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/example/wms/) | *   [Load data from an external GeoJSON file](https://github.com/mapbox/mapbox-maps-ios/blob/main/Sources/Examples/All%20Examples/HeatmapLayerGlobeExample.swift)<br>    <br>*   [Add a third party vector tile source](https://github.com/mapbox/mapbox-maps-ios/blob/main/Sources/Examples/All%20Examples/ExternalVectorSourceExample.swift)<br>    <br>*   [Add a third party raster tile source](https://github.com/mapbox/mapbox-maps-ios/blob/main/Sources/Examples/All%20Examples/RasterTileSourceExample.swift) | *   [Load data from an external GeoJSON file](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/maps/examples/heatmap-layer/)<br>    <br>*   [Add a third-party vector tile source](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/maps/examples/external-vector-source/)<br>    <br>*   [Add a third-party raster tile source](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/maps/examples/tilejson/) |

How do I style my data?[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/map-developer-journey/data-guide/#how-do-i-style-my-data "Direct link to How do I style my data?")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

You can either style your data at runtime or in Studio.

*   **Runtime Styling**: This option is ideal for event-based styling (for example, if a user hovers over data and it changes color). This option is also good if you want styling variants but don't want to support multiple styles in Studio.
*   **Styling in Studio**: This option is ideal if you want to style your data without having to alter client-side code or redeploy your application. It also keeps all your map styling in one place.

### **Style your data dynamically at runtime**[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/map-developer-journey/data-guide/#style-your-data-dynamically-at-runtime "Direct link to style-your-data-dynamically-at-runtime")

After adding your data to a new layer, style it using one of the available [paint properties](https://docs.mapbox.com/style-spec/reference/layers/#fill)
 for that layer type.

The example snippet below show how you can style custom line data on web with Mapbox GL JS:

    map.addLayer({  'id': 'route',  'type': 'line',  'source': 'route',  'layout': {    'line-join': 'round',    'line-cap': 'round'  },  'paint': {    'line-color': '#888',    'line-width': 8  }});

### **Style your map in Studio**[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/map-developer-journey/data-guide/#style-your-map-in-studio "Direct link to style-your-map-in-studio")

Any data uploaded to our platform is accessible from within Studio for quick styling. Changes you publish in Studio will be reflected in your mobile and web applications with no additional code changes needed.

Learn more about [styling in Studio](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/tutorials/aa-standard-in-studio/?tut-step=5)
.

How much will it cost?[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/map-developer-journey/data-guide/#how-much-will-it-cost "Direct link to How much will it cost?")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Tilesets uploaded in Mapbox Studio are free, and do not incur processing or hosting charges. Tilesets uploaded using the Mapbox Tiling Service (MTS) or the Uploads API are billed by [tileset hosting and tileset processing](https://www.mapbox.com/pricing/#processing10m)
.

Was this page helpful?YesNo

---

# camera | Help | Mapbox

[Skip to main content](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/glossary/camera/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

The **camera** is the map's field of view in a Mapbox map. The camera's viewport is determined by several factors: center, zoom level, pitch, and bearing.

Center[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/glossary/camera/#center "Direct link to Center")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The camera's **center** is the [longitude and latitude](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/glossary/lat-lon/)
 at which the camera is pointed.

Zoom level[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/glossary/camera/#zoom-level "Direct link to Zoom level")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The camera's **zoom level** is a number that determines how close the viewport is to the surface of the map. At zoom level 0, the viewport shows continents and oceans. A middle value of 11 shows city-level details, and at a higher zoom level the map begins to show buildings and points of interest. Learn more in the [zoom level](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/glossary/zoom-level/)
 glossary page.

Pitch[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/glossary/camera/#pitch "Direct link to Pitch")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The camera's **pitch** is the visual tilt of the map, which is determined by the angle towards the horizon measured in degrees. A pitch of 0° results in a two-dimensional map, as if your line of sight forms a perpendicular angle with the earth's surface, while a greater value like 60° looks ahead towards the horizon.

Bearing[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/glossary/camera/#bearing "Direct link to Bearing")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The camera's **bearing** is the visual rotation of the map, measured in degrees counter-clockwise from north, with a range between 0° and 360°. A bearing of 0° orients the map so that north is "up", 90° orients the map so that east is "up", and so on. Learn more in the [bearing](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/glossary/bearing/)
 glossary page.

* * *

**Related resources:**

*   [Mapbox GL JS `CameraOptions` documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/api/#cameraoptions)
    
*   [Mapbox Maps SDK for iOS camera documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/maps/guides/camera-and-animation/camera/)
    
*   [Mapbox Maps SDK for Android camera documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/maps/guides/camera-and-animation/camera/)
    
*   [Location Helper - Experiment with camera pitch, bearing, tilt, and zoom and get values to use in your code](https://labs.mapbox.com/location-helper/#3/40.78/-73.97)
    

Was this page helpful?YesNo

# Search Products Overview | Help | Mapbox

[Skip to main content](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/search/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

Mapbox provides a full suite of products and services that enable you to add location-based search to your applications or data projects. These products include front-end search libraries and SDKs for web and mobile applications, as well as web services APIs for integration into any environment.

In this guide, you'll learn about common search UX patterns and the Mapbox search products and services that support them. You'll also find links to documentation, code examples, and API playgrounds to help you get started with location-based search.

Search UX Patterns[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/search/#search-ux-patterns "Direct link to Search UX Patterns")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Below you can find common user experience patterns related to location-based search that you can implement in your applications using Mapbox products and services.

### Interactive Text Search[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/search/#interactive-text-search "Direct link to Interactive Text Search")

Interactive text search combined with an interactive map is the most common location search pattern for web and mobile applications. A floating search box allows users to search for points of interest, locations, and addresses. When a search result is selected, the map flies to its location and displays a marker.

Type in the search box to test it out. Results are biased towards New York City.

The demo integrates [Mapbox Search JS](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-search-js/)
 (powered by the [Search Box API](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/search/search-box/)
) with the [Mapbox GL JS](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/)
 library for rendering interactive maps.

### Category Search[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/search/#category-search "Direct link to Category Search")

Category search allows users to search for points of interest (POIs) by category, such as restaurants, hotels, or parks. These are usually connected to the map to search for category matches in the current viewport or near the user's location.

Click a category to see the search results. Results are biased to the center of the map. You can try moving to a different location and searching again.

### Coordinate Search (reverse geocoding)[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/search/#coordinate-search-reverse-geocoding "Direct link to Coordinate Search (reverse geocoding)")

Coordinate search, also known as reverse geocoding, allows users to search for addresses or points of interest by entering coordinates. This is useful for applications that need to display location information based on a user's current location or a selected point on a map.

Drag the marker to a new location to request a Coordinate Search result for nearby points of interest (POIs). The result's `name` property will be displayed in the panel at the top left of the map.

### Address Autocomplete[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/search/#address-autocomplete "Direct link to Address Autocomplete")

Address Autocomplete is the auto-filling and validating of address forms in web and mobile apps (also referred to as Address Autofill or Address Form Fill). Address autocomplete is a common feature in e-commerce, delivery, and other applications that require users to enter addresses, and helps prevent errors and improve the user experience.

Try address autofill using a live example in the Mapbox Search JS documentation:

[EXAMPLE\
\
Address Autofill Example\
\
Try address autofill in a simulated online checkout experience.](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-search-js/example/autofill-checkout-js/)

To get started with address autocomplete, see the guide for your platform:

[GUIDE\
\
Address Autofill\
\
Learn about address autofill in Mapbox Search JS.](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-search-js/guides/autofill/)
[GUIDE\
\
Address Form Fill\
\
Learn about address form fill in an iOS application.](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/search/guides/address-formfill/)
[GUIDE\
\
Address Autofill\
\
Learn about address autofill in an Android application.](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/search/guides/address-autofill/)

Frontend search products[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/search/#frontend-search-products "Direct link to Frontend search products")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Mapbox provides front-end search products for web and mobile applications. These products interact with the Mapbox Search APIs behind the scenes and provide standard user interface components that make it easier to add search functionality to applications with minimal effort.

### Mapbox Search JS[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/search/#mapbox-search-js "Direct link to Mapbox Search JS")

[Mapbox Search JS](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-search-js/)
 is a JavaScript library that provides a search box for use in web applications. The package includes React components as well as web components and is applicable in any web development environment. The search box provides autocomplete suggestions as users type, and displays search results on a map.

To get started with Mapbox Search JS, see its [documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-search-js/)
 and [code examples](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-search-js/examples/)
.

[EXAMPLE\
\
Add Search Box to a Web Map\
\
View sample code for adding a search box to a web map using Mapbox Search JS.](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-search-js/example/add-search-box-to-map/)

### Search SDK for Android[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/search/#search-sdk-for-android "Direct link to Search SDK for Android")

The [Mapbox Search SDK for Android](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/search/)
 is a software development kit (SDK) that provides a search box for use in Android applications. The search box provides autocomplete suggestions as users type, and displays search results on a map.

To get started with the Maps SDK for Android, see its [documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/search/)
 and [code examples](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/search/examples/)
.

[EXAMPLE\
\
Basic search + Maps SDK integration\
\
Add search UI to a map in an Android application.](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/search/examples/maps-sdk-integration/)

### Search SDK for iOS[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/search/#search-sdk-for-ios "Direct link to Search SDK for iOS")

The [Search SDK for iOS](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/search/)
 is a software development kit (SDK) that provides a search box for use in iOS applications. The search box provides autocomplete suggestions as users type, and displays search results on a map.

To get started with the Maps SDK for iOS, see its [documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/search/)
 and [code examples](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/search/examples/)
.

[EXAMPLE\
\
Search UI SDK and Maps SDK integration\
\
Add a drop-in search UI to a Mapbox map in an iOS application.](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/search/examples/search-ui-and-maps-sdk/)

These front-end search products may also include "core" APIs, interfaces that allow you to interact with the search functionality programmatically and bring your own user interface components. See search wrappers section below.

### Frontend Search Product Pricing[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/search/#search-product-pricing "Direct link to Frontend Search Product Pricing")

Pricing for frontend search products is based on usage of the underlying search APIs. See the [Search API Pricing](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/search/#search-api-pricing)
 section below for more information.

Search APIs[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/search/#search-apis "Direct link to Search APIs")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Mapbox has two web services APIs for searching locations and addresses: the **Search Box API** and the **Geocoding API**. You can access these APIs from any environment that can make HTTP requests, such as web browsers, mobile applications, and server-side code.

The differences between the Search Box API and the Geocoding API are subtle, but important. The Search Box API includes points of interest (POIs) in its results, while the Geocoding API does not. The Geocoding API also supports both permanent and temporary geocoding requests, batch processing, and structured input.

| Feature | Search Box API | Geocoding API |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Includes Points of Interest (POIs) | check | close |
| Session-based billing | check | close |
| Permanent Storage Allowed\* | close | check |
| Batch Processing | close | check |
| Structured Input | close | check |

\* Permanent geocoding incurs higher costs than temporary geocoding. See the [Mapbox Pricing Page](https://www.mapbox.com/pricing#permanent-geocoding-api)
 for details.

### Search Box API[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/search/#search-box-api "Direct link to Search Box API")

The Search Box API supports **interactive search**, **category search**, **forward search,** and **reverse search**. Search Box API responses can include addresses, places, and points of interest (POIs). The typical use case for the Search Box API is to provide search suggestions as users type in a search box, and to retrieve detailed information about a selected search result.

| Search Type | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Interactive Search | Provides autocomplete search suggestions, intended for use as a user types in a search input. |
| Category Search | Searches for points of interest (POIs) by category. |
| Forward Search | Converts a full search query to a list of matching locations (intended for non-interactive search input) |
| Reverse Search | Converts coordinates to a list of matching locations. |

To get started with the Search Box API, see its [reference documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/search/search-box/)
 or explore its API Playgrounds:

[PLAYGROUND\
\
Search Box API Playground - Suggest and Retrieve\
\
Experiment with parameters and see response data for the `/suggest` and `/retrieve` endpoints of the Search Box API.](https://docs.mapbox.com/playground/search-box/)
[PLAYGROUND\
\
Search Box API Playground - Forward and Reverse\
\
Experiment with parameters and see response data for the `/forward` and `/reverse` endpoints of the Search Box API.](https://docs.mapbox.com/playground/search-box/)

### Geocoding API[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/search/#geocoding-api "Direct link to Geocoding API")

The Geocoding API supports non-interactive geocoding of addresses and reverse geocoding of coordinates. The typical use case for the Geocoding API is to enrich your data with location information (for example, batch geocoding to add point coordinates to a list of addresses), or to power other location-based services.

Note

The Geocoding API does not include points of interest (POIs) in its results. If you need to search for POIs, use the Search Box API instead.

To get started with the Geocoding API, see its [reference documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/search/geocoding/)
 or try its API Playground:

[PLAYGROUND\
\
Geocoding API Playground\
\
Experiment with request parameters for the Geocoding API using a graphical interface, and inspect the API response object.](https://docs.mapbox.com/playground/geocoding/)

### Address Autocomplete[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/search/#address-autocomplete-1 "Direct link to Address Autocomplete")

Since address autocomplete is a frontend-specific feature, it is intended to be accessed via the frontend search products listed above and is not available as a separate web API.

### Wrapping Libraries[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/search/#wrapping-libraries "Direct link to Wrapping Libraries")

You can use Mapbox Search APIs directly, but you can also use one of several wrapper libraries to integrate them into applications across platforms. These libraries provide a more convenient way to interact with the APIs and may include additional features or functionality.

*   **Mapbox Search JS** - Use [`Search JS Core`](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-search-js/guides/#search-js-core)
     to interact with both the Search Box API and Geocoding API in a **web or node.js environment**.
*   **Mapbox Search SDK for iOS** - Use the [`Search Core`](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/search/api/core/2.9.0-rc.2/)
     module for iOS to interact with the Search Box API and Geocoding API in an **iOS environment**.
*   **Mapbox Search SDK for Android** - Use the [`Search Core`](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/search/api/core/2.8.0-rc.1/)
     module for Android to interact with the Search Box API and Geocoding API in an **Android environment**.

### Other use cases[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/search/#other-use-cases "Direct link to Other use cases")

*   **Bulk Geocoding** - Convert a list of addresses to coordinates in a single HTTP POST request to the Geocoding API.
*   **Address Validation** - Validate user-entered addresses to clean up data and prevent errors.
*   **GPS Trace Lookup** - Use reverse geocoding to convert GPS coordinates from vehicles to human-readable addresses.
*   **Discover Administrative Boundaries** - Reverse geocode coordinates to find the city, state, and country where a user point is located.

### Search API Pricing[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/search/#search-api-pricing "Direct link to Search API Pricing")

Pricing for search APIs depends on the product and type of request.

The Search Box API's interactive search (using the suggest and retrieve endpoints) is session-based. A single user making multiple requests in a short period of time is considered a single session. The Search Box API's forward and reverse search endpoints are not session-based, so each request is billed separately.

All Geocoding API requests are billed separately, but temporary versus permanent geocoding requests are priced differently.

For full details, see the [Mapbox Pricing page](https://www.mapbox.com/pricing#search)
.

Was this page helpful?YesNo

---

# Navigation Products Overview | Help | Mapbox

[Skip to main content](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/navigation/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

Mapbox provides a full suite of products and services for adding navigation experiences and routing-related data to applications or data projects. These products include front-end navigation libraries and SDKs for web and mobile applications, as well as web services APIs for integration into any environment.

This guide covers common navigation UX patterns and use cases, and provides an overview of the Mapbox products and services that support them. Also included are links to documentation, code examples, and API playgrounds to help you get started with Mapbox navigation products.

Navigation UX Patterns[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/navigation/#navigation-ux-patterns "Direct link to Navigation UX Patterns")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The following are the most common UX patterns using Mapbox navigation products:

### Route Preview[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/navigation/#route-preview "Direct link to Route Preview")

Route preview is a common navigation pattern that shows a user the route they will take on a map before they start navigating. This pattern is often used in driving, walking, and cycling directions, and helps the user understand the route and make any necessary adjustments before starting navigation.

Route Previews are rendered using a response from the [Directions API](http://docs.mapbox.com/api/navigation/directions/)
.

### Interactive Navigation[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/navigation/#interactive-navigation "Direct link to Interactive Navigation")

Interactive navigation presents a user with a map and turn-by-turn directions as they navigate, making use of a mobile device's live location to guide them to their destination. This pattern is used in driving, walking, and cycling directions, and is often used in conjunction with voice guidance, traffic data, and other features to help users reach their destination.

Interactive Navigation is possible with the Navigation SDKs for [iOS](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/maps/)
 and [Android](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/maps/)
, which are both powered by the [Directions API](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/navigation/directions/)
.

Your browser doesn't support HTML5 video. Open [link to the video](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/assets/medias/getting-started--navigation--demo-79a875d680bdfd57c3bc301941eafa3a.mp4)
.

Other Navigation Use Cases[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/navigation/#other-navigation-use-cases "Direct link to Other Navigation Use Cases")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Mapbox Navigation products and services support a wide range of use cases beyond the most common patterns listed above. Many of these are backend or data pipeline use cases that involve analyzing locations and routing in real-time.

### Calculate Travel Time and Distance between Multiple Points[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/navigation/#calculate-travel-time-and-distance-between-multiple-points "Direct link to Calculate Travel Time and Distance between Multiple Points")

For scenarios such as a fleet of vehicles on the move that may need to visit multiple locations, it is important to calculate the travel time and distance between multiple points at one time. This can help optimize routes, plan for traffic, and make sure that vehicles arrive at their destinations on time.

To calculate travel time and distance between multiple points, use the [Matrix API](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/navigation/matrix/)
.

### Spatial Joins with Time-Distance Isochrones[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/navigation/#spatial-joins-with-time-distance-isochrones "Direct link to Spatial Joins with Time-Distance Isochrones")

Spatial joins with time-distance isochrones are used to find all points within a certain travel time or distance of a given point. This can be useful for finding nearby points of interest, planning delivery routes, or analyzing the accessibility of a location.

To retrieve time-distance isochrones, us the [Isochrone API](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/navigation/isochrone/)
.

### Route Optimization (The Traveling Salesperson Problem)[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/navigation/#route-optimization-the-traveling-salesperson-problem "Direct link to Route Optimization (The Traveling Salesperson Problem)")

Route optimization is used to find the most efficient route that visits a set of locations. This is a common problem in logistics, delivery, and transportation, and can help save time, fuel, and money by optimizing routes for multiple vehicles or deliveries.

To optimize routes, use the [Optimization API](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/navigation/optimization/)
.

### Cleanup of Fuzzy Route Data for Display or Analysis[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/navigation/#cleanup-of-fuzzy-route-data-for-display-or-analysis "Direct link to Cleanup of Fuzzy Route Data for Display or Analysis")

GPS tracks regularly log a vehicle's location, but these points can be noisy or inaccurate. Map matching cleans up fuzzy route data by snapping GPS points to the road network, making it easier to display the actual route on a map or analyze the traveled road segments and maneuvers.

To clean up fuzzy route data, use the [Map Matching API](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/navigation/map-matching/)
.

### Electric Vehicle Charging[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/navigation/#electric-vehicle-charging "Direct link to Electric Vehicle Charging")

Electric vehicle charging is a growing market, and EV drivers need to find charging stations along their route. Drivers not only need to know where the charging stations are, but also need to know which stations are available, what types of connectors they support, and how long it will take to charge their vehicle.

To find EV charging stations, use the [EV Charge Finder API](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/navigation/ev-charge-finder/)
.

Frontend Navigation products[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/navigation/#frontend-navigation-products "Direct link to Frontend Navigation products")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Mapbox provides frontend products that allow integration of navigation experiences and data for web and mobile applications. These products include SDKs for iOS and Android, as well as plugins for Mapbox GL JS.

### Navigation SDK for iOS[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/navigation/#navigation-sdk-for-ios "Direct link to Navigation SDK for iOS")

The [Mapbox Navigation SDK for iOS](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/navigation/)
 is a software development kit (SDK) that provides turn-by-turn navigation for iOS applications. It includes features such as voice guidance, traffic data, and route preview, and can be customized to match the look and feel of your application. It also includes a core library that provides routing and navigation functionality without the UI components.

To get started with the Navigation SDK for iOS, see its [documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/navigation/)
.

[GUIDE\
\
Get Started with the Mapbox Navigation SDK for iOS\
\
Learn how to install the Navigation SDK for iOS and add turn-by-turn navigation to an iOS app.](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/navigation/guides/install/)

### Navigation SDK for Android[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/navigation/#navigation-sdk-for-android "Direct link to Navigation SDK for Android")

The [Mapbox Navigation SDK for Android](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/navigation/)
 is a software development kit (SDK) that provides turn-by-turn navigation for Android applications. It includes features such as voice guidance, traffic data, and route preview, and can be customized to match the look and feel of your application. It also includes a core library that provides routing and navigation functionality without the UI components.

To get started with the Navigation SDK for Android, see its [documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/navigation/)
 and [code examples](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/navigation/examples/)
.

[GUIDE\
\
Get Started with the Mapbox Navigation SDK for Android\
\
Learn how to install the Navigation SDK for Android and add turn-by-turn navigation to an Android app.](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/navigation/guides/install/)
[EXAMPLE\
\
Add a complete turn-by-turn navigation experience\
\
Add a full-featured navigation experience to an Android app.](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/navigation/examples/turn-by-turn-experience/)

### Mapbox GL Directions Plugin[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/navigation/#mapbox-gl-directions-plugin "Direct link to Mapbox GL Directions Plugin")

Though real-time navigation is less common in web applications, showing route previews on a map along with text-based directions is a common need for store locators and other web app use cases. The [Mapbox GL Directions plugin](https://github.com/mapbox/mapbox-gl-directions)
 is a library that provides written directions and route preview for web applications that use [Mapbox GL JS](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/)
. It includes features such as route previews, traffic data, and route optimization, and can be customized to match the look and feel of your application. It is integrated with [Mapbox Search APIs](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/search/#search-apis)
 to allow users to quickly search for an origin and destination, and provides an intuitive interface for searching for an origin and destination and displaying the route on a map.

[See the Mapbox GL Directions plugin on Githubchevron-right](https://github.com/mapbox/mapbox-gl-directions)

[EXAMPLE\
\
Display navigation directions on a map\
\
Add a route preview and text-based directions to a web app using the Mapbox GL Directions plugin.](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/example/mapbox-gl-directions/)

### Frontend Navigation Product Pricing[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/navigation/#frontend-navigation-product-pricing "Direct link to Frontend Navigation Product Pricing")

Pricing for the mobile Navigation SDKs is based on a combination of monthly active users and the number of monthly trips calculated. See the [Navigation SDK section](https://www.mapbox.com/pricing#navigation-sdk-v3x-metered-trips)
 of the Mapbox Pricing page for more information

Pricing for Mapbox GL Directions is based on usage of the underlying APIs. See the [Navigation API Pricing](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/navigation/#navigation-api-pricing)
 section below for more information.

Navigation APIs[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/navigation/#navigation-apis "Direct link to Navigation APIs")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Directions API[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/navigation/#directions-api "Direct link to Directions API")

The [Directions API](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/navigation/directions/)
 provides routing and navigation functionality for web and mobile applications. It allows you to calculate routes between two or more locations, and provides turn-by-turn directions, traffic data, and route optimization. The response includes spatial data for displaying the calculated route on a map. The Directions API is used by the Navigation SDKs for iOS and Android, as well as the Mapbox GL Directions plugin.

It also supports Electric Vehicle (EV) routing, which allows you to calculate routes that are optimized for electric vehicles, taking into account real-time factors such as battery range, charging stations, and charging time.

To get started with the Directions API, see its [reference documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/navigation/directions/)
 or try its API Playground using one of the links below.

[PLAYGROUND\
\
Directions API Playground\
\
Experiment with request parameters for the Directions API using a graphical interface, and inspect the API response object.](https://docs.mapbox.com/playground/directions/)
[PLAYGROUND\
\
Directions API Playground - Electric Vehicle Routing\
\
Experiment with request parameters for the Directions API for an Electric Vehicle (EV) using a graphical interface, and inspect the API response object.](https://docs.mapbox.com/playground/directions/?engine=electric&alternatives=false&annotations=state_of_charge%2Cduration)

### Matrix API[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/navigation/#matrix-api "Direct link to Matrix API")

The [Matrix API](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/navigation/matrix/)
 allows you to calculate travel times and distances between multiple points in a single request. This can be useful for scenarios such as route optimization, delivery planning, and fleet management.

To get started with the Matrix API, see its [reference documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/navigation/matrix/)
 or try its API playground:

[PLAYGROUND\
\
Matrix API Playground\
\
Create a matrix, experiment with request parameters, see a visualization of the response and inspect the response object.](https://docs.mapbox.com/playground/matrix/)

### Isochrone API[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/navigation/#isochrone-api "Direct link to Isochrone API")

The [Isochrone API](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/navigation/isochrone/)
 allows you to calculate isochrones, which are polygons that represent all points that can be reached within a certain travel time or distance from a given point. This can be useful for scenarios such as spatial joins, accessibility analysis, and delivery planning.

To get started with the Isochrone API, see its [reference documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/navigation/isochrone/)
 or try its API playground:

[PLAYGROUND\
\
Isochrone API Playground\
\
Experiment with request parameters for the Isochrone API using a graphical interface, and inspect the API response object.](https://docs.mapbox.com/playground/isochrone/)

### Map Matching API[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/navigation/#map-matching-api "Direct link to Map Matching API")

The [Map Matching API](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/navigation/map-matching/)
 allows you to clean up fuzzy route data by snapping GPS points to the road network. This can be useful for scenarios such as GPS trace lookup, route analysis, and route display.

To get started with the Map Matching API, see its [reference documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/navigation/map-matching/)
.

### EV Charge Finder API[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/navigation/#ev-charge-finder-api "Direct link to EV Charge Finder API")

The EV Charge Finder API allows you to find electric vehicle charging stations near a point or along a route. This can be useful for scenarios such as electric vehicle routing, trip planning, and charging station discovery.

To get started with the EV Charge Finder API, see its [reference documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/navigation/ev-charge-finder/)
 or try its API Playground:

[PLAYGROUND\
\
EV Charge Finder API Playground\
\
Experiment with request parameters for the EV Charge finder API using a graphical interface, and inspect the API response object.](https://docs.mapbox.com/playground/ev-charge-finder/)

### Wrapping Libraries[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/navigation/#wrapping-libraries "Direct link to Wrapping Libraries")

You can use Mapbox Navigation APIs directly, but you can also use one of several wrapper libraries to integrate them into applications across platforms. These libraries provide a more convenient way to interact with the APIs and may include additional features or functionality.

*   **Mapbox Navigation SDK for iOS** - Use the [`Navigation Core`](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/navigation/api/3.8.0/navigation/documentation/mapboxnavigationcore/)
     module to handle navigation without a UI in an **iOS environment**.
*   **Mapbox Navigation SDK for Android** - Use the [`Navigation Core`](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/navigation/api/coreframework/3.7.3/navigation/)
     module to handle navigation without a UI in an **Android environment**.
*   **Mapbox Java SDK** - Use the Java SDK's [Services module](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/java/guides/services/)
     to interact with the Directions, Matrix, Isochrone, and Map Matching APIs in a **Java environment** (including Android App development).

### Navigation API Pricing[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/navigation/#navigation-api-pricing "Direct link to Navigation API Pricing")

All Mapbox navigation APIs are priced based on usage and include a generous free tier for testing.

For full details, see the [Mapbox Pricing page](https://www.mapbox.com/pricing#navigation/)
.

# dataset | Help | Mapbox

[Skip to main content](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/glossary/dataset/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

A **dataset** is an editable collection of [GeoJSON](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/glossary/geojson/)
 [features](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/glossary/features/)
 that you can store in your Mapbox account and access or edit as needed.

You can download a dataset as GeoJSON, or export a dataset to a [tileset](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/glossary/tileset/)
 that can be used in a map [style](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/glossary/style/)
. Any dataset exported to a tileset can be used in a layer in the [Mapbox Studio style editor](https://docs.mapbox.com/studio-manual/)
 or in the [Data visualization component](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/tutorials/create-a-map-with-data-visualization-component/)
. Any GeoJSON feature or feature collection can be added at runtime as a [source](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/glossary/source/)
.

You can interact with datasets with these tools:

*   Use the [Mapbox Studio dataset editor](https://docs.mapbox.com/studio-manual/)
     to create, upload, edit, or download a dataset, or publish a dataset to a tileset.
*   Use the [Mapbox Datasets API](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/maps/datasets/)
     to create or edit a dataset or its features, or access features as GeoJSON.
*   Use the [Mapbox Uploads API](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/maps/uploads/)
     to publish a dataset to a tileset.

**Related resources:**

*   [Mapbox Studio](https://console.mapbox.com/studio/)
     - _Log in_
*   [Mapbox Studio manual dataset documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/studio-manual/reference/datasets/)
    
*   [Datasets API documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/maps/#datasets)
    
*   [Sample workflow for publishing a dataset to a tileset](https://docs.mapbox.com/studio-manual/guides/#sample-workflow)

# expression | Help | Mapbox

[Skip to main content](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/glossary/expression/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

An **expression** defines a formula for computing the value of any [layout property, paint property](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/glossary/layout-paint-property/)
, or [filter](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/glossary/filter/)
 within a map style. Expressions allow you to style data with multiple feature properties at once, apply conditional logic, and manipulate data with mathematical, logical, and string operators. This allows for sophisticated [runtime styling](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/glossary/runtime-styling/)
.

For a complete list of all available expressions, see the [Mapbox Style Specification](https://docs.mapbox.com/style-spec/reference/expressions/)
.

How to use expressions in Mapbox Studio

Learn how to use expressions for string and data operations to create a map with bilingual labels.

### Expressions in Mapbox GL JS[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/glossary/expression/#expressions-in-mapbox-gl-js "Direct link to Expressions in Mapbox GL JS")

[Mapbox GL JS](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/)
 expressions uses a Lisp-like syntax, using JSON arrays. Expressions in Mapbox GL JS follow this format:

    [expression_name, argument_0, argument_1, ...]

The `expression_name` is the expression operator. For example, you would use [`*`](https://docs.mapbox.com/style-spec/reference/expressions/#*)
 to multiply two arguments:

    ['*', ['pi'], ['^', 3, 2]]

The first argument is [`pi`](https://docs.mapbox.com/style-spec/reference/expressions/#pi)
, which is an expression that returns the mathematical constant pi. The second argument is another expression in which [`^`](https://docs.mapbox.com/style-spec/reference/expressions/#%5E)
 has two arguments of its own. It will return 32, and the result will be multiplied by pi.

### Expressions in the mobile Maps SDKs[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/glossary/expression/#expressions-in-the-mobile-maps-sdks "Direct link to Expressions in the mobile Maps SDKs")

The [Maps SDK for iOS](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/maps/)
 uses [`NSExpression` and `NSPredicate` syntax](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/maps/api/6.4.1/predicates-and-expressions.html)
 for expressions instead of JSON arrays.

In the [Maps SDK for Android](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/maps/)
 expressions are JSON arrays, in which the first element is the expression operator and the other elements are the arguments to the expression. For more information on using expressions in the Maps SDK for Android, see the [expressions documentation page](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/maps/guides/styles/use-expressions/)
. Expressions are an important part of the Maps SDK's concept of _data-driven styling_. The [Mapbox Annotations Plugin for Android](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/plugins/overview/annotation/)
 also simplifies using expressions to adjust the visual properties of annotations on a Mapbox map.

* * *

**Related resources:**

*   [Expression documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/style-spec/reference/expressions/)
    
*   Tutorial: [Get started with Mapbox GL JS expressions](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/tutorials/mapbox-gl-js-expressions/)
    
*   [Maps SDK for Android: Expressions documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/maps/guides/styles/use-expressions/)
    
*   [Maps SDK for iOS: Expressions documentation](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/maps/guides/styles/use-expressions/)
    
*   Tutorial: [Create interactive hover effects with Mapbox GL JS](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/tutorials/create-interactive-hover-effects-with-mapbox-gl-js/)

# Configure interactions | Help | Mapbox

[Skip to main content](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/map-developer-journey/configure-interactions/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

Configure interactions
======================

Turn your maps into powerful interactive experiences.

[Get started](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/map-developer-journey/)

![](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/img/dev-journey-map/arrow.svg)

[Add map](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/map-developer-journey/start-mapping/)

![](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/img/dev-journey-map/arrow.svg)

[Add data](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/map-developer-journey/data-guide/)

![](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/img/dev-journey-map/arrow.svg)

[Configure interactions](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/map-developer-journey/configure-interactions/)

![](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/img/dev-journey-map/arrow.svg)

[Deploy](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/map-developer-journey/post-launch-checklist/)

Mapbox has many options available to turn your maps into powerful interactive experiences across all platforms. Here are some of the most popular ways to add interactivity to maps with Mapbox.

Add search[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/map-developer-journey/configure-interactions/#add-search "Direct link to Add search")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Need to interactively search for locations in your product? Or convert addresses into geographic coordinates? Try adding search or geocoding into your app.

To add search follow along with [GL JS](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/example/mapbox-gl-geocoder/)
, or get started with [iOS](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/search/guides/install/)
 and [Android](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/search/guides/get-started/)
.

Web

iOS

Android

Web

iOS

Android

    // Example from: https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/example/mapbox-gl-geocoder/map.addControl(  new MapboxGeocoder({    accessToken: mapboxgl.accessToken,    mapboxgl: mapboxgl  }));

Web

iOS

Android

    // Example from: https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/search/guides/install/import UIKitimport MapboxSearchimport MapboxSearchUIclass ViewController: UIViewController {let searchController = MapboxSearchController()  override func viewDidLoad() {    super.viewDidLoad()    searchController.delegate = self    let panelController = MapboxPanelController(rootViewController: searchController)    addChild(panelController)  }}extension ViewController: SearchControllerDelegate {func searchResultSelected(_ searchResult: SearchResult) { }func categorySearchResultsReceived(results: [SearchResult]) { }func userFavoriteSelected(_ userFavorite: FavoriteRecord) { }}

Web

iOS

Android

    // Example from: https://docs.mapbox.com/android/search/examples/search/package com.mapbox.search.sample.api;import android.os.Bundle;import android.util.Log;import androidx.annotation.Nullable;import androidx.annotation.NonNull;import androidx.appcompat.app.AppCompatActivity;import com.mapbox.search.ResponseInfo;import com.mapbox.search.SearchEngine;import com.mapbox.search.SearchEngineSettings;import com.mapbox.search.SearchOptions;import com.mapbox.search.SearchSelectionCallback;import com.mapbox.search.common.AsyncOperationTask;import com.mapbox.search.result.SearchResult;import com.mapbox.search.result.SearchSuggestion;import com.mapbox.search.sample.R;import java.util.List;public class ForwardGeocodingJavaExampleActivity extends AppCompatActivity {  private SearchEngine searchEngine;  private AsyncOperationTask searchRequestTask;  private final SearchSelectionCallback searchCallback = new SearchSelectionCallback() {      @Override      public void onSuggestions(@NonNull List<SearchSuggestion> suggestions, @NonNull ResponseInfo responseInfo) {          if (suggestions.isEmpty()) {              Log.i("SearchApiExample", "No suggestions found");          } else {              Log.i("SearchApiExample", "Search suggestions: " + suggestions + "Selecting first...");              searchRequestTask = searchEngine.select(suggestions.get(0), this);          }      }      @Override      public void onResult(@NonNull SearchSuggestion suggestion, @NonNull SearchResult result, @NonNull ResponseInfo info) {          Log.i("SearchApiExample", "Search result: " + result);      }      @Override      public void onResults(@NonNull SearchSuggestion suggestion, @NonNull List<SearchResult> results, @NonNull ResponseInfo responseInfo) {          Log.i("SearchApiExample", "Category search results: " + results);      }      @Override      public void onError(@NonNull Exception e) {          Log.i("SearchApiExample", "Search error: ", e);      }  };  @Override  protected void onCreate(@Nullable Bundle savedInstanceState) {      super.onCreate(savedInstanceState);      searchEngine = SearchEngine.createSearchEngineWithBuiltInDataProviders(          new SearchEngineSettings(getString(R.string.mapbox_access_token))      );      final SearchOptions options = new SearchOptions.Builder()          .limit(5)          .build();      searchRequestTask = searchEngine.search("Paris Eiffel Tower", options, searchCallback);  }  @Override  protected void onDestroy() {      searchRequestTask.cancel();      super.onDestroy();  }}

Learn more about our [Search](https://docs.mapbox.com/api/search/)
 products including the Search Box API and the Geocoding API.

[Test the Search Box API, experiment with query parameters, and view the results.](https://docs.mapbox.com/playground/search-box/)

[Use this Developer API Sandbox to test forward and reverse geocoding queries, experiment with query parameters, and view the results.](https://docs.mapbox.com/playground/geocoding/)

Create markers and popups[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/map-developer-journey/configure-interactions/#create-markers-and-popups "Direct link to Create markers and popups")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Markers and annotations[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/map-developer-journey/configure-interactions/#markers-and-annotations "Direct link to Markers and annotations")

[Markers](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/glossary/marker/)
 are one of the most popular ways to highlight and visualize locations or data on a map on web.

Mobile devices do _not_ contain markers. You can mimic the same functionality on [iOS](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/maps/guides/markers-and-annotations/)
 and [Android](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/maps/guides/annotations/)
 in two different ways, either with a symbol layer if you want to mimic a default marker or a point annotation if you want to use more complex features.

If you want to learn more, start by exploring one of these tutorials.

| Mapbox GL JS | iOS | Android |
| --- | --- | --- |
| *   [Add custom markers in Mapbox GL JS](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/tutorials/custom-markers-gl-js/)<br>    <br>*   [Add custom icons with Markers](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/example/custom-marker-icons/) | *   [Add a marker in iOS](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/maps/examples/add-markers/)<br>    <br>*   [Add dynamic view annotations in iOS](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/maps/examples/view-annotation-basic/)<br>    <br>*   [Add custom point annotation in iOS](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/maps/examples/custom-point-annotation/) | *   [Add a marker in Android](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/maps/examples/add-a-marker-symbol/)<br>    <br>*   [Add dynamic view annotations in Android](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/maps/examples/view-annotation-dynamic/)<br>    <br>*   [Add custom point annotations in Android](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/maps/examples/add-point-annotations/) |

### Show information dynamically with popups[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/map-developer-journey/configure-interactions/#show-information-dynamically-with-popups "Direct link to Show information dynamically with popups")

Once you have data showing up on your map, you can use a [Popup](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/api/markers/#popup)
 to show relevant and contextual information when needed.

#### Popups in GL JS[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/map-developer-journey/configure-interactions/#popups-in-gl-js "Direct link to Popups in GL JS")

On web you can explore different types of interactions, maybe you want a popup on hover or on click.

[TUTORIAL\
\
Display a popup on hover\
\
When a user hovers over a custom marker, show a Popup containing more information.](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/example/popup-on-hover/)
[TUTORIAL\
\
Display a popup on click\
\
When a user clicks a circle, show a Popup containing more information.](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/example/popup-on-click/)

#### User Interaction Guides for Mobile[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/map-developer-journey/configure-interactions/#user-interaction-guides-for-mobile "Direct link to User Interaction Guides for Mobile")

For mobile, it’s important to pair the different types of content with the right gestures - we’ve put together guides on user interaction for both Android and iOS:

[GUIDE\
\
Android User Interaction Guide\
\
Learn how user interaction works in the Mapbox Maps SDK for Android.](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/maps/guides/user-interaction/)
[GUIDE\
\
iOS User Interaction Guide\
\
Learn how user interaction works in the Mapbox Maps SDK for iOS.](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/maps/guides/user-interaction/)

UI Controls in GL JS[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/map-developer-journey/configure-interactions/#ui-controls-in-gl-js "Direct link to UI Controls in GL JS")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

It's important to consider how your users will interact with your map. Mapbox provides some commonly used map [UI controls](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/api/markers/#fullscreencontrol)
 right out of the box including a scale element and on-screen zoom and tilt controls.

[TUTORIAL\
\
Display zoom and rotation controls\
\
Add zoom and rotation controls to the map.](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/example/navigation/)
[TUTORIAL\
\
Display map scale\
\
Add a scale to the map.](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/example/navigation-scale/)

Use camera movement and animations[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/map-developer-journey/configure-interactions/#use-camera-movement-and-animations "Direct link to Use camera movement and animations")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Your browser doesn't support HTML5 video. Open [link to the video](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/assets/medias/how-mapbox-works--configure-interactions--camera-demo-compressed-c01330fbad2aa7335e9418a4e8a83565.mp4)
.

Create stunning cinematic effects or smoothly transition map viewers from one place to another using the map camera. Start with GL JS by following one of these tutorials:

*   [Animate the camera around a point with 3D terrain](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/example/free-camera-point/)
    
*   [Fly to a location after search: Use a custom camera animation with a geocoder](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/example/mapbox-gl-geocoder-with-flyto/)
    

Mapbox’s mobile SDKs also give you the ability to display the user's location:

*   [Android: Display the user's location](https://docs.mapbox.com/android/maps/examples/location-tracking/)
    
*   [iOS: Display the user's location](https://docs.mapbox.com/ios/maps/examples/tracking-mode/)
    

Dynamically cluster data[​](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/getting-started/map-developer-journey/configure-interactions/#dynamically-cluster-data "Direct link to Dynamically cluster data")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Are you working with [GeoJSON](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/glossary/geojson/)
 [point](https://docs.mapbox.com/help/glossary/point/)
 data? Sometimes there is too much data to show cleanly when zoomed out of the map, this is where clustering comes in. Clustering can intelligently group data points so that they are revealed intentionally as a user zooms into and out of the map.

[Example: Create and style clusters\
\
Use Mapbox GL JS' built-in functions to visualize points as clusters.](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/example/cluster/)

[Example: Display HTML clusters with custom properties\
\
An example of a circle layer combined with HTML markers.](https://docs.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/example/cluster-html/)
